questions.answer: ● Not applicable
keyPoints: Not applicable

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Eyelid scrubs bid.
● Warm compresses bid.
● Erythromycin or bacitracin ointment qhs
● Unresponsive: Doxycycline 100mg PO bid
o Avoid in pregnant women, nursing mothers, children < 8 yrs
o Counsel regarding photosensitivity
● Associated dry eyes: preservative free artificial tears 4-8 times/day
● Short-term, low dose topical steroids for sterile corneal infiltrates or phlyctenulosis
● Rosacea: Facial lesions treated with metronidazole gel 0.75% BID

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Counsel the patient that this is a chronic condition that is controlled rather than cured. Avoid exacerbating foods, beverages,
environmental factors if rosacea. Follow-up is variable depending on severity of disease an involvement of cornea. Consider
dermatology referral for moderate to severe facial disease

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Warm compresses for 10min qid with light massage over lesion.
● If lesion draining or associated with blepharitis: erythromycin or bacitracin ointment bid.
● Doxycycline 100mg bid if associated w/ blepharitis/MGD
● If chalazion fails to resolve after 3-4 weeks of appropriate medical therapy, incision and curettage vs. injection of steroid (if
lesion near lacrimal apparatus).
o Steroid injection: risk of skin depigmentation, CRAO
● Send recurrent or unusual chalazia to pathology

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Good prognosis. Lesion usually resolves over weeks to months when the contents drain either externally through eyelid skin or
internally through tarsus. Small amount of scar tissue may remain. Treating underlying blepharitis and MGD will decrease
recurrences.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Counsel this patient that the cornea has lost sensation and has poor healing. This is a chronic problem. Acutely, the prognosis is
guarded as the epithelial defect can be slow to resolve and will need close follow-up to monitor for improvement.
F/u every 1-2 days until improvement then every 3-5 days until resolution.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Prognosis is good with treatment however if scarring involves visual axis will require corneal transplant with intensive, long-term
follow-up
In the acute stage, follow-up the patient every 3-7 days initially then 2-4 weeks. Slowly taper the steroid (months to years) and closely
monitor IOP. For chronic, inactive disease: yearly follow-up unless treatment necessary

cases.photoDescription: This external photo shows a large corneal epithelial defect from limbus to limbus. There appears to be some peri-limbal ischemia with chemosis. questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Copious irrigation to neutralize pH, cycloplegic (avoid phenylephrine 2/2 vasoconstriction), frequent artificial tears/gels,
topical erythromycin q1h while awake, oral pain control, oral or topical IOP control
● Significant inflammation without risk of corneal melting: Topical steroids (Tobradex ointment q2hrs)
● Consider amniotic membrane graft or Prokera
● Lysis of conjunctival adhesions vs symblepharon ring
● Corneal melting: Vit C, doxycycline, acetylcysteine
● Progressive melting or perforation: glue, patch graft, PK

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Counsel this patient that he suffered a very serious injury to his eye and requires close follow up. Follow up with this patient daily,
checking the epithelial defect, IOP, and AC inflammation, tapering the steroids if used when inflammation has resolved.
Prognosis depends on severity of injury. Severe injuries can lead to corneal scarring and long-term ocular surface and glaucoma
issues.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Prognosis is good for improvement in symptoms, but depends on the underlying etiology. Most causes are chronic and require
prolonged therapy. The patient should be followed-up in weeks to months depending on the severity of corneal disease and symptoms.
Patients with an underlying systemic disease should be followed more closely.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Fuchs is a slowly progressive condition that may require corneal transplantation in the future. Prior to cataract surgery, patient
should be counseled about the increased likelihood of corneal edema and prolonged recovery. A thorough evaluation should be
performed to determine if a combined cataract and corneal transplant procedure is indicated
Follow- up every 3-12 months based on severity of symptoms.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Debridement of loose epithelial tissue
● Antibiotic ointment or drop, cycloplegic if iritis present, consider pressure patch or bandage contact lens if defect is large
(unless CTL wearer)
● Oral analgesics for pain
● Maintenance: 1) frequent AT w/ night time ointment or Muro
● If recalcitrant 1) BSCL for 3-6 months 2) epithelial debridement with diamond burr 3) anterior stromal puncture if outside
visual axis 4) phototherapeutic keratectomy

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Follow up q1-2 days until healed, then every 1-3 months depending on symptoms. Counsel patient prognosis is very good with
appropriate treatment although some patients have recalcitrant recurrent erosions.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Serious eye condition that will require daily follow up to monitor for thinning/perforation and infection. Recovery is a very long
process and may involve surgical procedures such as patching the thinning cornea with tissue glue or PK. Close follow-up with a
rheumatologist will also be required. Protective eye wear needed at all times as perforation with minor trauma possible

questions.answer:
Although there is no treatment to prevent the disease from advancing, progression is slow. Decreased vision can occur due to against-

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Avoid rubbing eyes
● Progression => consider CXL (Not FDA approved)
● Glasses => SCL => RGP lens => Hybrid or scleral lenses
● Mild to moderate and unable to tolerate CL then => Intacs
● Severe and significant scarring => DALK/PK
● Acute Hydrops: Bacitracin ointment, cycloplegic, Muro-128, Glasses/Shield at all times

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
This is a progressive condition until middle age when the thinning stabilizes. Most patients do well with RGP and hybrid or scleral
contact lenses. The success rate with corneal transplantation in keratoconus is high.
Follow-up every 3-12 months depending on if there is progression note. After hydrops, 1-4 weeks until resolved

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Viral conjunctivitis is self-limited but usually gets worse for the first 4-7 days and may not resolve for 2-3 weeks. It is highly
contagious as long as the eye is red. Frequent hand-washing, contact precautions, no sharing personal items. Follow up in 2-3 wks.

keyPoints: Topical abx (polytrim or fluoroquinolone gtts QID) x 5-7days

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Must use antibiotics, and close follow-up is needed to tailor treatment according to culture/sensitivity results. Improvement should
occur within first few days, and resolution should occur within 1 week. Pt needs f/u every 2-3 days initially, then every 5-7 days until
resolved. If severe, needs daily follow-up.
If +gonococcal conjunctivitis, patient and sexual partners should be evaluated for other STIs.

● NSAID for mild non-necrotizing scleritis
● If no improvement on NSAID then Prednisone PO 60-100 mg PO QD x 1 week followed by taper

● If necrotizing: sclera patch grafting may be necessary if high risk of perforation
● Topical steroids ineffective

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Important to have follow-up with PMD regarding systemic work-up. Prognosis is good for nonnecrotizing scleritis, guarded for
necrotizing. Often requires co-management between ophtho and PMD. If initial work-up is negative should repeat annually.
Glasses/shield should be worn if any evidence of thinning

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
The eye pressure usually does not respond adequately to medical therapy alone and patient will need urgent cataract extraction.
The prognosis for vision can be good but it depends on the amount of damage to the optic nerve as well as the amount of inflammation
and pressure. If there is no APD, would expect with good control of pressure and inflammation that his vision would likely improve
after cataract extraction.
Patient should be seen at least daily, with the consideration of hospitalization, to monitor pressures until the cataract is removed AND
the IOP normalizes

questions.answer:
There is an increased risk of complications during cataract surgery due to poor dilation and weak zonules. Postoperative IOL
dislocation may occur with time
Condition may be relatively benign but can be associated with highly unstable IOP. Once IOP difficult to control, glaucoma may
progress rapidly. SLT/ALT have a higher success rate but shorter duration of effectiveness.

questions.answer:
● culture and sensitivity of any external purulent material encountered for grade 1 and 2. Vitreous cultures for grade 3.
o early: staph epi/staph aureous
o months - years: strep, haemophilus, s. aureous, moraxells, pseudomonas, serratia
keyPoints: culture and sensitivity of any external purulent material encountered for grade 1 and 2. Vitreous cultures for grade 3.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Grade 1: Hourly fortified antibiotics (vancomycin + tobramycin) or FQ q1hr; start steroids 24 hrs after abx and blebitis
resolving
● Grade 2: add a cycloplegic, consider oral FQ - more careful monitoring
● Grade 3: As in endophthalmitis
o HM or better: Tap and inject using intravitreal vancomycin + ceftazidime. Send for gram stain, culture, sensitivities.
Consider intravitreal steroids in select cases w/ severe vitreous inflammation
o LP or worse/worsening despite aggressive therapy: Vitrectomy with vitreous biopsy for cultures and gram/giemsa
stains.
keyPoints: Grade 1: Hourly fortified antibiotics (vancomycin + tobramycin) or FQ q1hr; start steroids 24 hrs after abx and blebitis

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Follow the patient q12 - 24 hours until improvement is evident. Inform the patient of a guarded prognosis as she is at risk for losing
vision permanently. However, with timely management good outcomes are possible.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
● Prognosis is good to very good depending on severity. Pterygium can continue to grow and cause increased dryness,
irritation and even decreased vision. Pterygium can recur after removal, occasionally worse than the original pterygium.
● If symptomatic and initiating treatment then follow-up every 1-3 months depending on severity and whether topical steroids
are used, otherwise every 6-12 months to monitor for progression.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
● Schnyder dz – Can recur in graft; Lipid management does not affect progression of dystrophy
● Cystinosis – can recur in graft; life expectancy is normal in adult form
● Bietti – corneal crystals may fade with time however retinal crystals may progress to RPE atrophy and choroidal sclerosis
leading to nyctalopia, poor dark adaptation, central and VF loss; family members should be evaluated
● Lymphoproliferative disorder – crystals usually resolve with treatment of underlying malignancy
● FQ Drops – crystals resolve with cessation of drops

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
oI would educate the patient and his family about the seasonal
cyclical nature of this disease, and that typically patients grow
out of this as they get older. Our goal would be to start treatment
with mast cell stabilizers and antihistamines before springtime to
pre-empt the usual cycle of symptoms, and would likewise avoid
use of contact lenses at this time of year. I would warn about
excessive eye rubbing as this can lead to keratoconus.

questions.question: What is your assessment?
questions.answer:
o Recurrent corneal erosion syndrome secondary to Map-Dot
Fingerprint (anterior basement membrane) Dystrophy

Plan:

o Recurrent bouts of eye pain upon awakening are due to

sloughing of corneal epithelium from palpebral conjunctiva
sticking to corneal epithelium, which does not adhere well to the
underlying basement membrane. Would treat acutely with
antibiotics, tears, and ointments at bedtime. Once healed, |
would continue with tears throughout the day and ointment at
bedtime (either artificial tear gel or Muro128 ointment). If
problems continue despite this, I would place a bandage contact
lens for 2-3 months. Although one can try scoring bowman with
a burr or bent 30-gauge needle; this is usually not performed
when RCE is due to MDF dystrophy. Therefore, if the above
steps fail, one could ultimately considering phototherapeutic
keratectomy, especially if due to dystrophy, as it treats a larger
area. While the success of this is good, hyperopic shift can
occur.
keyPoints: Recurrent corneal erosion syndrome secondary to Map-Dot
Fingerprint (anterior basement membrane) Dystrophy

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
I would obtain a full history especially asking about a history of
atopy or eye rubbing or a history of contact lens wear, Down
syndrome, RP, LCA, Marfan's, or mitral valve prolapse, all of
which are associated with keratoconus or other keratoectasia. |
would perform a full ocular examination, including best
refracted acuity and perform a streak to evaluate the red reflex
expecting scissoring. I would also look for Vogt striae and a
Fleischer ring at the base of the cone in addition to the Munson
sign. I would obtain corneal topography look for inferior
steeping suggestive of KCN or crab claw defect in Pellucid.
Keratoglobus much more clinically obvious with global thinning
and corneal protrusion such that the trabecular meshwork can be
visualized without the aid of a gonio lens. Keratoectasia would
elicit history of vision worsening weeks to years after LASIK
surgery. Would evaluate lens, retinal, optic disc to ensure that
these structures are not a factor in altered vision.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o In addition to ordering the testing mentioned above, I would start

out with optical correction. Initially this can be with glasses, but
as this gradually progresses, rigid gas permeable contact lenses
provide better acuity. I would also advise no eye rubbing and
treat symptoms that are associated with eye rubbing, such as
atopy and dry eye. I would follow every six months, with serial
topography. If hydrops develop, I would treat with muro128,
cycloplegia, glaucoma drops, and protective lenses. Vision often
improves after healing. If apical scarring reduces vision, then
PK can be considered.

cases.title: 45F - eye pain, photophobia and redness
cases.presentation: 45-year-old female with eye pain, photophobia and redness

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Anterior segment photograph showing peripheral corneal

thinning and vascularization.

ll Differential Diagnosis:


Peripheral Ulcerative Keratitis (PUK)
Terrien Marginal Degeneration
Mooren Ulcer

Furrow Degeneration

Ill History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:


We are told that this patient has pain, so Terrien's and Furrow
Degeneration are unlikely in this case. Terrien's usually starts
superiorly and then advances circumferentially. It typically has a
leading edge of lipid and stains negatively with fluorescein.
Furrow is seen in the clear zone between senile arcus and the
limbus; it is also painless. Mooren’s is possible but is a
diagnosis of exclusion. Given the pain and presentation, | am
most concerned for PUK. I would ask about a history of
rheumatoid arthritis (RA), or joint pain that might suggest RA.
Also inquire about other diseases associated with PUK such as
Lupus, Wegener’s, and Polyarteritis Nodosa. If the patient has
no known history of any of these, | would still consider obtaining
lab work to evaluate. I would also perform a complete eye
examination looking for other signs of inflammation, including
hypopyon, anterior uveitis, vitritis, retinochoroidal lesions, or
scleritis.

0 Peripheral Ulcerative Keratitis (PUK)

want to ignore the possibility of endophthalmitis, so 1 would also enquire
about recent eye surgery or eye trauma. I would ask about his family’s
regional background, as Behcet’s is much more common in people who
originate from countries along the Silk Road. | would perform a full eye
exam. Inspect eyelids for herpetic lesions and the cornea for keratitic
precipitates (KP). Stellate pattern KP (Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis
(FHIC)), Mutton Fat KP (typical for granulomatous causes such as
tuberculosis, syphillis, and sarcoidosis). Evaluate the lens for integrity to
rule out causes such as phacolytic, phacoanaphylactic, and lens particle
elaucoma. Perform gonioscopy of angle to identify peripheral anterior
would also evaluate for vitritis and evaluate the fundus for evidence of
retino-choroidal involvement, which would be more consistent with
Behcet’s or less likely, toxoplasmosis. Obtain uveitis labs based on clinical
findings such as HLA-B27, RPR, VDRL, PPD, ACE, Lysozyme, Chest CT,
Sacroiliac films, Lyme titers, ESR, and a CRP.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Treatment is with topical prednisolone every 1-2 hours and taper slowly as
anterior chamber reaction improves, and cycloplege to prevent synechiae
formation. Send patient to rheumatologist for systemic treatment, typically
with NSAIDs, and this will help with ocular treatment as well. Will follow
every 1-3 days initially to monitor for improvement. Occasionally oral
steroids are required, but must first rule out TB and syphilis.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
I would ask about history of blurry vision and halos after
exercise, and refraction status (we are told he is myopic); these
would all be consistent with PDS. However, I would also ask
about other causes of TID such as trauma, recent surgery, or
herpes infections. I would perform a complete eye exam;
including visual acuity, IOP, and slit lamp looking for
endothelial pigment (Krukenberg’s spindle), pigment on lens
zonules (Scheie stripe or Zentmeyer line), and also gonioscopy
to look for dense solid pigmentation of the trabecular meshwork.
Based on TID location, patient age, myopic refraction, the likely
diagnosis is PDS. The age, location of the TIDs and absence of
PXF material on the lens capsule make PXF less likely. The
TID in PDS are mid-peripheral radial slit-like iris defects
thought to be due to iris pigment liberation from contact between
a backward bowing iris and lens zonules. The biggest concern is
for pigmentary glaucoma, and therefore I would check IOP,
evaluate the optic nerve for glaucomatous changes, test with
HVF, and monitor carefully over time.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o PDS can usually be monitored and followed as a glaucoma

suspect with HVF, IOP checks, and monitoring for optic nerve
changes. If glaucoma develops, then treat with IOP lowering
agents as a first line. If this fails, consider argon laser
trabeculoplasty. I would try to avoid filtering surgery, as these
patients are at increased risk for hypotony.

the cornea for abrasion, infiltrates and anterior chamber reaction,

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o The photo shows mucous, debris and filaments on the cornea,

which is stained with fluorescein.

Differential Diagnosis:
o dry eye syndrome

o filamentary keratitis

© conjunctivitis

o exposure keratopathy

[I _History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

o I would take a detailed history, enquiring about any history of
autoimmune conditions that might predispose to dry eye. I
would perform a complete ophthalmologic examination, paying
special attention to filaments on the cornea inferiorly, and would
stain with fluorescein to better see the extent of epithelial
erosions. I would also perform a Schirmers test, measure the
tear break-up time, examine lid margins for blepharitis, evaluate
lid closure to rule out exposure risk (lagophthalmos), and look
for conjunctival papillary or follicular reaction.

Vv Assessment:

o Dry eye syndrome with filamentary keratitis

Plan:

o Dry eye treatment is step-wise: starting with artificial tear drops,
then ointments, and punctal plugs (collagen or silicone). Can
consider Restasis.

o Filaments can be treated by mechanical removal with jewler
forceps at the slit-lamp or topical treatment with Mucomyst.

o Therapeutic contact lens can offer symptomatic relief, must
monitor use closely.

| Patient Education:

o I would educate the patient that the prognosis is good, but he
may need treatment for life, and will need to monitor for
improvement and for corneal infections, given the epithelial
breakdown.

questions.answer:
> Corneal edema and a mid-dilated pupil

Differential Diagnosis:

5 Acute angle closure glaucoma, pupillary block, plateau iris

5 PAS, neovascular glaucoma

5  Phacomorphic glaucoma, zonular weakening (trauma, PXF, Marfan’s)

> Posterior segment mass

5 Choroidal effusion: Topamax, buckle, excess laser

o Aqueous misdirection

History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

5 Would ask about family history of acute angle closure, hyperopia. Would
ask about precipitating event (i.e. dim light, flu therapies), mydriatics, eye
surgery, retinal laser, Topamax, sulfonamides, and trauma. Examine for
keratitic precipitates (KP), posterior synechiae, iris atrophy, iris
neovascularization, mid-dilated pupil, swollen enlarged lens, anterior
chamber reaction, glaucomaflecken. Gonio angle to look for peripheral
anterior synechiae (PAS). May need to perform a dynamic gonio to open
angle to identify PAS. Evaluate opposite eye for occludable angle. Look
for findings of iris bombe and pupillary block. Perform a fundus exam
looking for ischemic factors (CRVO, DM, and CRAO). Would look at
optic nerve for spontaneous pulsation and cupping. Look for ciliary body
tumors and choroidal effusion. If no view of posterior pole; obtain a B
scan.

Assessment:

+ This Asian patient has a mid-dilated pupil, corneal edema, nausea and
vomiting, which suggest a diagnosis of acute angle closure.

Plan:

© Lower IOP with Timolol, CAI (unless sulfa allergy), brimonidine. Re-
check eye pressure and vision in 1 hour. If not better, admit, start oral
CAI’s; ensure renal function good and no intracranial bleed or hypotension.
Once pressure under control and cornea clear perform laser peripheral
iridotomy (LPI). If other eye has an occludable angle would consider LPI
of this eye in the near future.

© |faqueous misdirection; treat with medical therapy and mydriatics. If no
change, then open posterior capsule in pseudophakic patients. If patient
phakic and has a patent PI can laser vitreous face through PI, otherwise may
need vitrectomy to reverse aqueous misdirection.

© lf angle closure due to due to iris neovascularization then lower eye
pressure and perform laser PRP when possible.

© If choroidal effusion, then lower IOP, start steroids, and mydriatics.

2)

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Typically begins in one eye and spreads to other eye with
bilateral watery discharge for 10-14 days. Can develop
subepithelial infiltrates (SEI’s) that cause chronic eye irritation
and sometime alterations in vision. Would recommend requent
hand washing, cool tears, and discuss not sharing towels or linen,
as it is very contagious. If itching would suggest adding an
antihistamine drops. Would follow for resolution. If persistent
irritation past 14 days evaluate for SEI’s and consider cultures
for Chlamydia. Avoid steroids in the treatment of SEI’s, if at all
possible, as patient can become dependent.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Infection of the filter bleb can happen at any time after
trabeculectomy surgery. If Blebitis then treat with fortified
vancomycin and gentamicin every hour or fluoroquinolones
hourly. Only start steroid after 24 hours and showing
improvement. Observe closely over first 24 hrs. Grade 2 treated
the same may add oral fluoroquinolones. Grade 3: treat as
endophthalmitis with vitreous tap/culture and intravitreal
antibiotics of Vancomycin and Ceftazidime.

Patient Education:

Will follow daily until resolution. Guarded prognosis dependent
on grade.

vancomycin and gentamicin every hour or fluoroquinolones
hourly. Only start steroid after 24 hours and showing
improvement. Observe closely over first 24 hrs. Grade 2 treated
the same may add oral fluoroquinolones. Grade 3: treat as
endophthalmitis with vitreous tap/culture and intravitreal
antibiotics of Vancomycin and Ceftazidime.

cases.title: 60 - blurry vision that is worse in morn
cases.presentation: 60-year-old patient with blurry vision that is worse in morn

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Cornea clear with endothelial guttae and bronze metal beaten
appearance

Differential Diagnosis:

o Fuchs Endothelial Dystrophy

o Posterior Polymorphous Dystrophy (PPMD)

o Pseudophakic Bullous Keratopathy

o Congenital Hereditary Endothelial Dystrophy (CHED)

o Pigment Dispersion Syndrome (pigment can mimic guttae)

History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

o History suggests Fuchs dystrophy, but would evaluate the
endothelium at slit lamp for microcystic edema, endothelial
changes such as scalloped thickening on retro-illumination
(PPMD), and corneal thickness via pachymetry (unlikely CHED
due to age). Obtain specular microcopy, if available.

Assessment:

o Fuchs endothelial dystrophy

Plan:

> Generally slowly progressive with worsening vision in the
morning and improvement by the evening. Can develop pain if
patient develops bullous keratopathy with subsequent corneal
epithelial rupture. Treat with Muro-128 initially; consider blow
dryer in am to help deturgess the cornea. If eye pressure is
elevated, can decrease with topical mediations, which can
minimize the corneal edema. If patient develops recurrent
bullae, treat with Muro 128 ointment. May eventually need PK
or an endothelial transplant (DSEK).

o Important to evaluate corneal health prior to cataract surgery as
the phacoemulsification can damage the endothelium and lead to
corneal edema. Some patients may need a triple procedure
(cataract extraction with intraocular lens implantation, and a
Penetrating Keratoplasty or DSEK), especially when stromal
edema is present. Note: Thick pachymetry readings and a low
endothelial cell count alone are not indications for a triple
procedure.

Patient Education:

This condition typically progresses slowly and even slower with
therapy. Would council patient that cataract surgery alone may
worsen corneal condition.

labs, such as RPR and VDRL. Marfan’s patients can have aortic
aneurysms; Homocystinuria patients can have mental retardation
and have thrombotic events from general anesthesia

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o. Lens has white fibrillary flakey material in a concentric pattern.

Differential Diagnosis:

o> ©Pseudoexfoliation (PFX) Syndrome or PFX glaucoma

o True exfoliation

o Amyloid

o Uveitis

History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

o Ask about exposure to heat or occupation suggestive of risks like
glass blowers, welders. Ask about eye pain and photophobia.
Obtain ancestry and family history. Full eye exam, vision,
fields, eye pressure, slit lamp for deposits on anterior iris, pupil
border, and look for a K spindle, anterior chamber cell, mid-
periphery trans-illumination defects (TID), and gonio for
sampolesi line. Dilate to inspect lens for concentric rings of
fibrillary material, evaluate for phacodonesis, and look at optic
nerve for possible glaucomatous changes.

Assessment:

o PXF Syndrome

Plan:

o PXF patients are at risk for glaucoma, therefore these cases
should undergo glaucoma monitoring with visual fields, dilated
exams, optic nerve photos, and eye pressure check routinely
every 3-6 months depending on initial findings.

o If patients develop high IOP and/or VF changes suggestive of
glaucoma treat with pressure lowering eye drops. These cases
respond well to selective laser trabeculoplasty (SLT) and argon
laser trabeculoplasty (ALT); note the response is shorter lived
than with primary open angle glaucoma (POAG) cases. When
medical and laser therapy fail then filter surgery (i.e.
trabeculectomy) may be considered.

Patient Education:

© These cases are at high risk to develop glaucoma, have wide IOP
fluctuations, and make cataract surgery risky due to poor dilation
and weak zonules. Will need good follow up to monitor
glaucoma changes.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
© Strict bed rest, shield over eye at all times, elevate head of bed,
avoid narcotic or aspirin/NSAID for pain control. Can take
Tylenol for pain. Would start topical steroid, cycloplegia, and
eye pressure lower medications if eye pressure elevated. |
would avoid carbonic anhydrase inhibitors if sickle cell disease;
test for sickle cell if status unknown or patient at risk (African
American or Mediteranean). Would monitor daily for first three
days as patient at risk for rebleed and eye pressure elevation. If
there is evidence of corneal blood staining, or uncontrolled eye
pressure elevation, would consider an AC washout. Eventually
after a few weeks would perform gonioscopy to look for angle
recession.

V! Patient Education:

o Education regarding importance of strict bed rest and drop
regimen and follow-up compliance, as well as importance of eye
protection. Prognosis is good if no open globe or posterior
injury, assuming IOP is controlled.

etc...)

+ Due to the immediate presentation it is unlikely fungal or

oq oo ©

© Most likely Pseudomonas Keratitis
Plan:

+ | would initiate broad spectrum fortified topical antibiotics like
Vancomycin and ceftazidime every hour around the clock to
treat gram positive and gram negative bacteria.

Patient Education:

© Pseudomonas is an aggressive organism that can perforate the
cornea. The prognosis for restoring prior vision is guarded. This
organism once controlled will likely leave a dense central
stromal scar that can be treated with corneal transplant.
Discontinue contact lens wear; throw out all currently opens
contact lenses, cases, and solutions.

© I would monitor this patient daily until improvement and
monitor cultures for guidance on therapy.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Start treatment if possible at scene, or as soon as seen, using
copious irrigation of Lactated Ringers, Saline solution, or tap
water till pH at 7.0. Inspect fornices for debris; sweep,
irrigate fornices and start cycloplegics, antibiotics qlh, and
lower eye pressure with oral Acetozolamide and/or topical
Timolol. Use steroids for first 7 days and consider
Doxycycline for matrix metalloproteinase inhibition. Can
consider Ascorbate to augment healing. For severe cases
can consider amniotic membrane.

V! Patient Education:

o Guarded, will follow daily. Complications are glaucoma,
symblepharon, retinal necrosis (especially seen with alkali
burns). Some severe case may need a limbal stem cell
transplants.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Lids with telangiectasia, scurf, and crusting without lash loss

Differential Diagnosis:

o Blepharitis

o Ocular Roseacea with blepharitis

o Herpes Simplex Keratitis

o Sebaceous Cell carcinoma

History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

> External examination looking at face for rhinophyma, facial
telangiectasia, look at slit lamp for telangiectasia, scurf, lash
loss, cornea for pannus, dendrites

Assessment:

o Blepharitis

Plan:

o Chronic management usually required but as symptoms improve
the medications can be reduced. Treatment is with warm
compresses, lid washes, avoidance of exacerbating foods/drinks
like alcohol, spicy food if Rosacea. Doxycycline treatment for
matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) inhibition and metrogel for
control of facial rosacea. I would avoid ocular steroids in
patients with ocular rosacea as they can develop corneal
thinning, which can lead to perforation. Lastly these patients can
develop chalazia, which can be treated in a similar fashion as the
blepharitis; however some will need to be managed surgically
with incision and drainage.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o I would treat with warm compresses and lid scrubs initially and
would consider adding topical antibiotics. I would also consider
adding oral Doxycycline if patient also has concomitant
blepharitis or meibomian gland dysfunction. Ifno improvement
consider incision and curettage. Would send specimen to
pathology especially in recurrent cases to evaluate for
carcinoma. Additional treatment modalities to consider are
intralesional steroids but would counsel regarding post-injection
hypopigmentation, atrophy, and risk of retinal artery occlusion,
although the later is extremely rare.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
I would ask how long the lesion has been present, rate of growth,
and symptoms. Also would ask about any inciting trauma or
chemical burns to the eye. Also would ask about work
environment, eye protection, sunglass wear, and any complaints
of contact lens intolerance. Conjunctival healing after trauma
and chemical burns can look similar to pterygia but in these
cases it is only the anterior edge that is adherent in contrast to the
fibrovascular pannus seen with pterygia. | would perform a full
eye exam and evaluate the lesion in more detail at the slit lamp.

I would look for leukoplakia or a gelatinous appearance to the
lesion, which would make me suspicious for CIN, which is often
not wing shaped or in a location typical of a pterygia (3 and 9
o’clock).

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
If asymptomatic would recommend eye protection, sunglasses
while outdoors and natural tears. Would explain that inciting
factors such as wind, sun, and dust can cause progression and
eventual lead to symptoms requiring surgery. Some can
develop a dellen proximal to leading edge. This is treated with
tears and ointments.

If the pterygia is symptomatic, encroaching the visual axis,
causing contact lens intolerance and/or atypical in appearance,
history or growth I would recommend excision followed with
mitomycin and then repair either by conjunctival autograft,
rotational flap, or amniotic membrane graft.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Conjunctival melanoma most commonly arises from PAM with
atypia 75%, de novo 20%, and nevus 5%. Would get excisional
biopsy via no touch technique with wide margins, double
freeze/thaw cryotherapy to conjunctival edges, alcohol
epitheliectomy for corneal involvement, and map biopsies of
bulbar and palpebral conjunctiva. I would start mitomycin
topical therapy.

o If positive for melanoma would send to oncology for metastatic
workup, consider a sentinel node biopsy. If orbit involved then
exenteration, radiotherapy/chemotherapy.

for local disease is 85%; 5 year recurrence is 40%. Would

lesions and evaluate for metastasis (liver function tests, CT of
chest and brain). Would recommend continued follow-up with
oncologist and primary care physician.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Multifocal crusting lesion and erythematous rash in the cranial
nerve 5 V1 dermatomal distribution. Periocular erythema and
edema.

Differential Diagnosis:
o Herpes Zoster (HZV)

o Herpes Simplex (HSV)

I History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

o ZV is dermatomal and HSV typically is not. Would evaluate
skin for vesicles and ask about immune status, steroid use, stress,
sun exposure. Would evaluate conjunctiva for follicular reaction,
cornea for dendrites and disciform keratitis, evaluate for keratic
precipitate on endothelium and anterior chamber reaction.
Perform retro-illumination of the iris to detect trans-illumination
defects. Would perform dilated fundus exam for retinal necrosis
and optic nerve involvement. Check eye pressure, which can be
high with herpes uveitis. Diagnosis is clinical and cultures are
not routinely necessary unless doubt exist.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
> Start treatment with antivirals like oral acyclovir 800 mg 5 times
daily, topical antibiotic ointment for herpes related
conjunctivitis, corneal dendrites and skin lesions. If corneal
disciform keratitis with no epithelial defect would add topical
steroids. For skin lesion would use topical antibiotic. For
neuralgia would use capsaicin or EMLA cream, cool compresses
and oral pain meds. If uveitis without epithelial corneal defects
would use topical steroid, oral anti-viral, and a cycloplegic. If
retinitis would admit for intravenous anti-virals, oral prednisone,
and retinal photocoagulation to cordon areas of retinal necrosis,
which may minimize chance of retinal detachment. Also would
give consideration to administering intravitreal antivirals like
famvir.

cases.title: 60M - notes a painless lesion
cases.presentation: 60-year-old male notes a painless lesion.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Subconjunctival salmon colored mass in the fornix

Differential Diagnosis:

o Non-Hodgkin’s B-Cell Lymphoma, local or systemic

o Lymphoid Hyperplasia

o Amyloidosis

o Rare: T cell lymphoma, plasmacytoma, Hodgkins

History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

o Ask about history of systemic lymphoma, immunocompromised
status, skin lesion elsewhere. Perform complete eye exam, ocular
motility, Hertel, inspect lids for lesion. Check for anterior
chamber reaction, iris lesion, uveal lesion, choroidal lesion, optic
nerve. Obtain CT scan of orbits initially to determine extent of
lesion. Check systemically for adenopathy.

Assessment:

o This appears to be a lymphoma

Plan:

o 1 would recommend an excisional biopsy if possible but
incisional warranted if to large or concern for damage to
extraocular tissues. Send for pathology in non fixed manner for
PCR, Flow cytometry, EM; ensure to coordinate this with
pathology. If lymphoma will need systemic work- up by
oncology. Local external beam radiation is usually curative for
lesion confined to the orbit, if systemic then chemotherapy
needed under guidance of oncologist.

Patient Education:

> If local then good, if systemic the outcome is good if responsive
to chemo but more guarded. Best discussed with the oncology
experts.

questions.answer:

cell carcinoma, sebaceous cell carcinoma, inflamed actinic keratosis, tricholemmoma, and Merkel cell
tumor.

questions.answer: Complete excision of the lesion.

questions.question: How would the treatment differ for sebaceous cell carcinoma?
questions.answer:
Sebaceous cell carcinoma requires wide excision with frozen section and conjunctival map biopsy.
Exenteration is performed for orbital extension or pagetoid spread. Radiotherapy is used for palliation.

Exenteration is performed for orbital extension or pagetoid spread. Radiotherapy is used for palliation.

questions.question: What are the different types of scleritis?
questions.answer:
Anterior (98%) and posterior (2%). Anterior can be diffuse, nodular, or necrotizing with or without
inflammation.
keyPoints: Anterior (98%) and posterior (2%). Anterior can be diffuse, nodular, or necrotizing with or without
inflammation.
scoringKeywords: Anterior (98%) and posterior (2%). Anterior can be diffuse, nodular, or necrotizing with or without
inflammation.

questions.question: What are the possible etiologies?
questions.answer:
There is a systemic association in 50% of cases and 30% of cases have a collagen vascular disease
(rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, systemic lupus erythematosus, polyarteritis nodosa,
Wegener’s granulomatosis, or relapsing polychondritis). Other etiologies are herpes zoster, syphilis,
tuberculosis, leprosy, gout, porphyria, and idiopathic.
keyPoints: There is a systemic association in 50% of cases and 30% of cases have a collagen vascular disease
(rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, systemic lupus erythematosus, polyarteritis nodosa,
Wegener’s granulomatosis, or relapsing polychondritis). Other etiologies are herpes zoster, syphilis,
tuberculosis, leprosy, gout, porphyria, and idiopathic.
scoringKeywords: There is a systemic association in 50% of cases and 30% of cases have a collagen vascular disease

Wegener’s granulomatosis, or relapsing polychondritis). Other etiologies are herpes zoster, syphilis,
tuberculosis, leprosy, gout, porphyria, and idiopathic.

questions.question: What pharmacologic test can differentiate between scleritis and episcleritis?
questions.answer:
Topical phenylephrine 2.5% will blanch the injected area if it is due to episcleritis but will not if it is due
to scleritis.

questions.question: What are the signs of posterior scleritis?
questions.answer:
Chorioretinal folds, serous retinal detachments, vitritis, optic disc edema, and ‘T-sign’ and thickened
sclera on B-scan ultrasound.

questions.question: How would you manage this patient?
questions.answer:
This patient requires a systemic workup including: CBC with differential, ESR, RF, ANA, ANCA
(antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody), VDRL or RPR (rapid plasma reagin), FTA-ABS or MHA-TP
(microhemagglutination assay for Treponema pallidum), uric acid, BUN (blood urea nitrogen), PPD
(purified protein derivative of tuberculin), and chest X-ray. Treatment is with oral NSAIDs. Systemic
steroids, antibiotics, and possibly immunosuppressive agents may be necessary depending on the
underlying etiology.

questions.answer:
Lid tone (snapback test), lower lid margin position (sagging), and ability to rotate the lower lid by
pressing on the inferior tarsal border.
keyPoints: Lid tone (snapback test), lower lid margin position (sagging), and ability to rotate the lower lid by

questions.question: How would you manage this patient?
questions.answer:
The ocular surface must be protected from the inturned lashes with frequent instillation of lubricating
drops, gels, and / or ointments. Surgical repair is often necessary and the most appropriate procedure
depends on the type of entropion:
Cicatricial: excision of scar with possible anterior lamellar resection or recession, tarsal fracture, tarsal
graft, or conjunctival / mucous membrane grafts. Consider removing lashes.
Involutional: lid taping, thermal cautery, or Quickert suture; horizontal or vertical lid shortening; and / or
lid retractor repair.
Spastic: lid taping, thermal cautery, Botox injection, Quickert suture.

drops, gels, and / or ointments. Surgical repair is often necessary and the most appropriate procedure
depends on the type of entropion:
Cicatricial: excision of scar with possible anterior lamellar resection or recession, tarsal fracture, tarsal
graft, or conjunctival / mucous membrane grafts. Consider removing lashes.
Involutional: lid taping, thermal cautery, or Quickert suture; horizontal or vertical lid shortening; and / or
lid retractor repair.
Spastic: lid taping, thermal cautery, Botox injection, Quickert suture.
scoringKeywords: The ocular surface must be protected from the inturned lashes with frequent instillation of lubricating
drops, gels, and / or ointments. Surgical repair is often necessary and the most appropriate procedure

questions.question: How would you manage this patient?
questions.answer:
Perform corneal cultures and smears of the ulcer and start empiric antibiotic treatment with topical
fortified antibiotics (cefazolin and tobramycin) alternating every hour initially, and a cycloplegic drop.
Ask the patient about contact lens wear and if he does wear contacts the lens and case should be cultured
as well. This vision-threatening corneal infection initially requires daily follow-up monitoring the vision,
IOP, size, depth, and density of the corneal infiltrate, and presence and size of any overlying epithelial
defect. Therapy should be adjusted based on the culture and sensitivity results. Confocal microscopy if
available is useful for identifying fungi and Acanthamoeba.

cases.title: 46M - a history of multiple ocular surgeries for glaucoma and...

questions.answer: Band keratopathy.
keyPoints: Band keratopathy.
scoringKeywords: Band keratopathy.

questions.question: What is the pathology?
questions.answer:
Band keratopathy consists of calcium deposition in the cornea (epithelial basement membrane,
Bowman’s membrane, and anterior stroma) with destruction of Bowman’s membrane. It is usually due to
chronic ocular inflammation but also some systemic diseases (i.e. hypercalcemia, gout).
keyPoints: Band keratopathy consists of calcium deposition in the cornea (epithelial basement membrane,
Bowman’s membrane, and anterior stroma) with destruction of Bowman’s membrane. It is usually due to

scoringKeywords: Band keratopathy consists of calcium deposition in the cornea (epithelial basement membrane,

chronic ocular inflammation but also some systemic diseases (i.e. hypercalcemia, gout).

questions.question: With which ocular disease is this most commonly associated?

with interstitial keratitis, phthisis, and trauma.

questions.answer: Interstitial keratitis (IK).

questions.question: What are the etiologies?
questions.answer:
IK is usually infectious. The most common infections are syphilis, HSV (herpes simplex virus), and
tuberculosis; also VZV (varicella zoster virus), mumps, rubella, leprosy, and onchocerciasis. Other causes
include sarcoidosis and Cogan’s syndrome.

cases.title: 26F - red, irritated eyes for 3 weeks. She wears disposable...
cases.presentation: A 26-year-old woman complains of red, irritated eyes for 3 weeks. She wears disposable contact lenses but cannot tolerate them for more than an hour or two. Yesterday she noticed some mucous discharge in the morning. She wants to know if this is a flare-up of her blepharitis. Her primary doctor gave her an antibiotic ointment, which she puts in her eyes twice a day, and she has been using lid scrubs for the past week. She also uses artificial tears once or twice a day and takes flaxseed oil capsules. Exam shows rosacea with minimal meibomian gland dysfunction. There are scant white mucus strands in the inferior fornix OD and minimal conjunctival injection.

questions.answer:
Blepharoconjunctivitis, acute bacterial conjunctivitis, conjunctivitis due to allergy or toxicity to the
antibiotic ointment, giant papillary conjunctivitis (GPC), contact lens overwear, or corneal ulcer.

questions.answer:

monitored.

of involvement, and is with external beam radiation, chemotherapy, and surgery.

questions.question: If this lacrimal gland mass were a neoplasm, what would be the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Lacrimal gland tumors are lymphoproliferative (50%) or epithelial (50%). Half of the epithelial tumors
are pleomorphic adenomas (benign mixed tumors) and half are malignant (adenoid cystic carcinomas
and malignant mixed tumors).

and malignant transformation.

The more common nodular BCC has raised, pearly, nodular borders, and telangiectasia. There may be
central ulceration (rodent ulcer) and distortion of the surrounding normal eyelid architecture with
scarring and eyelash loss. The morpheaform BCC, which is rarer but more aggressive, appears as a firm,
flat plaque with ulceration and indistinct borders that penetrates in to the dermis and can have pagetoid
spread.

questions.question: What are the characteristic findings of sebaceous cell carcinoma?
questions.answer:
Sebaceous cell carcinoma is highly malignant and appears as a hard, yellow nodule. It can masquerade as
chronic unilateral blepharitis or recurrent chalazion with thickened, red lid margin inflammation and
loss of lashes.

questions.question: What is the treatment for BCC?
questions.answer:
Wide surgical excision with frozen section margin control. Consider Mohs’ micrographic surgery. Canthal
tumors require orbital CT scan to assess posterior involvement.
keyPoints: Wide surgical excision with frozen section margin control. Consider Mohs’ micrographic surgery. Canthal
tumors require orbital CT scan to assess posterior involvement.
scoringKeywords: Wide surgical excision with frozen section margin control. Consider Mohs’ micrographic surgery. Canthal
tumors require orbital CT scan to assess posterior involvement.

questions.question: Which location on the eyelids has the worst prognosis?
questions.answer: Medial canthus because the tumor often extends deeper and can involve the lacrimal drainage system.
keyPoints: Medial canthus because the tumor often extends deeper and can involve the lacrimal drainage system.
scoringKeywords: Medial canthus because the tumor often extends deeper and can involve the lacrimal drainage system.

questions.question: How do acid and alkali burns differ?
questions.answer:
Acid tends to cause less severe injury than alkali. Acid denatures and precipitates proteins, which form a
barrier to further penetration. Alkali denatures but does not precipitate proteins, and also saponifies fats
(disrupts lipid membranes), causing deeper penetration into ocular tissues.

questions.question: What is the acute treatment for chemical burns?
questions.answer:
Copious irrigation checking pH level, débridement (any necrotic conjunctiva and particulate matter),
frequent lubrication, topical antibiotic and cycloplegic, consider topical (10%) and oral sodium ascorbate
(aids collagen synthesis and scavenges superoxide radicals), collagenase inhibitor (acetylcysteine or
EDTA), steroids (for first 5–10 days only to reduce corneal and intraocular inflammation and help
prevent symblepharon, but can enhance collagenase-induced corneal melting, which often begins 1–2
weeks after injury), may require control of elevated IOP, bandage contact lens, or tarsorrhaphy.
Symblepharon lysis is performed with a glass rod.

questions.question: What are the most common causes of Stevens-Johnson syndrome?
questions.answer:

infectious (HSV, Mycoplasma, adenovirus, Streptococcus).

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
OCP is a chronic, progressive disease. Surgery can cause exacerbations and should be used with caution.
PK has a poor success rate, and keratoprosthesis also has limited success but is used in end-stage disease.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis of peripheral corneal thinning?
questions.answer: Mooren’s ulcer, marginal keratolysis, staphylococcal marginal keratitis, Terrien’s marginal degeneration.

questions.question: What findings help distinguish Mooren’s ulcer from Terrien’s marginal degeneration?
questions.answer:
Mooren’s ulcer is painful and has an undermined leading edge with an overhanging margin, absent
epithelium in active areas, and may have conjunctival injection.
Terrien’s marginal degeneration is bilateral, painless, and has a leading edge of lipid, steep central edge,
sloping peripheral edge, intact epithelium, and superficial vascularization.
keyPoints: Mooren’s ulcer is painful and has an undermined leading edge with an overhanging margin, absent
epithelium in active areas, and may have conjunctival injection.

sloping peripheral edge, intact epithelium, and superficial vascularization.

Terrien’s marginal degeneration is bilateral, painless, and has a leading edge of lipid, steep central edge,

questions.question: How would you workup this patient?
questions.answer:
Mooren’s ulcer is a diagnosis of exclusion whereas marginal keratolysis is due to systemic disease, so it is
necessary to order the following lab tests: CBC with differential, ESR, RF, ANA, ANCA, BUN, creatinine,
and urinalysis. If infection is suspected, then culture and smears should be obtained.

questions.question: The C-ANCA is positive. What is the diagnosis and treatment?
questions.answer:
Wegener’s granulomatosis, which requires systemic steroids and immunosuppressive therapy
(cyclophosphamide). The eye should be treated with lubrication, punctal occlusion, and possibly
tarsorrhaphy to heal the epithelium and prevent further corneal melting. Topical cyclosporine,
acetylcysteine, and conjunctival recession or resection may be helpful. Tectonic or penetrating
keratoplasty may be required for significant thinning.

questions.question: What are the most likely diagnoses?
questions.answer:
This pigmented lesion is probably a conjunctival nevus or primary acquired melanosis (PAM); malignant
melanoma is less likely.
keyPoints: This pigmented lesion is probably a conjunctival nevus or primary acquired melanosis (PAM); malignant
melanoma is less likely.
scoringKeywords: This pigmented lesion is probably a conjunctival nevus or primary acquired melanosis (PAM); malignant

questions.question: What are the characteristics of each?
questions.answer:
Nevus: a discrete, elevated, variably pigmented lesion that contains cysts. It may enlarge during puberty
and is rarely malignant.
PAM: a patchy, diffuse, flat lesion with indistinct margins that does not contain cysts. It may grow
(nodular thickening) and involve the cornea.
Malignant melanoma: a nodular, variably pigmented lesion with blood vessels that does not contain
cysts. It may arise from a nevus (20%), PAM (50–75%), or de novo (25%).
keyPoints: Nevus: a discrete, elevated, variably pigmented lesion that contains cysts. It may enlarge during puberty
and is rarely malignant.
PAM: a patchy, diffuse, flat lesion with indistinct margins that does not contain cysts. It may grow
(nodular thickening) and involve the cornea.
Malignant melanoma: a nodular, variably pigmented lesion with blood vessels that does not contain
cysts. It may arise from a nevus (20%), PAM (50–75%), or de novo (25%).
scoringKeywords: Nevus: a discrete, elevated, variably pigmented lesion that contains cysts. It may enlarge during puberty
and is rarely malignant.

questions.question: What would you tell him about the treatment and prognosis?
questions.answer:
PAM is a precancerous lesion that should be treated with complete surgical excision and cryotherapy.
Recurrence can be treated with topical interferon alpha-2b or mitomycin C. The overall risk of malignant
transformation is 20–30% and is more likely if there is atypia.

questions.question: What signs would you look for on exam?
questions.answer:
Irregular astigmatism (decreased visual acuity, scissors reflex on retinoscopy, steep keratometry with
irregular mires, abnormal corneal topography), apical corneal thinning and scarring, Fleischer ring
(epithelial iron deposition around base of cone), Vogt’s striae (deep, stromal, vertical stress lines at apex
of cone), prominent corneal nerves, Munson’s sign (protrusion of lower lid with downgaze), and
Rizzuti’s sign (triangle of light on iris from penlight beam focused by cone), may have hydrops (opaque
edematous cornea, ciliary injection, and anterior chamber cells and flare).

irregular mires, abnormal corneal topography), apical corneal thinning and scarring, Fleischer ring
(epithelial iron deposition around base of cone), Vogt’s striae (deep, stromal, vertical stress lines at apex
f cone), prominent corneal nerves, Munson’s sign (protrusion of lower lid with downgaze), and

questions.question:
What other anterior corneal dystrophies may have a similar appearance with tiny dots or subepithelial
scarring?
questions.answer: Meesmann’s dystrophy and Reis-Bucklers’ dystrophy.
keyPoints: Meesmann’s dystrophy and Reis-Bucklers’ dystrophy.
scoringKeywords: Meesmann’s dystrophy and Reis-Bucklers’ dystrophy.

questions.question: What is the pathology of each?
questions.answer:
Anterior basement membrane dystrophy: abnormal epithelial adhesion causes intraepithelial and
subepithelial basement membrane reduplication with intraepithelial microcysts (dots) and subepithelial
ridges and lines (map-like and fingerprint-like).
Meesmann’s dystrophy: epithelial cells contain PAS-positive material (peculiar substance) and the
epithelial basement membrane is thickened.
Reis-Bucklers’ dystrophy: absence of Bowman’s layer, replacement by connective tissue, and irregular
saw-toothed epithelium with curly filaments (electron microscopy).
keyPoints: Anterior basement membrane dystrophy: abnormal epithelial adhesion causes intraepithelial and
subepithelial basement membrane reduplication with intraepithelial microcysts (dots) and subepithelial
ridges and lines (map-like and fingerprint-like).
Meesmann’s dystrophy: epithelial cells contain PAS-positive material (peculiar substance) and the
epithelial basement membrane is thickened.
Reis-Bucklers’ dystrophy: absence of Bowman’s layer, replacement by connective tissue, and irregular
saw-toothed epithelium with curly filaments (electron microscopy).
scoringKeywords: Anterior basement membrane dystrophy: abnormal epithelial adhesion causes intraepithelial and
subepithelial basement membrane reduplication with intraepithelial microcysts (dots) and subepithelial
ridges and lines (map-like and fingerprint-like).
Meesmann’s dystrophy: epithelial cells contain PAS-positive material (peculiar substance) and the
epithelial basement membrane is thickened.
Reis-Bucklers’ dystrophy: absence of Bowman’s layer, replacement by connective tissue, and irregular
saw-toothed epithelium with curly filaments (electron microscopy).

questions.question: What are the possible sequelae of this patient’s disorder?
questions.answer: Recurrent erosions (10%) or decreased vision (from subepithelial scarring).
keyPoints: Recurrent erosions (10%) or decreased vision (from subepithelial scarring).
scoringKeywords: Recurrent erosions (10%) or decreased vision (from subepithelial scarring).

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Asymptomatic patients require no treatment.
Erosions are treated with lubrication and hypertonic saline (Muro 128 5% ointment at bedtime for up to
1 year) after the epithelial defect heals. For recurrences, consider a bandage contact lens, epithelial
débridement, anterior stromal puncture, or PTK. Also, consider treatment with matrix
metalloproteinase-9 inhibitors (oral doxycycline and topical steroids).
Subepithelial scarring causing decreased vision or monocular diplopia is treated with débridement.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic patients require no treatment.

1 year) after the epithelial defect heals. For recurrences, consider a bandage contact lens, epithelial
débridement, anterior stromal puncture, or PTK. Also, consider treatment with matrix
metalloproteinase-9 inhibitors (oral doxycycline and topical steroids).
Subepithelial scarring causing decreased vision or monocular diplopia is treated with débridement.
scoringKeywords: Asymptomatic patients require no treatment.

questions.question:

questions.answer:
Cavernous hemangioma
Infection and hemorrhage can both present with rapidly developing proptosis.
Orbital lyinphangioma may produce acute proptosis when there is a hemorrhage
from interstitial capillaries. The cavernous hemangioma is a slow-growing lesion that
creates proptosis over years or months.

Orbital infectious disease occurs most commonly as a result of spread from
surrounding sinusitis, most commonly the ethmoid sinuses. Whether the presence
of a subperiosteal fluid collection on (TT (in the presence of stable vision and
clinical signs) is an absolute indication for surgery is controversial. Most
ophthalmologists would agree that medical management and careful observation
may be employed when the patient's clinical signs are stable and the vision is
normal. Proptosis and motility limitation help to differentiate orbital cellulitis from
presepial cellulitis. Indications for surgical intervention in orbital cellulilis include
decreasing vision and afferent pupillaiy defect, failure to respond to IV antibiotics,
and progression of clinical signs such as motility changes and proptosis.

cases.title: 7M - a sudden onset of rapid evolution of unilateral
cases.presentation:
A 7-year-old white boy presents with a sudden onset of rapid evolution of unilateral
ptosis for approximately 1 week. Examination shows significant edema in the
periorbital area with a palpable mass in the superior nasal quadrant of the eyelid.
Biopsy is performed immediately and the diagnosis of rhabdoinyosaicoma is made.
How is rhabdomyosarcoma best treated?

questions.question:
A 7-year-old white boy presents with a sudden onset of rapid evolution of unilateral
ptosis for approximately 1 week. Examination shows significant edema in the
periorbital area with a palpable mass in the superior nasal quadrant of the eyelid.
Biopsy is performed immediately and the diagnosis of rhabdoinyosaicoma is made.
How is rhabdomyosarcoma best treated?
questions.answer:
Radiation with or without additional chemotherapy
Before 1965, the standard treatment for orbital rhabdomyosarcoma involved
exenteration, and it had a poor survival rate. Since 1965, this mutilating procedure
has been abandoned as primary management. Radiation and systemic chemotherapy
are the mainstays of treatment based on the guidelines set forth by the Intergroup
Rhabdomyosarcoma Study. The total dose of local radiation varies from 4500 to
6000 rad given over a period of 6 weeks. Chemotherapy is used to eliminate
microscopic cellular metastasis. The survival rate using these modalities has
improved significantly.

questions.question:
A 55-year-old while man presents wilh bilateral prnpiosis, double vision, and
ehemosis. Figure 6-14 is an external photograph. What is ihe most likely diagnosis;
questions.answer:
Thyroid-related orbitopathy
The clinical signs of thyroid-related orbitopathy can be generally grouped into two
independent manifestations: type 1 and type 2 orbitopathy. Type 1 orbilopathy
comprises symmetric proptosis with symmetric eyelid retraction, minimal orbital
inflammation, and minimal exiraocular muscle inflammation or restrictive
myopathy. Type 2 orbitopathy comprises extraocular muscle myositis, restrictive
myopathy, orbital inflammation, and chemosis. Compressive optic neuropathy is
more commonly a feature of type 2 orbitopathy.

Ipsilateral hemangiomas of the meninges are common.
The girl has meningoculaneous angiomatosis (Sturge-Weber syndrome,
encephalotrigeminal angiomatosis). The syndrome consists of mostly unilateral
meningeal calcifications, facial nevus flammeus (port-wine stain) frequently along
the distribution of the trigeminal nerve, and congenital glaucoma (30%). Heredity
- does not seem to be an important factor. Histologically, the syndrome is
characterized by cavernous hemangiomas of the skin, lids, choroid, and meninges.
The disease has no tendency for spontaneous regression as observed with capillary
hemangiomas of the child.

trauma cases.
Posttraumatic endophthalmitis after penetrating trauma typically occurs in fewer
than 10% of cases. The most common agents responsible for this infection include
Staphylococcus epidennidis and Bacillus species, although many other organisms have
been recovered. The prognosis for visual recovery in this setting is generally poor,
especially when Bacillus cereus is implicated.

alternative to medication (timolol) in the initial treatment of POACJ. However,

therapy to control IOP. Laser-treated eyes had a lower mean IOP.

Secondary angle closure glaucoma may be associated with each of the following
conditions EXCEPT:
A] intraocular tumor

questions.answer:
Schwartz's syndrome
Schwartz's syndrome is the name given to open angle glaucoma after
rhegmatogenous retinal detachment. The elevated IOP results from obstruction of
outflow by inflammation, pigment released from the retinal pigment epithelium
(RPE), glycosaminoglycans released by the photoreceptors, or photoreceptor outer
segments. It usually resolves after repair of the retinal detachment (RD). Scleral
buckles may interfere with venous drainage of the uveal tract, leading to swelling
and anterior rotation of the ciliary body with resultant angle closure. In

questions.answer:
Excision of involved iris tissues with cryotherapy to remaining membranes on
conical tissues
Epithelial downgrowth has been reported most commonly as a complication of
cataract surgeiy, but it can occur after penetrating keratoplasty, glaucoma
surgeiy, penetrating trauma, and partial removal of epithelial cysts of the
anterior segment. It is more likely when surgeiy is associated with hemorrhage,
inflammation, vitreous loss, or incarcerated tissue. It usually results in intractable
secondary angle closure glaucoma unless successfully treated. The treatment is
difficult and usually unrewarding. All the techniques attempt to close the fistula
and destroy the epithelium inside the eye. The extent of iris involvement can be
outlined using the argon laser. Irradiation and photocoagulation have been
abandoned as ineffective. Excision of involved iris tissue with cryotherapy to
remaining corneal membranes is currently the best technique, although good
vision is maintained in only a few cases. Prevention is much more effective than
the treatment of established disease.
keyPoints: Excision of involved iris tissues with cryotherapy to remaining membranes on
conical tissues

questions.answer:
Figure 10-11
Conjunctival malignant melanoma (Fig. 10-11) is a pigmented elevated lesion that
enlarges progressively with time. It occurs most commonly on the bulbar
conjunctiva. These tumors are veiy vascular, and dilated vessels may be seen feeding
the tumor. The prognosis is generally better than cutaneous melanomas. A systemic
melastatic work-up is necessary to direct treatment. Conjunclival melanomas can
arise de novo (30%), from nevi (40%), or from primary acquired melanosis (30%).
A conjunctival nevus (Fig. 10-12) is a flat or slightly elevated pigmented lesion of
the bulbar conjunctiva. It may have epithelial inclusion cysts within its substance.
These lesions can grow or enlarge during adolescence or with pregnancy. Suspicious
lesions should be biopsied to rule out melanoma.
Primary acquired melanosis (Fig. 10-14) is a unilateral condition found in middle-
aged white people. These multiple, superficial, flat patches of pigmentation develop
into malignant melanoma in 20% to 30% of these patients. Excisional biopsy should
be performed on any suspicious nodular lesions.
Racial or ocular melanosis (Fig. 10-13) is found most commonly in pigmented indi
viduals and represents benign collections of melanin in the conjunctiva. It appears as
a perilimhal dusting of light brown pigment. No malignant potential exists.

cases.title: All of ihe following statements regarding the condition...

questions.answer:
it occurs as a unilateral disease
Anterior membrane (inap-dot-fingerprint) dystrophy of the cornea occurs bilaterally
but may be asymmetric. It may be dominantly inherited. Characteristic clinical
findings consist of cystic, geographic, or fingerprint formations that may be seen
with sclerotic scatter or relroillumination. Pathologic findings consist of thickened
basement membrane, abnormal epithelial cells with inicrocyst-s, and fihrillar material
between the basement membrane and Bowman's layer. Symptoms typically occur
between the fourth and sixth decades of life. Treatment options include topical
lubricants, 5% sodium chloride, scraping, patching, anterior stromal puncture, or
possibly phototherapeutic keratectomy.
keyPoints: it occurs as a unilateral disease

cases.title: 25 - severe pain in her righl eye after
cases.presentation:
A 25-year-old soft contact lens wearer presents with severe pain in her righl eye after
wearing her daily disposable contact lens overnight lor I week. Pictured is her righl
eve. (Fig. I(M.r>). The least appropriate therapy is:

questions.question:
A 25-year-old soft contact lens wearer presents with severe pain in her righl eye after
wearing her daily disposable contact lens overnight lor I week. Pictured is her righl
eve. (Fig. I(M.r>). The least appropriate therapy is:
questions.answer:
topical cela/olin
Pictured is a Pseudoinonas aeruginosa corneal ulcer in a contact lens wearer. Initial
antimicrobial therapy should provide broad-spectrum coverage for both Grani-
positive and Gram-negative microbes. Combination therapy with a cephalosporin or
vancomycin for Gram-positive coverage and an aminogylcoside (tobramycin or
gentamicin) provide excellent coverage. Monotherapy with fourth generation
fluoroquinolones (moxifloxacin and galifloxacin) is also used. They have shown to
provide coverage against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative microbes.
Monotherapy with a cephalosporin (cefa/olin) does not provide adequate Gram-
negative coverage.
keyPoints: topical cela/olin

questions.question:
A 76-year-old man is scheduled for cataract surgery in his right eye as his visual acuity
has dropped to 20/200 due to lens opacity. He previously underwent cataract
surgery in the opposite eye, which resulted in an expulsive choroidal hemorrhage
and no light perception vision. All of the following may be steps to prevent an
expulsive hemorrhage in this eye EXCEPT:
questions.answer:
clear corneal incision
Risk factors lor expulsive choroidal hemorrhage include myopia, glaucoma.
atherosclerotic vascular disease, hypertension, and. previous expulsive hemorrhage
in the opposiie eye. I'sing as small an incision as possible along with being ready to
perform a sclerotomy is important. Decompression before opening the globe with
digital pressure or other device may be helpful. Close the wound with
nonabsorbable. preferably nylon, sutures to prevent delayed hemorrhage. It is also
beneficial to keep blood pressure controlled and level of anesthesia deep (if general
anesthesia is used) during surgery. Shelved or self-sealing incisions allow mine rapid
closure and repressiiri/aiion ol die globe should bleeding occur.

patients without uveitis. 1 lowever, complications in the early or late postoperative
period may arise in certain nvekides. Elective cataract surgery is besi performed alter
inflammation IS quiel for several months. Even minimal or baseline inflammation
preoperatively may result in marked inflammation postoperaiivelv. Prophylaxis or
treatment may require hourly topical steroids or even systemic steroids.
KM. implantation is contraindicated in patients with juvenile rheumatoid
arthritis—associated iridocyclitis. Development of cyclitic membranes and subsequent
< iliary body detachment following extracapsuiar cataract extraction suggest the need
for complete capsuiar removal. Therefore, combined Icnseciomy and subtotal
vitrectomy is recommended either through the Umbus or pars plana. Patients with

cataract extraction and after KH.s have been safely implanted. A transient
postoperative hyphema may develop but resolves without sequelae. Although those
with pars planhis do not apparently have a .significantly increased risk ol
i complications associated with routine cataract surgery, pars plana lensectomy-
vitrectomy may lie indicated as postoperative vitreous opacities may limit visual
acuity. Visual acuity may also be limited by cystoid macular edema.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Natural Hx: Hydroxychloroquine, increased risk with 6.5 mg/kg ideal body weight (>400 mg/day). With chloroquine, >3.0 mg/kg
ideal body weight (>250 mg/day).
Prognosis: Once toxicity, vision loss rarely recovers and can to continue to progress even with discontinuation
Follow-up: Annual screening exam; more frequently if toxicity or risk factors for toxicity: > 5 yrs of use, renal or liver disease, age
>60 yrs old

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Natural History: Proliferative retinopathy most common with SC(33%), SThal(14%) then SS(3%). RD usually begins in ischemic
peripheral retina and tears usually occur at base of sea fans; often precipitated by photocoagulation.
Prognosis: Incidence of significant visual loss from sickle cell retinopathy is variable but appears to be relatively low.
Follow-up: Yearly if no retinopathy, 3 months with retinopathy

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
This condition can progress to neovascularization, glaucoma and further vision loss. Emphasize control of HTN, Dm and cholesterol
with PMD as there is 10% chance of contralateral eye involvement
Prognosis: Visual prognosis most closely related to extent of capillary damage and retinal ischemia in the macula as seen on FA.
Follow-up: Initially every mo for first 4 months and then every 3-12 mo checking for neovascularization and macular edema

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Natural History: Neovascularization develops in 20% of patients at mean of 4 weeks after onset.
Prognosis: With time central retinal artery reopens and retinal edema clears but effect on visual acuity is usually permanent because
inner retina has been infracted.
Follow-up: Repeat eye exam 4 weeks look for NVI/NVD/NVA ; treat with PRP, anti-vegf if needed.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Peripheral or Immunocompetent patient: observation only, treat IOP, if AC inflammation cyclopegic with topical steroid
● Macular involving, immunocompromised, severe vitritis: Pyrimethamine (do not give if pregnant or breast feeding), Folinic
acid (minimize bone marrow toxicity of pyrimethamine), Sulfadiazine. (needs weekly CBC). May add prednisone 24 hours
after antibiotic administration. Alternate therapies: Clindamycin, bactrim, atovaquone, azithromycin, tetracycline.
● Vitrectomy if nonclearing.
● Maintenance therapy if patient is immunosuppressed.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Toxoplasmosis is self limited in immuncompetent patients but may recur in immunocompromised patients and have a prolonged
clinical course. Prognosis is good for extra-macular lesions in immunocompetant patients. Shorter interval follow-up for macula
threatening lesions (1-3 days) then follow-up every q1-2 weeks on therapy once stable/improving.

questions.answer:
I would counsel this patient that with a central macular scar vision is unlikely to return. If one eye has OHS with CNV or disciform
scarring risk of visual loss in fellow eye is related to presence or absence of focal macular histo spots.
I would educate on monocular precautions and recommend monitoring with Amsler at home. If macular changes present then f/u
every 6 mo. If no lesions in macula, monitor yearly.

questions.answer:
I would reassure this patient and follow the patient annually to establish stability or growth of the nevus. I would counsel this patient
that the risk for malignancy is < than 5% without high-risk findings for malignant melanoma. High-risk lesions should be followed
every 3-6 months

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
I would have a very serious discussion with the patient regarding the poor prognosis of the disease and propensity for retinal
detachment in the affected eye. I would also explain that the patient is at risk for the other eye being affected, but with aggressive
treatment this possibility will be decreased. This patient will need daily follow-up while admitted specifically for scleral depressed
dilated exams to check for retinal tears/holes

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Chronic and recurrent inflammation can lead to cataract, glaucoma and CNV. The visual prognosis is generally good with treatment
but outcomes may be adversely affected by glaucoma, CNV, cataract, pthisis bulbi. I will initially follow the patient daily, then weekly,
then monthly with close monitoring for recurrent inflammation and IOP.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
I would counsel this patient on the poor prognosis associated with ocular ischemic syndrome especially when neovascularization is
present. I would also inform this patient on the importance of follow-up with their PMD to optimize treatment of vasculopathic risk
factors as this patient is at increased risk for a stroke.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Bedrest if HOB elevated 45 degrees
● No heavy lifting, bending or straining
● Avoid NSAIDS/ASA and use Tylenol for pain
● Treat any retinal breaks with laser or cryo.
● Vitrectromy for any RD or break on B-scan not amenable to above treatments, non-clearing VH > 3 mo, VH with NVI,
hemolytic or ghost cell glaucoma
