questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
15% of patients with symptomatic PVD have a retinal tear however this goes up to 50-70% if associated with vitreous hemorrhage.
Prognosis is good as long as there is no retinal detachment. Blood may take several weeks to clear. I would follow this patient daily
the first 2-3 days then qweek with serial DFEs or B-scans if necessary. I would instruct the patient to return immediately for
worsening vision or new flashing lights/floaters.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Treat symptomatic retinal breaks with laser, cryotherapy or scleral buckle; consider treating asymptomatic breaks if there is
no surrounding pigmentation
● Focal laser for extrafoveal or juxtafoveal CNVM; PDT or anti-VEGF for subfoveal CNV
● One-piece polycarbonate safety goggles for sports due to increased risk of choroidal rupture with minor trauma

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Vision loss from atrophy may occur slowly over years. CNV may develop in 5 to 10% of eyes. The prognosis depends on the presence
and location of CNV. I would train the patient on using an Amsler grid and give RD precautions. In the absence of complications, I
would reexamine the patient every 6 to 12 months.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
The majority of patients (85-90% ) with dry ARMD have slow and gradual vision loss. The AREDS vitamins can help slow this
progression. 10-15% can develop exudative ARMD so patients should be followed every 6-12 months and come in immediately if there
are any changes on the Amsler grid.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Visual prognosis depends on whether bleeding involves central macular region. Visual improvement can occur when the hemorrhage
is primarily located within the superficial layers of the retina. Follow-up frequency is dependent on amount and location of exudate
and hemorrhage.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Macular holes are grouped into stages. Stage | is an impending
— hole, stage II-IV is full thickness hole. Stage II is <400 microns,
no posterior vitreous detachment (PVD), Stage III is >400
microns and no PVD or operculum, Stage IV is hole with cuff
and complete PVD. Observe stage I as 50% will resolve
spontaneously. Vitrectomy with or without internal limiting
membrane (ILM) peel for stage II-IV. If operated within first 6
months can regain half the visual loss. Others operate up to a
year. Small portion of patients may benefit even if long standing
history of hole. New drugs in the horizon like ocriplasmin may
be used for stage II hole with 50-60% success rate.
| Patient Education:
o Lowrisk for RD but must explain signs and symptoms to patient.


106 107

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
>  Fundus photo showing a bulls eye maculopathy
1] Differential Diagnosis:
fe) Chloroquine/Hydroxychloroquine toxicity
o Cone dystrophy
o Macular Degeneration (ARMD)
9 Stargardt or Fundus Flavimaculatus
o Battens
oul History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:
> Would ask about medications like Chloroquine and
Hydroxychloroquine as well as dosages. Ask about family
history of retinal diseases, symptoms of photophobia, seizures,
ataxia, and dementia. Would obtain best corrected visual acuity,
measure eye pressure and perform dilated fundus exam looking
for macular pigment changes, drusen, macular or peripheral
pisciform (fish-like) flecks, choroidal neovascularization (CNV).
I would perform a neurologic exam. If patient younger, no RA
and not taking chloroquine or hydroxychloroquine I would order
an Electroretinogram (ERG), and an IVFA. If ERG shows a
non-recordable photopic response | would suspect a diagnosis of
Cone Dystrophy. If the IVFA shows silent choroid I would
suspect Stargardt’s. Battens patients typically have history of
seizures and progressive dementia with retinal findings of
pigmentary retinopathy. Batten’s is an autosomal recessive
lysozyme storage disease.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
> Would discuss that once toxicity starts it may continue despite
stopping the medication. It’s always best to have baseline exam
prior to instituting medication. Baseline testing includes: vision,
fundus photos, HVF (red test), and home monitoring with a red
on black Amsler grid. In this way the patient and physician can
stop medication earlier.

| History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:


Would ask about a medical history of sickle cell, diabetes or
sarcoid. Would ask about intravenous drug abuse. I would
perform a complete eye exam looking for uveitis, iris nodules,
and neovascularization of the iris (NVI), and perform a dilated
fundus exam. In this case I would order sickledex, ACE,
lysozyme, fasting blood glucose, and a CT of the chest if sarcoid
suspected. I would conduct intravenous fluorescien angiography
to assist evaluation and development of a treatment plan.

questions.answer:
o This patient has evidence of neovascularization and would
perform pan-retinal photocoagulation (PRP) to ischemic area
located anterior to sea fan.

o For patients with sickle cell history and no evidence of
retinopathy | would plan follow-up exams every 6-12 months.

o For patients who develop retinopathy I would plan closer follow-

ups (every 3-4 months); performing PRP as needed.

‘@)

Prognosis is variable: with good patient education and
comprehensive interval follow-ups patients can expect to
maintain good visual potential. Also will need to have primary
care involved and discuss signs/symptoms of sickle crisis.

Additional finding in sickle cell: Black sunburst (mid-peripheral pig. lesion

with spiculated borders), Salmon patch (intraretinal hemorrhage), -Iridescent
spots (intraretinal spots after resporption of salmon patch), Angioid streaks,
Conjuctival comma-shaped capillaries in inferior fornix

Peripheral ateriovenous anastomoses, Stage 3: Neovascularization, Stage 4:
Vitreous hemorrhage, Stage 5: RD

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Fundus shows diffuse four quadrant flame-shaped hemorrhages

following the nerve fiber layer. The optic disc is swollen.

Differential Diagnosis:

Central Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO)
Diabetes

Radiation Retinopathy

Hypertensive retinopathy

Papilledema

{Il History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

I would ask about history of diabetes, radiation, hypertension,
headaches, transient visual obscurations, glaucoma, thyroid
disease, hypercoaguable diseases, vasculitis (lupus, syphilis,
sarcoid), medications (OCP, diuretics, tetracycline, trenitoin),
and orbital tumors. Would perform an eye exam checking
vision, pupils, Hertel, ocular motility, gonioscopy (looking for
neovascularization of the angle and iris (NVA/NV)J), and eye
pressure. Would perform a dilated exam looking for
neovascularization of the disk and macular edema. | would
perform [VFA and OCT to define macular edema and whether
condition is ischemic or non-ischemic. I suspect IVFA will
show non-perfused areas and the OCT macular swelling. |
would check blood pressure, evaluate for diabetes (FBS,
HgAIC), order screening labs (CBC, PT/PTT, ESR, lipid profile,
homocysteine), and for sarcoid suspects (ACE, lysozyme, Chest
CT), and atypical cases (FT-ABS, Lyme titer, ANA). I would
refer for cardiovascular and hypercoaguable work-up as needed.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Ischemic cases can develop NVI, NVA. Therefore I would

follow monthly for six months performing eye exams and
gonioscopy looking for neovascularization. Should this develop
I would initiate PRP to regress NVI/NVA. For macular edema I
would discuss using intravitreal triamcinolone or anti-VEGF.

cases.presentation: 65-year-old with painless loss of vision.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Retinal whitening with cherry red spot.

1] Differential Diagnosis:

Central Retinal Artery Occlusion (CRAO)
Tay Sachs,

Niemann-Pick (present younger children)
111 History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

Would ask about history of hypertension, carotid disease,
cardiovascular disease, sickle cell, hypercoaguable states,
syphilis, migraines, and collagen vascular disease. Would
perform a full eye exam checking vision, pupils for an RAPD,
dilated fundus exam to search for emboli. Would seek etiology
of emboli as this can be helpful. I would look for emboli: (1)
cholesterol emboli (hollenhorst plaque) are retractile bodies seen
at bifurcations arising from carotid artery atheromatous plaques,
(2) calcium emboli are white seen distally in retina and arise
from cardiac valves, (3) platelet-fibrin emboli are dull white long
arising from carotid atheroma. CRAO can also occur with giant
cell arteritis (GCA), therefore would evaluate for temple pain,
headaches and obtain an immediate ESR and CRP. Would ask
about onset of vision loss as timing will direct treatment plan.

risk individuals. Can have elevated eye pressure in active
toxoplasmosis thus helping support diagnosis.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o For elevated eye pressure; treat with pressure lowering drops other than

prostaglandins. If immunocompromised and lesion(s) are near the
macula or optic disc; recommend starting pyrimethamine, and
sulfadiazine with folinic acid to prevent pyrimethamine aplastic
anemia. Will need weekly CBC while on pyrimethamine. If patient
allergic to sulfa, consider clindamycin or azithromycin. Would use
topical and oral steroids, except in immunocompetent patients.
Treatment is typically 6 weeks; immunocompromised cases are longer.
Consider other recent treatment protocols; Bactrim DS twice daily has
the benefit of being easier to take and cheaper. Another alternative is
Atovaquone that has the benefit of activity against tachyzoites and
dormant bradyzoites, but is expensive. Can observe patients who are
immunocompetent, have peripheral retinal lesions and non-threatened
vision. Would consider working-up for TB, syphilis, and sarcoid if
planning to institute systemic steroids.
keyPoints: For elevated eye pressure; treat with pressure lowering drops other than
prostaglandins. If immunocompromised and lesion(s) are near the
macula or optic disc; recommend starting pyrimethamine, and
sulfadiazine with folinic acid to prevent pyrimethamine aplastic
anemia. Will need weekly CBC while on pyrimethamine. If patient
allergic to sulfa, consider clindamycin or azithromycin. Would use
topical and oral steroids, except in immunocompetent patients.
Treatment is typically 6 weeks; immunocompromised cases are longer.

o Would ask about timing of vision loss, medications, medical history,
and family history. Ask about history of dementia, seizures suggestive
of Batten’s. Would perform a complete eye exam with additional
testing such as an IVFA, electroretinogram (ERG), and
electrooculogram (EOG). Would expect to see a “silent choroid”
pattern on IVFA with abnormal ERG and EOG based on fundus
appearance if Stargardt’s. | would not suspect Battens in this 20 year
old patient without history of seizure or dementia. But would obtain
peripheral blood smears looking for fingerprint inclusions on electron
microscopy (EM). Consider Cone Dystrophy if color vision was
decreased, ERG photopic responses were attenuated, and fundus photo
showed a bull’s-eye pattern. Also in the differential; fundus
albipunctatus but this typically has white dot pattern in the mid-
periphery; often lumped into the differential of crystalline retinopathy
that has night blindness, prolonged dark adaptation, normal fields and
vision. Also consider retinitis punctate albescens (similar to
albipunctatus), but has declining vision, fields, night blindness, and
abnormal ERG.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
o Would discuss that this condition is autosomal recessive, typically has
slow progressive vision loss starting in teenage years. Would explain
there are no current medical or surgical treatments. Would continue to
follow and maximize vision with low vision aides. Most maintain
vision between 20/80-20/200 and can have productive careers. Stem
cell and neuroprotection research on the horizon.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
I would ask about immune status, specifically HIV. I would
perform a full eye exam, document fundus findings with fundus
photography, order a HIV test, and CD4 count (counts <50 very
high risk of cytomegalovirus (CMV)). I would also have
polymerase chain reaction (PCR) done to evaluate for CMV. I
would perform thorough retinal evaluation looking for retinal
holes and retinal detachment. I would work up atypical clinical
findings with testing for syphilis, cryptococcus, and
pneumocystiscarinil. Would consider neuroimaging for possible
intracranial involvement.

‘V Assessment:
©. This case likely represents a CMV retinitis.
Plan:

I would admit this patient and initiate treatment with intravenous
ganciclovir, foscarnet or cidofovir. For patients that progress
despite therapy or those that have macular encroachment can use
intravitreal injections of antiviral medications. Those who have
retinal detachments I would consider vitrectomy with silicon oil
and laser barricade to mitigate progression. For retinal holes I
would consider performing laser barricade for peripheral and
perimacular holes and explain to the patient that this treatment is
controversial with regard to benefit. Additionally I would work
with an internist to help with highly active antiretroviral therapy
(HAART) to improve CD4 count which will augment the
patient’s ocular stability.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
Would ask patient about prior knowledge of having an
intraocular lesion to help with onset and timing. Ask about
visual symptoms and history of other cancers. Would perform
an ultrasound to assess lesion height and look for extrascleral
extension. Obtain an A scan expecting to see low internal
reflectivity. The lesion in this case, nevus unlikely and
melanocytomas typically are more darkly pigmented and near
the optic nerve. Metastases are usually orange and multifocal.

| Vv Assessment:

© Choroidal melanoma is most likely diagnosis.
Plan:
o I would perform an ultrasound, as mentioned above. There is no

clear role for MRI or CT imaging of the orbit. 1 would consider
intravenous flouroscien angiography (IVFA) looking for double
circulation pattern. This is not required to make the diagnosis. I
would assess for metastases, especially to the liver and lungs and
would order liver function and blood count blood test, abdominal
imaging, and chest CT. I would also refer to an oncologist for
further evaluation and consultation for metastasis. Based on the
Collaborative Ocular Melanoma Study (COMS): would plan (1)
observation for small chorodial melanomas, (2) plaque
brachytherapy rather than enucleation for medium sized
choroidal melanomas, and (3) enucleation without preceding
radiation for large melanomas. This case is likely a medium-
sized melanoma. In centers that have access to proton beam
therapy, this would be my preferred mode of treatment.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
Would ask about history of trauma, laser therapy, living in Ohio
River Valley, and hypertension. Examine macula and periphery
looking for punched out chorioretinal lesions (histo spots). Look
for drusen or choroidal neovascular membrane (CNVM). Would
obtain an IVFA looking for focal leakage to classify pathology
as classic or occult. Would perform OCT for baseline retinal
thickness and define retinal pathology. If hemorrhage an issue |
can obtain indocyanine green (ICG) angiography to look for
“hairpin” turns of retinal angiomatous proliferation (RAP
lesion) or the polyp patterns seen in PCV.

Vv Assessment:

Neovascular AMD

Plan:

Use IVFA and OCT to determine extent of CNVM (i.e. occult or
classic). Would consider monthly treatment with anti-VEGF.
Would follow macular thickness/fluid via OCT. The ANCHOR
(Anti-VEGF vs. PDT) and MARINA (Anti-VEGF .3mg vs. mg
vs. Sham) trial showed that monthly anti- VEGF for 24 months
was effective at reducing vision loss and improving vision. The
Comparison of AMD Treatment Trial (CATT) proved Lucentis
and Avastin have equal efficacy. There are safety concerns for
Avastin versus Lucentis, but results are inconclusive. If lesion is
temporal to the fovea consider other options such as (1) photo-
dynamic therapy (PDT), as per the “Treatment of AMD with
PDT (TAP), or (2) laser photocoagulation per the “Macular
Photocoagulation Study (MPS). Neither study improved acuity
nor had rates of stability like MARINA and ANCHOR.

Crystalline retinopathy
=» Exogenous (tamoxifen, canthaxanthine, halothane anesthetic;

Il! History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

I would enquire about a history of breast cancer and Tamoxifen
therapy (dosage and duration), anesthesia with
halothane/methoxyflurane anesthetics, tanning chemicals, IV
drug use which can be contaminated with talc, diabetes or
hypertension which could cause hard exudates.

I would examine the contralateral eye for drusen, and would
examine both eyes for corneal crystals, which can be seen in
Bietti’s or cystinosis, and for evidence of subretinal fluid,
microaneurysms, and neovascularization that can cause hard
exudates, though the former would tend to be circinate.

If evidence of diabetes or neovascularization I would obtain an
intravenous flouroscien angiogram (IVFA), OCT, and a fasting
blood glucose.

Crystalline retinopathy (tamoxifen most likely based on pattern
of crystal deposits)

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o  If(1) macular edema (ME) within 200 microns of the fovea, or (2)

exudates within 500 microns of the fovea associated with ME, or (3)
one disc area (DA) of ME within 1 disc diameter (DD) of the center of
the fovea I would perform focal laser to leaking microaneurysms or a
grid laser per the “Early Treatment of Diabetic Retinopathy Study”
(ETDRS). Currently, a patient with diffuse edema anti-VEGF is
preferred (multiple injections need to be given) since it tends to give
better visual outcome. If there is only a small focal area of thickening,
laser maybe better. Patients with proliferative diabetic retinopathy
(PDR) and high-risk characteristics defined as (1) NVD > 1/3 of the
DA, (2) any NVD with preretinal/vitreous heme, (3) NVE> %ZDA
with preretinal/vitreous heme, or (4) any NVI/NVA, then PRP per the
diabetic retinopathy study (DRS) would be best. Although anti-VEGF
agents are being used successfully for NPDR and PDR.

cases.title: 47F - blurry vision for several weeks
cases.presentation: A 47-year-old woman reports blurry vision for several weeks.

questions.question: What does she have?
questions.answer:
A dome-shaped, exudative retinal detachment (RD) without corrugation or surface membranes. In
addition, there is no evidence of retinal traction. No obvious mass is seen in the picture.
keyPoints: A dome-shaped, exudative retinal detachment (RD) without corrugation or surface membranes. In
addition, there is no evidence of retinal traction. No obvious mass is seen in the picture.
scoringKeywords: A dome-shaped, exudative retinal detachment (RD) without corrugation or surface membranes. In
addition, there is no evidence of retinal traction. No obvious mass is seen in the picture.

questions.question: What are the possible etiologies?
questions.answer:
The causes of an exudative retinal detachment include uveitis (VKH, sympathetic ophthalmia, pars
planitis, posterior scleritis), tumors (especially retinal capillary hemangioma / von Hippel-Lindau
disease, choroidal hemangioma, choroidal malignant melanoma), and hypertension. Other causes are
glomerulonephritis, eclampsia / pre-eclampsia, hypothyroidism, Coats’ disease, scleritis, and central serous
retinopathy.
keyPoints: The causes of an exudative retinal detachment include uveitis (VKH, sympathetic ophthalmia, pars
planitis, posterior scleritis), tumors (especially retinal capillary hemangioma / von Hippel-Lindau
disease, choroidal hemangioma, choroidal malignant melanoma), and hypertension. Other causes are
glomerulonephritis, eclampsia / pre-eclampsia, hypothyroidism, Coats’ disease, scleritis, and central serous
retinopathy.
scoringKeywords: The causes of an exudative retinal detachment include uveitis (VKH, sympathetic ophthalmia, pars
planitis, posterior scleritis), tumors (especially retinal capillary hemangioma / von Hippel-Lindau
disease, choroidal hemangioma, choroidal malignant melanoma), and hypertension. Other causes are
glomerulonephritis, eclampsia / pre-eclampsia, hypothyroidism, Coats’ disease, scleritis, and central serous
retinopathy.

questions.question: What other exam findings would you look for?
questions.answer:
In general, exudative RDs are located inferiorly, display shifting fluid, and have a smooth appearance.
The appearance of a smooth, retinal detachment behind the lens is almost pathognomonic for exudative
RD. Chronic exudative RD can lead to neovascular glaucoma.
It is important to perform a careful depressed dilated fundus examination to evaluate for peripheral
retinal tears and breaks and rule out a rhegmatogenous retinal detachment; as well as evaluation for
traction membranes to rule out a traction retinal detachment. Traction RDs are usually taut and
immobile with a concave surface that does not extend to the ora serrata.

questions.question: What other tests would be helpful?
questions.answer:
A fluorescein angiogram (FA) is useful to evaluate for tumors (intrinsic vasculature, feeder vessels)
and vascular abnormalities. Indocyanine green angiography (ICG) is superior to FA to show intrinsic
vascularity, hot spots, and washout phenomenon for tumors. A B-scan ultrasound should be performed
to confirm the shifting fluid, evaluate choroidal thickness, and more importantly to evaluate for masses.
An A-scan ultrasound is used to evaluate internal reflectivity if a mass is found. Optical coherence
tomography (OCT) is useful to verify thickened choroid using enhanced depth imaging techniques in
uveitic conditions, image the subretinal fluid and / or cystoid macular edema. Although rarely necessary,
orbital imaging can be performed.
keyPoints: A fluorescein angiogram (FA) is useful to evaluate for tumors (intrinsic vasculature, feeder vessels)
and vascular abnormalities. Indocyanine green angiography (ICG) is superior to FA to show intrinsic
vascularity, hot spots, and washout phenomenon for tumors. A B-scan ultrasound should be performed
to confirm the shifting fluid, evaluate choroidal thickness, and more importantly to evaluate for masses.
An A-scan ultrasound is used to evaluate internal reflectivity if a mass is found. Optical coherence
tomography (OCT) is useful to verify thickened choroid using enhanced depth imaging techniques in
uveitic conditions, image the subretinal fluid and / or cystoid macular edema. Although rarely necessary,
rbital imaging can be performed.

vascularity, hot spots, and washout phenomenon for tumors. A B-scan ultrasound should be performed
to confirm the shifting fluid, evaluate choroidal thickness, and more importantly to evaluate for masses.
An A-scan ultrasound is used to evaluate internal reflectivity if a mass is found. Optical coherence
tomography (OCT) is useful to verify thickened choroid using enhanced depth imaging techniques in

questions.question: What is the most likely diagnosis and why?
questions.answer: Choroidal hemangioma because of the high internal reflectivity.
keyPoints: Choroidal hemangioma because of the high internal reflectivity.
scoringKeywords: Choroidal hemangioma because of the high internal reflectivity.

questions.question: How would you treat the patient?
questions.answer:
The decision to treat is individualized based on extent of symptoms, loss of vision, and potential for
visual recovery. The aim of treatment is to induce tumor atrophy with resolution of subretinal fluid and
tumor-induced foveal distortion without destroying function of overlying retina. The goal is not to
obliterate tumor. Treatment options consist of laser photocoagulation (moderately intense, white
reaction on the tumor surface to eliminate serous exudation), cryotherapy, ocular photodynamic therapy
(PDT) with verteporfin (Visudyne) using standard treatment parameters, transpupillary thermotherapy,
I-125 plaque brachytherapy, and low-dose external beam radiation therapy.
keyPoints: The decision to treat is individualized based on extent of symptoms, loss of vision, and potential for
visual recovery. The aim of treatment is to induce tumor atrophy with resolution of subretinal fluid and
tumor-induced foveal distortion without destroying function of overlying retina. The goal is not to

reaction on the tumor surface to eliminate serous exudation), cryotherapy, ocular photodynamic therapy

I-125 plaque brachytherapy, and low-dose external beam radiation therapy.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
Visual loss can be progressive and irreversible when the fovea is involved in chronic cases. Poor visual
acuity results can be expected despite resolution of fluid exudates from chronic macular edema and
photoreceptor loss.
keyPoints: Visual loss can be progressive and irreversible when the fovea is involved in chronic cases. Poor visual
acuity results can be expected despite resolution of fluid exudates from chronic macular edema and
photoreceptor loss.
scoringKeywords: Visual loss can be progressive and irreversible when the fovea is involved in chronic cases. Poor visual
acuity results can be expected despite resolution of fluid exudates from chronic macular edema and

cases.title: 44M - A 44-year-old man is worried about blurry vision in the...
cases.presentation: A 44-year-old man is worried about blurry vision in the right eye for the past 2 weeks.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Central serous chorioretinopathy (CSC), inflammatory choroidal disorders (VKH syndrome), uveal
effusion syndrome, optic nerve pit, choroidal tumor, vitelliform macular detachment, pigment epithelial
detachment from other causes including choroidal neovascularization (CNV).
keyPoints: Central serous chorioretinopathy (CSC), inflammatory choroidal disorders (VKH syndrome), uveal
effusion syndrome, optic nerve pit, choroidal tumor, vitelliform macular detachment, pigment epithelial
detachment from other causes including choroidal neovascularization (CNV).
scoringKeywords: Central serous chorioretinopathy (CSC), inflammatory choroidal disorders (VKH syndrome), uveal
effusion syndrome, optic nerve pit, choroidal tumor, vitelliform macular detachment, pigment epithelial
detachment from other causes including choroidal neovascularization (CNV).

questions.question: What tests would be helpful?
questions.answer:
OCT to characterize the features present along with the obvious subretinal fluid including checking for
macular schisis or optic nerve excavations seen with an optic nerve pit, a thickened choroid that is seen
in VKH, central serous chorioretinopathy, and uveal effusion syndrome, or the characteristic OCT
appearance of vitelliform lesions. A fluorescein angiogram (FA) would be useful to rule out choroidal
neovascularization (although rare in this age group) and to visualize the hyperfluorescence early with
late pooling of a pigment epithelial detachment and the subretinal fluid. If both these tests fail to
determine the diagnosis, indocyanine green angiography (ICG) can show hyperfluorescence with late
staining in CSC, vascularity with tumors, and rule out CNV.

macular schisis or optic nerve excavations seen with an optic nerve pit, a thickened choroid that is seen

appearance of vitelliform lesions. A fluorescein angiogram (FA) would be useful to rule out choroidal

questions.question: What is demonstrated, and what is the diagnosis?
questions.answer:
The FA shows ‘smoke-stack’ leakage into the pigment epithelial detachment characteristic of CSC;
however, this classic appearance is seen in only about 10% of cases.
keyPoints: The FA shows ‘smoke-stack’ leakage into the pigment epithelial detachment characteristic of CSC;
however, this classic appearance is seen in only about 10% of cases.
scoringKeywords: The FA shows ‘smoke-stack’ leakage into the pigment epithelial detachment characteristic of CSC;
however, this classic appearance is seen in only about 10% of cases.

questions.question: How would you manage this patient?
questions.answer:
Observation initially since most cases resolve spontaneously over 6 weeks. Laser treatment or verteporfin
(Visudyne) ocular photodynamic therapy (PDT) can be considered for patients who require more rapid
visual recovery because of occupational reasons, poor vision in the fellow eye due to CSC, no resolution
of fluid after several months, recurrent episodes with poor vision, or in severe forms of CSC. Treatment
reduces the duration of symptoms but does not affect the final visual acuity. Finally, experimental use of
oral rifampin and mifepristone (RU486) has recently been suggested especially in chronic or bilateral
cases.

cases.title: 49M - acute eye pain with decreased vision and photophobia
cases.presentation: A 49-year-old man complains of acute eye pain with decreased vision and photophobia.

questions.question: What does the photo demonstrate?
questions.answer: A well-defined area of retinal necrosis with retinal whitening and intraretinal hemorrhages.
keyPoints: A well-defined area of retinal necrosis with retinal whitening and intraretinal hemorrhages.
scoringKeywords: A well-defined area of retinal necrosis with retinal whitening and intraretinal hemorrhages.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Acute retinal necrosis, progressive outer retinal necrosis (PORN), syphilis, cytomegalovirus retinitis,
toxoplasmosis, sclopoteria, lymphoma, sarcoidosis, and aminoglycoside toxicity.
keyPoints: Acute retinal necrosis, progressive outer retinal necrosis (PORN), syphilis, cytomegalovirus retinitis,
toxoplasmosis, sclopoteria, lymphoma, sarcoidosis, and aminoglycoside toxicity.
scoringKeywords: Acute retinal necrosis, progressive outer retinal necrosis (PORN), syphilis, cytomegalovirus retinitis,
toxoplasmosis, sclopoteria, lymphoma, sarcoidosis, and aminoglycoside toxicity.

questions.question: What other findings would you look for on exam?
questions.answer: Evidence of granulomatous anterior uveitis, vitritis, and retinal vasculitis.
keyPoints: Evidence of granulomatous anterior uveitis, vitritis, and retinal vasculitis.
scoringKeywords: Evidence of granulomatous anterior uveitis, vitritis, and retinal vasculitis.

questions.question: What lab tests would be helpful?
questions.answer:
PCR testing of intraocular fluid is the most precise method to determine the cause of the viral retinitis:
herpes zoster virus (HZV), herpes simplex virus (HSV), or, rarely, cytomegalovirus (CMV). Alternatively,
blood testing for HZV and HSV (type 1 and 2) immunoglobulin G and M titers can be performed. The
immune status should be obtained to verify whether the patient is immunocompetent as it is important
to differentiate ARN from PORN that occurs in immunocompromised patients with minimal
inflammation and vasculitis.

herpes zoster virus (HZV), herpes simplex virus (HSV), or, rarely, cytomegalovirus (CMV). Alternatively,
blood testing for HZV and HSV (type 1 and 2) immunoglobulin G and M titers can be performed. The
immune status should be obtained to verify whether the patient is immunocompetent as it is important

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Acute retinal necrosis.
keyPoints: Acute retinal necrosis.
scoringKeywords: Acute retinal necrosis.

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Immediately treat with antivirals since any delay in therapy can cause a dramatic increase in the retinitis.
Typically, systemic acyclovir (IV until resolution of the retinitis then oral for 1–2 months); alternatively, if
the lesions are more peripheral then use oral therapy with valacyclovir instead of IV therapy. Ganciclovir
is an alternative. It is important to follow blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine levels for
nephrotoxicity. Both oral and topical steroids can be started after the patient begins to respond to
prevent inflammatory complications.
keyPoints: Immediately treat with antivirals since any delay in therapy can cause a dramatic increase in the retinitis.

the lesions are more peripheral then use oral therapy with valacyclovir instead of IV therapy. Ganciclovir
is an alternative. It is important to follow blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine levels for
nephrotoxicity. Both oral and topical steroids can be started after the patient begins to respond to
prevent inflammatory complications.

questions.question: What are the potential complications?
questions.answer:
Patients with ARN are at high risk of developing rhegmatogenous retinal detachments with numerous
holes and giant tears due to retinal necrosis. In addition, very careful observation of the fellow eye is
important to rule out involvement.
keyPoints: Patients with ARN are at high risk of developing rhegmatogenous retinal detachments with numerous
holes and giant tears due to retinal necrosis. In addition, very careful observation of the fellow eye is
important to rule out involvement.
scoringKeywords: Patients with ARN are at high risk of developing rhegmatogenous retinal detachments with numerous
holes and giant tears due to retinal necrosis. In addition, very careful observation of the fellow eye is
important to rule out involvement.

cases.title: 65M - blurred vision and floaters in both eyes for several...
cases.presentation: A 65-year-old man presents with blurred vision and floaters in both eyes for several months. On exam, his eyes are white and quiet, and there are bilateral dense sheets of vitreous cells. He says he has been treated by an outside doctor with topical steroids with no response.

questions.question: What additional history would you want to know?
questions.answer:
Has he had any unexplained fevers and chills, night sweats, fatigue, headaches, or weight loss? What is
the past medical history, especially any cancer history? Is there any lymphadenopathy (especially cervical
and supraclavicular), or any CNS symptoms?

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Anything that produces a chronic vitritis including infectious and non-infectious uveitis such as birdshot
chorioretinopathy, pars planitis, toxoplasmosis, syphilis, sarcoidosis, multifocal choroiditis, acute
posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy, Behçets disease, tuberculosis, and acute retinal
necrosis; however, for a patient in this age group with these signs, primary intraocular lymphoma (PIOL)
is of greatest concern.

necrosis; however, for a patient in this age group with these signs, primary intraocular lymphoma (PIOL)

questions.question:
What tests would you perform?
Additional findings: the pathology from a vitreous biopsy shows:
questions.answer:
Since PIOL is a masquerade syndrome, complete laboratory testing to rule out uveitis entities should be
done including CBC, ESR, angiotensin-converting enzyme level, HLA-B 27 and 51, ANA test, and VDRL
or RPR, FTA-ABS or MHA-TP testing. For additional testing paradigm see Case 105.
With negative laboratory testing, a thorough neurological evaluation should be performed in search of
CNS involvement including magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain and lumbar puncture for
CNS cytology. If both are negative then a diagnostic pars plana vitrectomy should be performed. An
undilute vitreous biopsy (approximately 1 cc) should be sent for cytology, flow-cytometry analysis for
B- and T-cell markers and kappa / lambda light chains. Other ancillary tests include the measurement of
IL-6 and IL-10 (high IL-10 and high ratio of IL-10 to IL-6 are suggestive of intraocular lymphoma).

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Diffuse large B-cell non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma.
keyPoints: Diffuse large B-cell non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma.
scoringKeywords: Diffuse large B-cell non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma.

questions.question: How would you treat this patient?
questions.answer:
The treatment of primary intraocular lymphoma is still controversial and includes intravitreal
methotrexate and / or rituximab; and orbital radiation in cases without any CNS involvement. However,
the majority of patients do develop CNS involvement, so most oncologists treat with chemotherapy with
blood–brain barrier disruption or high-dose systemic methotrexate.

methotrexate and / or rituximab; and orbital radiation in cases without any CNS involvement. However,
the majority of patients do develop CNS involvement, so most oncologists treat with chemotherapy with
blood–brain barrier disruption or high-dose systemic methotrexate.
scoringKeywords: The treatment of primary intraocular lymphoma is still controversial and includes intravitreal
methotrexate and / or rituximab; and orbital radiation in cases without any CNS involvement. However,
the majority of patients do develop CNS involvement, so most oncologists treat with chemotherapy with

questions.question: What additional testing should be performed?
questions.answer: A complete metastatic survey (imaging studies of the chest and abdomen) and bone marrow biopsy.
keyPoints: A complete metastatic survey (imaging studies of the chest and abdomen) and bone marrow biopsy.
scoringKeywords: A complete metastatic survey (imaging studies of the chest and abdomen) and bone marrow biopsy.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
60–80% will develop CNS lymphoma within a mean of 29 months. The prognosis is poor if there is
brain involvement.

brain involvement.

questions.question: How would you work up a patient with granulomatous uveitis?
questions.answer:
A basic battery of lab tests is recommended to determine the cause of granulomatous uveitis in a patient
with a negative history, review of systems, and medical examination. This includes complete blood count
(CBC) with differential, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), Venereal Disease Research Laboratory
(VDRL; syphilis) or rapid plasma reagin (RPR; syphilis), treponemal antibody absorption test (FTA-ABS;
syphilis) or microhemagglutination assay for treponema pallidum (MHA-TP; syphilis), ELISA or indirect
immunofluorescence assay (IFA) for toxoplasma IgM and IgG titers, angiotensin-converting enzyme
(ACE; sarcoidosis), lysozyme.
Other labs tests can be ordered according to the patient’s history including antinuclear antibody (ANA),
rheumatoid factor (RF; juvenile rheumatoid arthritis), ELISA for Lyme immunoglobulin M (IgM) and
immunoglobulin G (IgG), HIV antibody test, chest radiographs or CT scan (sarcoidosis, tuberculosis),
sacroiliac radiographs (ankylosing spondylitis), and urinalysis.
Special diagnostic lab tests can also be considered if the diagnosis is still unclear including HLA typing
(HLA-A29: Birdshot choroidopathy), in the presence of vasculitis: ANCA (Wegener’s granulomatosis,
polyarteritis nodosa), Raji cell and C1q binding assays for circulating immune complexes (SLE, systemic
vasculitides), complement proteins: C3, C4, total complement (SLE, cryoglobulinemia,
glomerulonephritis), and soluble IL-2 receptor.
keyPoints: A basic battery of lab tests is recommended to determine the cause of granulomatous uveitis in a patient
with a negative history, review of systems, and medical examination. This includes complete blood count
(CBC) with differential, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), Venereal Disease Research Laboratory
(VDRL; syphilis) or rapid plasma reagin (RPR; syphilis), treponemal antibody absorption test (FTA-ABS;
syphilis) or microhemagglutination assay for treponema pallidum (MHA-TP; syphilis), ELISA or indirect
immunofluorescence assay (IFA) for toxoplasma IgM and IgG titers, angiotensin-converting enzyme
(ACE; sarcoidosis), lysozyme.
Other labs tests can be ordered according to the patient’s history including antinuclear antibody (ANA),

with a negative history, review of systems, and medical examination. This includes complete blood count

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Toxoplasmosis chorioretinitis.
keyPoints: Toxoplasmosis chorioretinitis.
scoringKeywords: Toxoplasmosis chorioretinitis.

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Topical steroids and cycloplegic are prescribed to treat the anterior inflammation. Systemic steroids are
added for posterior pole lesions or those with intense inflammation.
Small peripheral lesions may be observed since they often heal spontaneously especially in
immunocompetent individuals. If a patient has decreased vision, moderate to severe vitreous
inflammation, or lesions that threaten the macula, papillomacular bundle, or optic nerve he / she should
be treated for 4–6 weeks with antibiotics that kill tachyzoites in the retina (note: they do not affect
cysts). Most patients respond well to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim). For aggressive lesions or
posterior pole lesions, triple therapy can be considered with pyrimethamine (Daraprim), folinic acid
(leucovorin), and one of the following: sulfadiazine, clindamycin, clarithromycin, azithromycin, or
atovaquone. Immunocompromised patients and high-risk patients may require prophylactic treatment.

questions.question: What abnormality is present, and what is the likely cause?
questions.answer: Iris defect due to an intraocular foreign body (IOFB).
keyPoints: Iris defect due to an intraocular foreign body (IOFB).

questions.question: How would you work up this patient?
questions.answer:
Ask a detailed history about the type of work he was performing (i.e. hammering, sawing, power tools)?
Did he wear eye protection? Did he feel something hit or poke his eye?
Obtain an orbital CT or X-ray to identify any intraocular foreign body (not an MRI if a metallic IOFB is
suspected).

questions.question: How are foreign bodies classified?
questions.answer:
Inert: do not require removal (i.e. glass, plastic, sand, stone, ceramic, gold, platinum, silver, aluminum).
Reactive: cause inflammation / toxicity and must be removed (i.e. copper [≥85% causes severe
endophthalmitis, <85% causes chalcosis, <70% is relatively inert], iron [siderosis], wood / plant material
[significant inflammation and higher risk of endophthalmitis]).
keyPoints: Inert: do not require removal (i.e. glass, plastic, sand, stone, ceramic, gold, platinum, silver, aluminum).
Reactive: cause inflammation / toxicity and must be removed (i.e. copper [≥85% causes severe
endophthalmitis, <85% causes chalcosis, <70% is relatively inert], iron [siderosis], wood / plant material
[significant inflammation and higher risk of endophthalmitis]).
scoringKeywords: Inert: do not require removal (i.e. glass, plastic, sand, stone, ceramic, gold, platinum, silver, aluminum).
Reactive: cause inflammation / toxicity and must be removed (i.e. copper [≥85% causes severe
endophthalmitis, <85% causes chalcosis, <70% is relatively inert], iron [siderosis], wood / plant material
[significant inflammation and higher risk of endophthalmitis]).

questions.question: What findings are seen in chalcosis and siderosis?
questions.answer:
Chalcosis: mild intraocular inflammation, deposition of copper in the anterior lens capsule (sunflower
cataract) and Descemet’s membrane (Kayser-Fleisher ring), and retinal degeneration. The iris may
become green and the pupil sluggishly reactive to light.
Siderosis: iris heterochromia (hyperchromic on involved side), mid-dilated minimally reactive pupil, lens
discoloration (brown-orange dots from iron deposition in lens epithelium, generalized yellowing from
involvement of cortex), vitritis, pigmentary RPE degeneration with sclerosis of vessels, retinal thinning,
and atrophy. In both diseases, the ERG is reduced or even absent.
keyPoints: Chalcosis: mild intraocular inflammation, deposition of copper in the anterior lens capsule (sunflower
cataract) and Descemet’s membrane (Kayser-Fleisher ring), and retinal degeneration. The iris may
become green and the pupil sluggishly reactive to light.
Siderosis: iris heterochromia (hyperchromic on involved side), mid-dilated minimally reactive pupil, lens
discoloration (brown-orange dots from iron deposition in lens epithelium, generalized yellowing from
involvement of cortex), vitritis, pigmentary RPE degeneration with sclerosis of vessels, retinal thinning,
and atrophy. In both diseases, the ERG is reduced or even absent.
scoringKeywords: Chalcosis: mild intraocular inflammation, deposition of copper in the anterior lens capsule (sunflower
cataract) and Descemet’s membrane (Kayser-Fleisher ring), and retinal degeneration. The iris may
become green and the pupil sluggishly reactive to light.
Siderosis: iris heterochromia (hyperchromic on involved side), mid-dilated minimally reactive pupil, lens
discoloration (brown-orange dots from iron deposition in lens epithelium, generalized yellowing from
involvement of cortex), vitritis, pigmentary RPE degeneration with sclerosis of vessels, retinal thinning,
and atrophy. In both diseases, the ERG is reduced or even absent.

questions.question: How would you treat this patient?
questions.answer:
Surgical exploration and repair with removal of any reactive FB material should be performed as soon as
possible. The use of intravitreal and systemic antibiotics is controversial but usually performed to prevent
endophthalmitis.

possible. The use of intravitreal and systemic antibiotics is controversial but usually performed to prevent
endophthalmitis.

possible. The use of intravitreal and systemic antibiotics is controversial but usually performed to prevent

questions.question: What findings are depicted?
questions.answer: Multiple cotton wool spots (CWS) and one intraretinal hemorrhage.
keyPoints: Multiple cotton wool spots (CWS) and one intraretinal hemorrhage.
scoringKeywords: Multiple cotton wool spots (CWS) and one intraretinal hemorrhage.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
The most common causes of CWS are diabetes and hypertension. Other causes include: ischemic (retinal
vein occlusion, ocular ischemic syndrome, severe anemia, pre-eclampsia, carotid artery obstruction),
embolic (carotid emboli, cardiac emboli, deep venous emboli, white blood cell emboli [Purtcher’s
retinopathy], severe chest compression / long bone fractures, foreign bodies [IVDA], amniotic fluid
embolization), infectious (HIV, Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever, cat-scratch fever [Bartonella henselae],
toxoplasmosis, subacute bacterial endocarditis, leptospirosis, onchocerciasis [river blindness], fungemia),
toxic (interferon, methotrexate), radiation induced, neoplastic (lymphoma, leukemia, metastatic
carcinoma, Hodgkin’s disease), epiretinal membrane traction, immune-mediated (systemic lupus
erythematosis, sarcoidosis, dermatomyositis, polyarteritis nordosa, scleroderma, giant cell arteritis,
cryoglobulinemia), traumatic nerve fiber layer laceration (note: CWS will not resolve), blood diseases
(aplastic anemia, dysproteinemia, pernicious anemia [vitamin B12 deficiency]), hyperviscosity syndromes
(multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia), hypercoagulability syndromes (factor V Leiden,
prothrombin 20210A, hyperhomocysteinemia, protein S/C deficiency, antithrombin III deficiency,
dysfibrinogenemia, factor XII deficiency) and idiopathic.

questions.question: How would you work up this patient?
questions.answer:
A very careful history (radiation, chemotherapy, trauma) and review of systems are important to guide
the laboratory evaluation. The patient needs a systemic workup for diabetes and hypertension. Initial
blood tests include CBC and differential, platelet count, glycosylated hemoglobin, ANA, and HIV
antibody test. Depending on the patient’s cardiovascular risk factors, a workup looking for cardiac
valvular disease, carotid stenosis or deep venous sources of emboli may be done. Infectious workup is
based on other systemic symptoms.

months.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Macular hole, pseudohole, lamellar hole, cystoid macular edema, solar retinopathy (usually bilateral),
epiretinal membrane, and exudative maculopathies including central serous chorioretinopathy (CSC) and
wet age-related macular degeneration (AMD).
keyPoints: Macular hole, pseudohole, lamellar hole, cystoid macular edema, solar retinopathy (usually bilateral),
epiretinal membrane, and exudative maculopathies including central serous chorioretinopathy (CSC) and
wet age-related macular degeneration (AMD).
scoringKeywords: Macular hole, pseudohole, lamellar hole, cystoid macular edema, solar retinopathy (usually bilateral),
epiretinal membrane, and exudative maculopathies including central serous chorioretinopathy (CSC) and

questions.question:
How would you evaluate this patient?
Additional findings: the OCT shows:
questions.answer:
Perform a dilated fundus examination with evaluation for a Watzke-Allen sign (absolute scotoma in the
hole).
An optical coherence tomography (OCT) scan differentiates a macular hole from other entities including
lamellar hole, pseudohole, solar retinopathy, cystoid macular edema, central serous chorioretinopathy,
epiretinal membrane, and vitreomacular traction syndrome. It also illustrates intraretinal abnormalities
including cystoid macular edema, intraretinal thickening, and the amount of traction on the edges of the
hole. OCT is useful for staging the hole:
Stage 1: premacular or impending hole with foveal detachment, decreased / absent foveal depression, and
macular cyst (1A = yellow spot, 100-200 µm in diameter, 1B = yellow ring, 200-300 µm in diameter).
There is no PVD, Weiss’ ring, or vitreofoveal separation.
Stage 2: early, small, full-thickness hole either centrally within the ring or eccentrically at the ring’s
margin. The OCT shows the partial thickness opening of the hole.
Stage 3: full-thickness hole (<400 µm) often with yellow deposits at level of retinal pigment epithelium
(Klein’s tags), operculum over the hole within the hyaloid face, cuff of subretinal fluid at hole edges,
cystoid macular edema at hole edges, absence of a Weiss ring, and positive Watzke-Allen sign (subjective
interruption of slit beam on biomicroscopy).
Stage 4: stage 3 and posterior vitreous detachment (PVD).
A fluorescein angiogram is generally not required to differentiate this entity from others. It would show a
window defect corresponding to the hole.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Stage 3 macular hole.
keyPoints: Stage 3 macular hole.
scoringKeywords: Stage 3 macular hole.

questions.question: What are the risk factors?
questions.answer:
Cystoid macular edema, vitreomacular traction on the macula, trauma, postsurgical, post-laser treatment
and postinflammatory disorders.
keyPoints: Cystoid macular edema, vitreomacular traction on the macula, trauma, postsurgical, post-laser treatment
and postinflammatory disorders.
scoringKeywords: Cystoid macular edema, vitreomacular traction on the macula, trauma, postsurgical, post-laser treatment
and postinflammatory disorders.

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Stage 1 and many stage 2 holes are usually observed for spontaneous release of the hyaloid and hole
closure. Stage 2 and higher holes are treated with pars plana vitrectomy with release of traction from the
edges of the hole and placement of a nonexpansile, gas tamponade (usually sulfur hexafluoride) with
face-down positioning for several days to 1 week after surgery.

closure. Stage 2 and higher holes are treated with pars plana vitrectomy with release of traction from the
edges of the hole and placement of a nonexpansile, gas tamponade (usually sulfur hexafluoride) with
face-down positioning for several days to 1 week after surgery.
scoringKeywords: Stage 1 and many stage 2 holes are usually observed for spontaneous release of the hyaloid and hole
closure. Stage 2 and higher holes are treated with pars plana vitrectomy with release of traction from the
edges of the hole and placement of a nonexpansile, gas tamponade (usually sulfur hexafluoride) with
face-down positioning for several days to 1 week after surgery.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
The prognosis depends on the duration and size of the hole. It is good for recent onset and smaller
diameter holes, as well as patients with good preoperative visual acuity. The prognosis is poor for holes
>1 year duration and >400 µm wide. Surgery is successful anatomically in 80–100% of cases depending
on duration of the hole and hole width, with 65–85% of patients gaining 3 or more lines of visual
acuity.

1 year duration and >400 µm wide. Surgery is successful anatomically in 80–100% of cases depending
n duration of the hole and hole width, with 65–85% of patients gaining 3 or more lines of visual
acuity.

keyPoints: Separation of the posterior hyaloid face from the retina with a Weiss’ ring.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD).
keyPoints: Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD).

questions.question: What other examination findings would you look for?
questions.answer:
Retinal tear (seen in 10–15% of PVDs) and rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RD) especially when
pigmented anterior vitreous cells are present, and vitreous hemorrhage (seen in 7.5% of PVDs) from a
torn vessel during vitreous separation (70% risk of a retinal tear).

questions.question: If the retinal exam is normal, how would you manage this patient?
questions.answer:
Instruct patient in the signs / symptoms of a retinal tear / detachment and repeat a dilated retinal exam in
4–6 weeks.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Retinal tear and rhegmatogenous retinal detachment.
keyPoints: Retinal tear and rhegmatogenous retinal detachment.
scoringKeywords: Retinal tear and rhegmatogenous retinal detachment.

questions.question: What are the risk factors?
questions.answer:
Risk factors for retinal tears include age, history of RD in fellow eye (15%), high myopia / axial length
(7%), family history, lattice degeneration, trauma, cataract surgery (1% after ICCE; 0.1% after ECCE with
intact posterior capsule), diabetes, and Nd : YAG laser posterior capsulotomy.

(7%), family history, lattice degeneration, trauma, cataract surgery (1% after ICCE; 0.1% after ECCE with
intact posterior capsule), diabetes, and Nd : YAG laser posterior capsulotomy.
scoringKeywords: Risk factors for retinal tears include age, history of RD in fellow eye (15%), high myopia / axial length
(7%), family history, lattice degeneration, trauma, cataract surgery (1% after ICCE; 0.1% after ECCE with

questions.question: What is the etiology?
questions.answer:
Retinal tears occur when the vitreous detaches posteriorly and reaches a site with firmer attachment. The
vitreous remains attached at the posterior margin of the retinal flap creating the horseshoe appearance.
With additional traction, retinal tissue may avulse leading to an operculum over a round / oval hole.
keyPoints: Retinal tears occur when the vitreous detaches posteriorly and reaches a site with firmer attachment. The
vitreous remains attached at the posterior margin of the retinal flap creating the horseshoe appearance.
With additional traction, retinal tissue may avulse leading to an operculum over a round / oval hole.
scoringKeywords: Retinal tears occur when the vitreous detaches posteriorly and reaches a site with firmer attachment. The
vitreous remains attached at the posterior margin of the retinal flap creating the horseshoe appearance.
With additional traction, retinal tissue may avulse leading to an operculum over a round / oval hole.

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Horseshoe tears and symptomatic retinal holes should be treated with laser photocoagulation or
cryopexy to prevent retinal detachment. Asymptomatic retinal holes can be observed. In this case, the
patient has developed a rhegmatogenous retinal detachment that requires surgical repair. If the tears are
superior and localized to 1 clock hour, the patient is a candidate for pneumatic retinopexy. If not, a
scleral buckle or primary pars plana vitrectomy should be performed.

cases.title: 57F - blurred vision, nyctalopia, and peripheral photopsias in...
cases.presentation: A 57-year-old woman complains of blurred vision, nyctalopia, and peripheral photopsias in both eyes for 3 days.

questions.question: What does the fundus photo show?
questions.answer:
Multiple, small, discrete, ovoid, creamy yellow-white spots at the level of the choroid and RPE scattered
like a birdshot blast from a shotgun in the midperiphery radiating from the optic nerve. Notably, the
lesions are not pigmented.
keyPoints: Multiple, small, discrete, ovoid, creamy yellow-white spots at the level of the choroid and RPE scattered
like a birdshot blast from a shotgun in the midperiphery radiating from the optic nerve. Notably, the
lesions are not pigmented.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
The differential diagnosis includes inflammatory disorders such as birdshot chorioretinopathy, other
white dot syndromes (MEWDS, APMPPE), pars planitis, punctate inner choroidopathy, and sarcoidosis;
infectious etiologies such as tuberculosis, syphilis, diffuse unilateral subacute neuroretinitis (DUSN), and
toxoplasmosis; masquerade processes such as ocular lymphoma, metastatic disease, and choroidal
lymphoproliferative diseases.
keyPoints: The differential diagnosis includes inflammatory disorders such as birdshot chorioretinopathy, other
white dot syndromes (MEWDS, APMPPE), pars planitis, punctate inner choroidopathy, and sarcoidosis;
infectious etiologies such as tuberculosis, syphilis, diffuse unilateral subacute neuroretinitis (DUSN), and
toxoplasmosis; masquerade processes such as ocular lymphoma, metastatic disease, and choroidal
lymphoproliferative diseases.
scoringKeywords: The differential diagnosis includes inflammatory disorders such as birdshot chorioretinopathy, other
white dot syndromes (MEWDS, APMPPE), pars planitis, punctate inner choroidopathy, and sarcoidosis;

toxoplasmosis; masquerade processes such as ocular lymphoma, metastatic disease, and choroidal
lymphoproliferative diseases.

questions.question: What ancillary studies would you obtain to make the diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Laboratory testing for HLA typing would be most useful.
An FA is useful to characterize the lesions. Birdshot lesions show mild hyperfluorescence early (active
lesions may hypofluoresce early) and late staining. Late views show profuse vascular incompetence with
leakage, petalloid cystoid macular edema, and secondary retinal staining. The phenomena of ‘quenching,’
where dye seems to disappear rapidly from the retinal circulation, can be seen in these patients.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Birdshot chorioretinitis, also known as vitiliginous chorioretinitis, is associated with HLA-A29 (90–98%),
although 7% of the general population is positive for HLA-A29.

questions.question: What other exam findings would you look for?
questions.answer:
Other signs of birdshot include mild vitritis, mild anterior chamber cells and flare (in 25% of cases),
cystoid macular edema, retinal vasculitis, and variable amount of disc edema. Late findings include optic
atrophy and epiretinal membranes. Macular CNV is rare.

questions.question: What is the epidemiology of this disease?
questions.answer:
Birdshot chorioretinopathy is a rare uveitis that occurs in 50- to 60-year-old females (70%) and almost
exclusively in Caucasians of northern European descent.
keyPoints: Birdshot chorioretinopathy is a rare uveitis that occurs in 50- to 60-year-old females (70%) and almost
exclusively in Caucasians of northern European descent.

questions.question: How would you treat this patient?
questions.answer:
Treatment is reserved for patients with decreased visual acuity, significant inflammation, or complications
including cystoid macular edema.
Despite historically poor responses to steroids, initial improvement can be seen with oral steroids.
Intravitreal or sub-Tenon’s steroid injection is performed in patients with severe inflammation or cystoid
macular edema. Early introduction of steroid-sparing agents, immunomodulatory agents including
cyclosporine, azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, daclizumab, methotrexate, or intravenous polyclonal
immunoglobulin can also be considered.

including cystoid macular edema.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
Birdshot is a chronic, slowly progressive, recurrent disease with variable visual prognosis. Most patients
lose vision from chronic CME. Rarely, it is a self-limited disease.

cases.title: 28F - sudden vision loss in her left eye after having flu-like...
cases.presentation: A 28-year-old woman reports sudden vision loss in her left eye after having flu-like symptoms the week before.

questions.question: What findings are shown?
questions.answer: Marked optic disc edema with disc hemorrhage and lipid exudation in a star shape.
keyPoints: Marked optic disc edema with disc hemorrhage and lipid exudation in a star shape.
scoringKeywords: Marked optic disc edema with disc hemorrhage and lipid exudation in a star shape.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
The patient has a macular star exudate. Infectious etiologies to consider and rule out include Bartonella
henselae, syphillis, Lyme disease, tuberculosis, tularemia, toxoplasmosis, viral retinitis (HSV, HZV, EBV),
DUSN, and toxocariasis. Non-infectious etiologies to consider include hypertensive retinopathy, diabetic
retinopathy, anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION), retinal vein occlusion, acute macular
neuroretinopathy, sarcoidosis, and papilledema.

questions.answer:
Check blood pressure. Laboratory testing for VDRL or RPR, FTA-ABS or MHA-TP, purified protein
derivative (PPD), and indirect fluorescent antibody test for Bartonella henselae.

derivative (PPD), and indirect fluorescent antibody test for Bartonella henselae.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Neuroretinitis, also known as Leber’s idiopathic stellate neuroretinitis, is due to a pleomorphic,
Gram-negative, bacillus called Bartonella henselae (formerly known as Rochalimaea), which is associated
with cat-scratch disease.

questions.question: How would you treat this patient?
questions.answer:
There are no definitive treatment guidelines given the self-limited and good prognosis. The use of
systemic antibiotics (doxycycline, rifampin, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, trimethoprim [Bactrim]) and
steroids are controversial. The typical regimen for an immunocompetent patient is doxycycline for 2–4
weeks. For severe infections, intravenous doxycycline can be given along with rifampin.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
Good with 67% regaining ≥20/20 vision, and 97% regaining >20/40 vision. The disc edema resolves over
8–12 weeks, whereas the macular star takes longer, resolving over 6–12 months. Optic atrophy and
retinal pigment epithelial changes may develop late.

questions.question: What does the photo depict?
questions.answer:
Small, round, yellow-brown, punched-out chorioretinal lesions (‘histo spots’) in the midperiphery and
posterior pole, and juxtapapillary atrophic changes. In the macula there is a pigmented chorioretinal scar
with choroidal neovascularization. The media is notably clear, indicating no vitreous haze.

questions.answer:

anti-VEGF agents (ranibizumab, bevacizumab, aflibercept, pegaptanib) are used to treat POHS related
CNV with good success.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Surgery: removal of CNV with subretinal surgery was evaluated in Subretinal Surgery Trials (Group
H); however, surgery was not found to be beneficial in patients with vision better than 20/100.
Patients with vision worse than 20/100 had a better chance of improving vision over 2 years of
follow up. There was a high rate of CNV recurrence, cataract formation, and retinal detachment.

questions.question: What are the risk factors for this disease?
questions.answer:
Endemic in the Ohio and Mississippi river valleys, the fungus is present on feathers of chickens, pigeons,
and blackbirds, in addition to infected bat droppings. Humans inhale fungus, which then disseminates
into the bloodstream. POHS is rare in African Americans. It is associated with HLA-B7.

questions.question: What other clinical findings are common?
questions.answer:
Over 60% of cases are bilateral. Most patients are asymptomatic unless they develop CNV. The 4
characteristic findings of POHS are: punched-out chorioretinal lesions (histo-spots), peripapillary
atrophic pigmentary changes, lack of vitritis, and CNV.

questions.answer:
The differential diagnosis of a ‘bull’s eye’ maculopathy includes cone and cone–rod dystrophy, AMD,
Stargardt’s disease / fundus flavimaculatus, central areolar choroidal dystrophy, chloroquine /

questions.question: What tests would you obtain?
questions.answer:
An FA would demonstrate the ‘bull’s eye’ pattern of hypofluorescence with a ring of hyperfluorescence
that is often visible before the fundus lesion. It would rule out the ‘dark choroid’ seen in Stargardt’s
disease. OCT shows thinning of the retina in the area of the bull’s eye with absent inner / outer segment
junction in a ring pattern. This pattern is very characteristic and obviates the need for FA in many
patients.

that is often visible before the fundus lesion. It would rule out the ‘dark choroid’ seen in Stargardt’s
disease. OCT shows thinning of the retina in the area of the bull’s eye with absent inner / outer segment
junction in a ring pattern. This pattern is very characteristic and obviates the need for FA in many
patients.

questions.question: What other history would be helpful?
questions.answer:
It is important to ask the patient specifically about systemic lupus erythematosis, rheumatoid arthritis,
short-term pulse treatment for graft versus host disease, and amebiasis, the use of toxic medications such
as quinolones, and a history of any relatives with a similar eye problem to rule out a hereditary
maculopathy.
keyPoints: It is important to ask the patient specifically about systemic lupus erythematosis, rheumatoid arthritis,
short-term pulse treatment for graft versus host disease, and amebiasis, the use of toxic medications such
as quinolones, and a history of any relatives with a similar eye problem to rule out a hereditary
maculopathy.

maculopathy.

questions.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Hydroxychloroquine retinal toxicity. Quinolones, first used as an antimalarial agents in World War II, are
now used to treat systemic lupus erythematosis, rheumatoid arthritis, short-term pulse treatment for graft
versus host disease, and amebiasis.

questions.question: What are the risk factors for this problem?
questions.answer:
Doses >3 mg/kg/day or 300 g total of chloroquine, and >6.5 mg/kg/day (<400 mg/day appears safe) or
700 g total of hydroxychloroquine may produce the maculopathy. The total daily dose seems more
critical than the total cumulative dose. The maculopathy often progresses after medications are
discontinued because the drug concentrates in the eye. Obesity, kidney or liver disease, and older age are
other risk factors for retinal toxicity in patients taking these medicines.

questions.question: How would you follow this patient?
questions.answer:
Check visual acuity, red Amsler grid, and visual fields (central 10° with red test object) at baseline and
every 6 months (chloroquine) or 12 months (hydroxychloroquine) while the patient is taking the
medication. Color fundus photographs (especially if abnormalities are seen), OCT, and color vision
(preferably including the blue-yellow axis) can also be used to follow for signs of retinopathy in patients
taking these drugs.
Patients with >5 years’ drug use with high fat level body habitus, renal or liver disease, and age >60 years
old, especially if frail or extremely thin, are at higher risk of developing toxicity and should be checked
more frequently.

questions.question: Describe the forms of this disease.
questions.answer:
Albinism is a congenital disorder of melanogenesis in which the synthesis of melanin is reduced or
absent. Mutations in at least 13 genes can give rise to various forms of albinism depending on which
structures are involved.
Ocular albinism: this is a disorder, limited to the eye, with a decreased number of melanosomes
(although each melanosome is fully pigmented).
Oculocutaneous albinism: this is a systemic problem with decreased melanin in all melanosomes. These
patients lack pigmentation of the hair, skin, and eyes. This form can further be categorized into
tyrosinase positive (some pigmentation, which increases with age) and tyrosinase negative (no
pigmentation). Two lethal variants of oculocutaneous albinism are:
Chediak-Higashi: patients have reticuloendothelial incompetence with neutropenia, anemia,
thrombocytopenia, recurrent infections, leukemia, and lymphoma.
Hermansky-Pudlak: patients have clotting disorders and bleeding tendencies secondary to platelet
abnormalities.
All of the above forms of albinism can be further classified clinically based on the degree of ocular
involvement:

questions.question: What are the risk factors for this disease?
questions.answer:
This is a heritable disease. Certain ethnic populations have a higher incidence. In particular, Africans and
African Americans have a higher incidence than Caucasians, but often have incomplete forms of
albinism.
keyPoints: This is a heritable disease. Certain ethnic populations have a higher incidence. In particular, Africans and
African Americans have a higher incidence than Caucasians, but often have incomplete forms of
albinism.
scoringKeywords: This is a heritable disease. Certain ethnic populations have a higher incidence. In particular, Africans and
African Americans have a higher incidence than Caucasians, but often have incomplete forms of
albinism.

questions.question: How would you treat this patient?
questions.answer:
There is no effective treatment. Refraction, tinted glasses, and low vision aids are helpful for older
patients. Medical and hematology consultation is advisable to rule out potentially lethal variants. All
forms of albinism are heritable, necessitating genetic counseling.
keyPoints: There is no effective treatment. Refraction, tinted glasses, and low vision aids are helpful for older
patients. Medical and hematology consultation is advisable to rule out potentially lethal variants. All
forms of albinism are heritable, necessitating genetic counseling.
scoringKeywords: There is no effective treatment. Refraction, tinted glasses, and low vision aids are helpful for older
patients. Medical and hematology consultation is advisable to rule out potentially lethal variants. All
forms of albinism are heritable, necessitating genetic counseling.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
The prognosis is variable depending on the form of albinism. This disease is not degenerative, however,
and visual acuity can even improve over the first two decades of life.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Optic nerve drusen slowly enlarge over time which can lead to compression of nerve fibers and visual field defects. The blood vessels
can also be compressed, leading to vascular occlusion and hemorrhage(very rare). Follow with annual HVF, photos, IOP check and
monitor for glaucoma

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Patients typically have normal visual acuity but can (rarely)
develop vision loss from an associated non-arteritic ischemic
optic neuropathy (NAION) or vein occlusion.

Patients can develop visual field defects, which are typically an
arcuate defect or a peripheral depression.

An enlarged blind spot is more typical of true papilledema
secondary to increased intracranial pressure.

Obtain baseline visual fields and disc photos for future exams.
Would follow annually.

cases.title: 10 - the following fundus finding
cases.presentation: 10-year-old with the following fundus finding. Image Description:

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Fusiform enlargement of the optic nerve

1V Assessment:

Optic nerve glioma

o Rare, but the most common primary optic nerve tumor in

children. Can involve the optic nerve, the chiasm, or both. Most
are diagnosed within the first decades of life and associated with
decreased vision, proptosis, afferent pupillary defect (APD), disc
edema, and strabismus. Most common findings are proptosis,
decreased vision, and an APD. Glioma has an association with
NF-1, therefore should ask and look for neurofibromas, café-au-
lait spots, axillary freckling, and Lisch nodules. Some believe
that glioma in NF-1 has a more benign prognosis in comparison
to unassociated isolated gliomas. Adult gliomas are considered
highly malignant with a guarded prognosis.

Plan:
©. There is no universally accepted management protocol. Biopsy

is typically not necessary, as attempts to biopsy would likely
compromise vision.

0 The typical course is slow gradual progression of proptosis and

decreasing vision. Tumors confined to the orbit are observed so

long as proptosis is not disfiguring or complicating ocular health.

If tumor is extending intracranial and encroaching the chiasm
surgical excision can be considered, although chemotherapy and
radiotherapy are plausible; the results of such therapeutic
modalities are inconclusive.

deviation measurements are stable over 6 months. In the interim Mi

amblyopia). Can offer Fresnel prism but would mention that this
option would not alleviate any torsional component of diplopia. i

temple pain, jaw claudication, and vision changes. Would ask about
recent illness, vaccinations, and ear infections. Would perform a
complete neurological and eye exam (cranial nerves, lid function, lid
position, fatigue on up gaze, corneal sensation, Hertel, pupils (APD),
dilated exam, and look in middle ear). Would check blood pressure
and obtain labs and studies guided by exam and history. This patient is
60 years old and has new onset of diplopia that is likely due to acquired
CN 6 palsy from his underlying diabetes and cardiovascular disease
(htn). If patient had history of cancer, optic disc edema, bilateral CN 6
palsy, and no history of cardiovascular disease or diabetes, would order
imaging of the brain/orbits and consider a lumbar puncture. If suspect
GCA, would get ESR, CRP, start steroids and schedule a temporal
artery biopsy. Would consider ordering Lyme titers, VDRL, RPR,
MHA-TP, and a TB test.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?

Left facial nerve palsy involving upper and lower face.

Bell’s Palsy (diagnosis of exclusion)

Trauma (temporal bone fracture)

Infection (Lyme, Otitis, Ramsay Hunt, Guillain-Barre, syphilis)
Infiltrative (sarcoid, metastasis, collagen vascular disease)
Cancer: (nasopharyngeal, parotid, carcinomatous meningitis)
Vasculopathic: (GCA, DM)

iL Narrow Down (History, Exam Findings, Workup)


Determine if upper or lower motor neuron. If forehead is spared then
upper motor neuron; consider intracranial processes (stroke or mass).
Ask about history: onset, duration, recurrence, facial pain, trauma,
stroke, infections, diplopia, hearing loss, tinnitus, dizziness, vertigo,
sarcoid, cancer. Would examine old photos. Complete neuro exam to
sort out central (lower face) from peripheral (upper and lower). Assess
taste and cranial nerves 5,6,7,8. Evaluate motor weakness, ataxia, and
balance. Eye exam for nystagmus, motility, vision, pupils (APD),
visual fields, lid closure, orbicularis strength, corneal health, and
fundus for disc edema. Evaluate ears, palpate parotid, lymph nodes,
skin for vesicles, and check hearing. Trauma: get CT. If cancer
history with associated neurologic signs, duration >3mo: get MRI and
lumbar puncture. If suspicion for sarcoid order: ACE, lysozyme, chest
CT, Lyme, EBV, HIV, RPR, and VDRL. Ifsuspicion for collagen
vascular disease: get ESR, ANCA, ANA. If stroke suspected: get an
MRI, ECHO, and neurology consult.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
o Ask about the onset of ptosis and anisocoria, associated
sign/symptoms, trauma, chiropractic therapy, eye meds, and STD’s.
Would evaluate older photos. Would perform a neurologic exam (i.e.
motor, reflexes, and cranial nerves). Would look for iris heterochromia
and iritis. Would check pupils to determine if anisocoria worse in light
or dark. If worse in dark, then smaller pupil the issue, if worse in light
then larger pupil the issue. Same anisocoria difference in light/dark,
then likely physiologic. Would check for RAPD and evaluate eye
motility. Would check for dilation lag (Horner’s) and vermiform
movements (Adie’s). If patient has dilation lag, anisocoria worse in
dark, and ptosis on same side, then perform 10% cocaine test: no
dilation with cocaine then Horner’s. To help localize a preganglionic
(1°/2"! order) versus postganglionic (3" order) Horner’s, perform a
paradrine eye drop test. If the pupil dilates then preganglionic; no pupil
dilation then postganglionic.
= | o DDX for 1%, 2", and 3" order: (1) Ist order: stroke, MS, brain tumors,
.| vertebrobasilar insufficiency; (2) 2nd order: tumor of lung (apical,
Pancoast), metastasis, neuroblastoma, lymphoma; (3) 3" order:
migraine, carotid dissection, Raeder’s syndrome, herpes zoster, Tolosa-
Hunt, trauma, congenital forceps injury.
© If anisocoria is worse in the light, check pupil for tears, vermiform
| movements, and accommodation: Adie’s (constricts with dilute
pilocarpine .1%). No response with .1% then test with 1%: pupil
constricts work up CN 3palsy, no constriction then likely
pharmacologic.

o Discuss the natural history, which is for continued symptoms and
visual field loss in the absence of treatment. However, with weight loss

can resolve and field loss can be prevented. I would discuss symptoms
with acetazolamide (polyuria, metallic taste with soda, and
parasthesias). Also emphasize the need for compliance with
medication and follow-ups (monitoring of visual fields, optics discs and
symptoms). Would follow-up in 2 weeks and extend follow-ups
monthly as appropriate.
