questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
ie)


Will discuss natural history, which is generally poor. Prognosis of
regaining vision in the affected eye is low. Would stress the
importance of treatment, since without it, the risk of contralateral vision
loss, within days, is high. GCA affects medium to large arteries,
therefore without treatment involvement of the great vessels of the
heart is possible, which can be fatal. Systemic steroids are effective at
preventing progression to the other eye. Treatment typically is
continued for many months; steroid dosing/taper is guided by
symptoms and the ESR if it was elevated initially.

This young woman with sudden onset painful pupil-involving third
nerve palsy is suspicious for an expanding PCOM aneurysm

parasympathetic fibers.

The involvement of the pupil makes a microvascular etiology less
likely, and the sudden onset makes thyroid eye disease, myasthenia,
and an expanding tumor less likely. The involvement of the pupil also
argues against myasthenia.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o My plan would depend on whether signs of optic nerve

compression were present. In the absence of optic nerve
compression, I would refer the patient to an endocrinologist for
management of her underlying thyroid disease, and would treat
her eyes with ointment lubrication for any keratopathy, and with
cold compresses and head elevation at night for conjunctival and
lid congestion. |I would treat symptomatic diplopia with prisms
until there is stability of any tropia, and would see her in a
couple months to make sure no nerve compression was
developing. If there was evidence of optic nerve compression as
evidenced by decreased acuity, dyschromatopsia, nerve swelling
or pallor, an APD, or visual field loss, I would start systemic
steroids immediately and proceed to orbital wall decompression.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
I would educate the patient about the natural history of the
disease, which is for gradual progression for!-2 years before
generally remaining stable. I would explain that she needs to be
evaluated and treated by an endocrinologist, and that she must
immediately stop smoking if she smokes, as this is known to
worsen the orbitopathy. If there is compression and surgery is
indicated, I would educate her that any necessary strabismus
surgery should be delayed until after the decompression, and any
lid surgery should be left for last.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Observation is reasonable option if vision good, ocular health is
stable and tumor not extending into the optic canal or
intracranially. If vision worsening can consider stereotactic
fractionated radiotherapy. This therapeutic modality can improve
vision, slow tumor progression, and improve disc edema. If
progressing intracranially will plan referral to neurosurgery.

Her right optic nerve head appears normal and her left optic nerve (ON) head is depicted below:

questions.question: How would you manage this patient?
questions.answer:
It is important to determine whether this patient’s VF defect is due to glaucoma or the ON head drusen.
Therefore, it would be helpful to obtain her old records to know what old field tests showed, what her
maximum eye pressure was, and whether her optic nerve appearance has changed. If she never had
elevated IOP or progression of the scotoma or optic cupping, then the glaucoma diagnosis may be
suspect and it is reasonable to stop the glaucoma drop and follow her IOP. A diurnal curve may also
be useful for determining her maximum IOP. If previous records are unavailable, then new baselines
should be established with visual fields, gonioscopy, corneal pachymetry, and optic nerve head images.

questions.question: What are the potential complications of optic nerve drusen?
questions.answer:
Optic nerve drusen can cause VF defects (typically enlarged blind spot, arcuate scotoma, or sectoral
scotoma that is stable / nonprogressive), anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, choroidal neovascularization,
subretinal or vitreous hemorrhage, and vascular occlusion.
keyPoints: Optic nerve drusen can cause VF defects (typically enlarged blind spot, arcuate scotoma, or sectoral
scotoma that is stable / nonprogressive), anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, choroidal neovascularization,
subretinal or vitreous hemorrhage, and vascular occlusion.

scotoma that is stable / nonprogressive), anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, choroidal neovascularization,

cases.title: 70M - intermittent double vision for the past week and is...
cases.presentation: A 70-year-old man reports intermittent double vision for the past week and is panicked because of blurry vision in the right eye for 2 days. He says his vision has been excellent since his cataract surgery several years earlier. His takes medication for hypertension and high cholesterol. Ocular exam shows visual acuity of 20/200 OD and 20/25 OS. There is a positive relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) in the right eye, extraocular motility is full, and anterior segment exam reveals well-positioned posterior chamber IOLs. The retina is within normal limits and the right optic nerve is pictured above.

questions.question: What are you concerned about and what further history would you obtain?
questions.answer:
The history and findings are suggestive of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION). It is important to
distinguish between the arteritic (GCA) and nonarteritic (NAION) forms because the arteritic form must
be treated to prevent visual loss in the fellow eye. NAION is most often spontaneous but is associated
with hypertension, diabetes, ischemic heart disease, hypercholesterolemia, and smoking. This patient
has increased blood pressure and cholesterol, but it cannot be assumed that his optic neuropathy is
nonarteritic. The patient must be asked about the characteristic symptoms of GCA: headache, scalp
tenderness, jaw claudication (pain with chewing), weight loss, fever, and anorexia, as well as neck pain,
eye pain, diplopia, joint pain (symptoms of polymyalgia rheumatica), and history of anemia.
keyPoints: The history and findings are suggestive of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION). It is important to
distinguish between the arteritic (GCA) and nonarteritic (NAION) forms because the arteritic form must
be treated to prevent visual loss in the fellow eye. NAION is most often spontaneous but is associated
with hypertension, diabetes, ischemic heart disease, hypercholesterolemia, and smoking. This patient
has increased blood pressure and cholesterol, but it cannot be assumed that his optic neuropathy is
nonarteritic. The patient must be asked about the characteristic symptoms of GCA: headache, scalp
tenderness, jaw claudication (pain with chewing), weight loss, fever, and anorexia, as well as neck pain,
eye pain, diplopia, joint pain (symptoms of polymyalgia rheumatica), and history of anemia.

be treated to prevent visual loss in the fellow eye. NAION is most often spontaneous but is associated

questions.question: How would you work up this patient?
questions.answer:
A stat erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR; sed rate) is required to rule out arteritic AION. An elevated
ESR is considered to be >(age/2) for males and >([age+10]/2) for females. Other labs that should be
ordered are C-reactive protein (elevated CRP ([>2.45 mg/dL]) and complete blood count (CBC) (low
hematocrit, high platelets). Fluorescein angiography shows choroidal nonperfusion in the arteritic form.
Some physicians routinely obtain a temporal artery biopsy (sometimes bilateral) on all patients, which
should be performed within 2 weeks of initiating steroid treatment. Because of skip lesions, the biopsy
specimen should be at least 3 cm in length.

questions.question:
What treatment would you prescribe if this patient has giant cell arteritis (GCA), and what is the
prognosis?
questions.answer:
Emergent treatment with high-dose steroids (prednisone 60–120 mg orally; consider IV initially [1 g qd
for 3 days]), which should not be delayed while waiting for the temporal artery biopsy. Unfortunately,
treatment does not improve the outcome in the affected eye but is necessary to prevent visual loss in
the fellow eye (65% risk of involvement without treatment; however, some patients lose vision despite
treatment). Patients should be followed by an internist and / or rheumatologist to monitor the response
to therapy and to slowly taper the steroids. GCA is also associated with other ocular and systemic
complications: retinal artery occlusion, ophthalmic artery occlusion, anterior segment ischemia, cranial
nerve palsy (especially CN 6), and stroke.

questions.question: What is Foster-Kennedy syndrome and pseudo-Foster-Kennedy syndrome?
questions.answer:
Foster-Kennedy syndrome is a frontal lobe mass (usually meningioma) that causes anosmia, ipsilateral
optic atrophy from tumor compression, and contralateral optic nerve edema from elevated intracranial
pressure. The same optic nerve findings (disc edema in one eye and disc pallor in the other eye) can
occur in bilateral AION, which is called pseudo-Foster-Kennedy syndrome.
keyPoints: Foster-Kennedy syndrome is a frontal lobe mass (usually meningioma) that causes anosmia, ipsilateral
ptic atrophy from tumor compression, and contralateral optic nerve edema from elevated intracranial
pressure. The same optic nerve findings (disc edema in one eye and disc pallor in the other eye) can
ccur in bilateral AION, which is called pseudo-Foster-Kennedy syndrome.

questions.question: What are the diagnosis and possible etiologies?
questions.answer:
This patient has a pupil-involving CN 3 palsy OD. Despite a history of hypertension, she is young and
has pupil involvement, and pain. It is therefore most important to rule out a posterior communicating
artery aneurysm, which is a neurosurgical emergency. It is unlikely to be due to other etiologies such as
microvascular (diabetes, hypertension), trauma (no history of this), tumor, or infection.

questions.question:
How would your treatment differ if this patient were older than age 50 with an isolated pupil-sparing
third nerve palsy?
questions.answer:
Older patients with isolated pupil-sparing 3rd nerve palsies should be observed carefully for pupil
involvement during the first week, but generally such cases are due to microvascular disease (80% are
pupil sparing) and resolve spontaneously in 3 months. Workup is performed if the pupil becomes
involved, there is a history of cancer, there are other neurologic abnormalities, or the palsy does not
resolve after 3 months. The diplopia can be treated by occluding the paretic eye.

questions.question: What are the possible etiologies?
questions.answer:
Most commonly it is vasculopathic in adults, but other etiologies include trauma, temporal arteritis,
infection, multiple sclerosis, cerebrovascular accidents, increased intracranial pressure, and rarely tumors.
Other conditions in the differential diagnosis of CN 6 palsy are thyroid-related ophthalmopathy, orbital
inflammatory pseudotumor (idiopathic orbital inflammation), myasthenia gravis, convergence spasm,
strabismus, medial orbital wall fracture, and orbital myositis.

questions.question: What workup and treatment are required?
questions.answer:
Treatment of CN 6 palsy depends on the underlying etiology. This is presumably an ischemic isolated
mononeuropathy due to diabetes, which is self-limited and should resolve within 3 months so a workup
is not necessary. The patient should be reassured and told that he can temporarily cover his left glasses
lens or patch the left eye to prevent diplopia or use prism glasses. He should be monitored for other
neurologic symptoms. If any develop or his condition does not resolve in 3 months, then neuroimaging
is indicated. Additional workup to consider is checking BP, lab tests (CBC, ESR [erythrocyte
sedimentation rate], VDRL [(Venereal Disease Research Laboratory] or RPR [rapid plasma reagin],
FTA-ABS [fluorescent treponemal antibody absorption] or MHA-TP [microhemagglutination assay for
Treponema pallidum], ANA [antinuclear antibody]), lumbar puncture, and Tensilon test.

cases.title: 64M - left-sided facial pain, swollen eyelid, and double vision
cases.presentation: A 64-year-old man presents to the emergency room (ER) with left-sided facial pain, swollen eyelid, and double vision. He is a low myope with no significant past ocular history. His visual acuity is 20/40 OD and 20/60 OS without correction and extraocular motility in the left eye is restricted in all positions of gaze.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
This patient appears to have multiple CN palsies. These can be caused by tumor, infection, inflammation,
meningitis, and vascular lesions in the brain stem, subarachnoid space, cavernous sinus, or orbital apex.
Other disorders that mimic multiple CN palsies include chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia
(CPEO), myasthenia gravis, multiple sclerosis, Guillain-Barré syndrome, and progressive supranuclear
palsy.

questions.question: What other exam findings might be present?
questions.answer:
Depending on the location of the pathology, other cranial nerves and sympathetic nerves may be
affected, so a CN exam must be performed with particular attention to best-corrected visual acuity,
anisocoria, pupillary response, extraocular motility, lid position, facial sensation, and facial muscle
strength. Possible findings include decreased visual acuity and color vision (orbital apex syndrome),
ptosis, strabismus, negative forced ductions, decreased facial sensation in CN V -V distribution, relative
1 2
afferent pupillary defect, miosis (Horner’s syndrome), and trigeminal (facial) pain. There may also be
proptosis, conjunctival injection, chemosis, increased intraocular pressure, bruit, and retinopathy in
cases of high-flow arteriovenous fistulas. Fever, lid edema, and signs of facial infection occur in cases of
cavernous sinus thrombosis.

questions.answer:
Head, orbital, and sinus imaging (CT/MRI-MRA) is required to localize the pathology and direct
treatment. Lab tests to order include fasting blood glucose, CBC with differential, ESR, VDRL or RPR,
FTA-ABS or MHA-TP, ANA, and blood cultures if infection is suspected. Other possible tests include
lumbar puncture and Tensilon test.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer: Preseptal or orbital cellulitis.
keyPoints: Preseptal or orbital cellulitis.
scoringKeywords: Preseptal or orbital cellulitis.

questions.question: What would you look for on exam to distinguish between these two diagnoses?
questions.answer:
Preseptal cellulitis is an infection anterior to the orbital septum and does not affect the globe, whereas
orbital cellulitis is an infection extending posterior to the septum and involves the globe (therefore has
eye findings – specifically, decreased vision, positive RAPD, restricted ocular motility, proptosis, pain on
eye movement, periorbital swelling, chemosis, and optic disc swelling).
keyPoints: Preseptal cellulitis is an infection anterior to the orbital septum and does not affect the globe, whereas
rbital cellulitis is an infection extending posterior to the septum and involves the globe (therefore has
eye findings – specifically, decreased vision, positive RAPD, restricted ocular motility, proptosis, pain on
eye movement, periorbital swelling, chemosis, and optic disc swelling).
scoringKeywords: Preseptal cellulitis is an infection anterior to the orbital septum and does not affect the globe, whereas
rbital cellulitis is an infection extending posterior to the septum and involves the globe (therefore has
eye findings – specifically, decreased vision, positive RAPD, restricted ocular motility, proptosis, pain on
eye movement, periorbital swelling, chemosis, and optic disc swelling).

questions.question: What organisms cause this infection and what are you most concerned about?
questions.answer:
Orbital cellulitis is most commonly due to Gram-positive bacteria (Streptococcus and Staphylococcus
species) but can also be caused by fungi (Phycomycetes). Mucormycosis, an aggressive infection by
Mucor fungi that causes necrosis, vascular thrombosis, and orbital invasion, must be suspected in this
case of an immunocompromised patient.

species) but can also be caused by fungi (Phycomycetes). Mucormycosis, an aggressive infection by
Mucor fungi that causes necrosis, vascular thrombosis, and orbital invasion, must be suspected in this
case of an immunocompromised patient.

species) but can also be caused by fungi (Phycomycetes). Mucormycosis, an aggressive infection by
Mucor fungi that causes necrosis, vascular thrombosis, and orbital invasion, must be suspected in this

questions.question: What is the appropriate treatment?
questions.answer:
This is Mucor infection (the histology shows broad nonseptate fungal hyphae and angiothrombosis), a
life-threatening condition requiring emergent IV antifungals and surgical debridement. A CT scan should
be obtained to evaluate the extent of involvement. The diabetes must be treated and controlled.

life-threatening condition requiring emergent IV antifungals and surgical debridement. A CT scan should

questions.question: What are the possible complications?
questions.answer:
Mucor can cause retinal vascular occlusions and orbital apex syndrome. It may extend intracranially to
cause cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, brain abscess, and death.
keyPoints: Mucor can cause retinal vascular occlusions and orbital apex syndrome. It may extend intracranially to
cause cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, brain abscess, and death.

cause cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, brain abscess, and death.

cases.title: 28F - her eyes have been red for the past year and seem bigger
cases.presentation: A 28-year-old woman reports her eyes have been red for the past year and seem bigger.

questions.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.answer: Thyroid-related ophthalmopathy (TRO), most commonly Graves’.
keyPoints: Thyroid-related ophthalmopathy (TRO), most commonly Graves’.
scoringKeywords: Thyroid-related ophthalmopathy (TRO), most commonly Graves’.

questions.question: What other ocular findings are associated with this condition?
questions.answer:
In addition to proptosis, other findings include: lid signs (lid retraction, lid lag on downgaze,
lagophthalmos), restricted extraocular motility (strabismus), exposure (conjunctival hyperemia,
keratopathy), and optic nerve compression (optic neuropathy). (The Werner Classification of eye findings
in TRO can be remembered with the mnemonic: NO SPECS [No signs or symptoms, Only signs, Soft
tissue involvement, Proptosis, Extraocular muscle involvement, Corneal involvement, Sight loss (optic
nerve compression)].)

lagophthalmos), restricted extraocular motility (strabismus), exposure (conjunctival hyperemia,
keratopathy), and optic nerve compression (optic neuropathy). (The Werner Classification of eye findings
in TRO can be remembered with the mnemonic: NO SPECS [No signs or symptoms, Only signs, Soft
tissue involvement, Proptosis, Extraocular muscle involvement, Corneal involvement, Sight loss (optic

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Dry eye is treated with lubrication, patching / taping / goggles at bedtime, punctal occlusion, and
sometimes with tarsorrhaphy. Extreme proptosis causing severe keratopathy or optic nerve compression
is treated with orbital decompression. Diplopia can be treated with prism glasses and eventually surgery
(rectus recessions when the strabismus is stable for at least 6 months). Steroids and sometimes radiation
are used to reduce muscle enlargement. Surgery is performed in a stepwise fashion with orbital
decompression first, then strabismus surgery, and finally lid surgery (eyelid recession). The underlying
thyroid disease must also be treated.

sometimes with tarsorrhaphy. Extreme proptosis causing severe keratopathy or optic nerve compression
is treated with orbital decompression. Diplopia can be treated with prism glasses and eventually surgery
(rectus recessions when the strabismus is stable for at least 6 months). Steroids and sometimes radiation

questions.question: What are other causes of proptosis in an adult?
questions.answer:
The differential diagnosis of proptosis in an adult includes: idiopathic orbital inflammation (IOI), orbital
and lacrimal gland tumors, orbital vasculitis, trauma, cellulitis, arteriovenous fistula, and cavernous sinus
thrombosis.
keyPoints: The differential diagnosis of proptosis in an adult includes: idiopathic orbital inflammation (IOI), orbital
and lacrimal gland tumors, orbital vasculitis, trauma, cellulitis, arteriovenous fistula, and cavernous sinus
thrombosis.
scoringKeywords: The differential diagnosis of proptosis in an adult includes: idiopathic orbital inflammation (IOI), orbital
and lacrimal gland tumors, orbital vasculitis, trauma, cellulitis, arteriovenous fistula, and cavernous sinus
thrombosis.

questions.question: What tests are helpful in determining the correct diagnosis?
questions.answer:
A complete eye exam must be performed with attention to visual acuity, color vision, pupils, motility,
forced ductions, eyelid position and movement, exophthalmometry, cornea, tonometry (increased IOP
in upgaze), and ophthalmoscopy. Check visual fields as a baseline study in early cases and to rule out
optic neuropathy in advanced cases. Thyroid function tests (thyroid stimulating hormone [TSH],
thyroxine [total and free T ], and triiodothyronine [T ]) and thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin (TSI)
4 3
should be ordered. Orbital CT scan may show extraocular muscle enlargement with sparing of the
tendons (the inferior rectus is most commonly involved, followed by medial, superior, and lateral), in
contrast to IOI, in which the tendons are also enlarged.

questions.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.answer: Leber’s hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON).
keyPoints: Leber’s hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON).
scoringKeywords: Leber’s hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON).

questions.question: What are the characteristic findings of this disease?
questions.answer:
The typical appearance is disc hyperemia, peripapillary telangiectatic vessels, tortuous vessels,
peripapillary nerve fiber layer edema, and eventually optic disc pallor.
keyPoints: The typical appearance is disc hyperemia, peripapillary telangiectatic vessels, tortuous vessels,
peripapillary nerve fiber layer edema, and eventually optic disc pallor.
scoringKeywords: The typical appearance is disc hyperemia, peripapillary telangiectatic vessels, tortuous vessels,
peripapillary nerve fiber layer edema, and eventually optic disc pallor.

questions.question: What do you tell him about the disease?
questions.answer:
LHON is a hereditary optic neuropathy with maternal inheritance, so it is transmitted to all sons (only
50% are affected). Visual loss occurs in the 2nd–3rd decade of life with bilateral sequential loss of vision
to worse than 20/200. Unfortunately there is no treatment.

questions.question: What drugs can cause toxic optic neuropathy?
questions.answer:
Ethambutol is the most common; others include isoniazid, chloramphenicol, streptomycin, methanol,
digitalis, chloroquine, and quinine.
keyPoints: Ethambutol is the most common; others include isoniazid, chloramphenicol, streptomycin, methanol,
digitalis, chloroquine, and quinine.

questions.question: What do you notice about the optic nerve?
questions.answer: Optic disc pallor horizontally.

questions.question: What additional testing would you perform in the office?
questions.answer: VF (tangent screen, Humphrey, or Goldmann) and color vision testing.

questions.question: Does the VF test localize the pathology and, if so, where is the lesion?
questions.answer:
The VF defect is a junctional scotoma due to a lesion of the optic nerve near the chiasm, which involves
the knee of von Willebrand (inferonasal retinal fibers that cross in the chiasm and then travel anteriorly
approximately 4 mm into the opposite [contralateral] optic nerve before running posteriorly to the
brain) causing central visual loss in the ipsilateral eye and a superotemporal field defect in the
contralateral eye.

neuritis.

questions.answer:
Check color vision, visual fields, and perform a head / orbital MRI to look for periventricular white matter
demyelinating lesions or plaques (the best predictor of future development of multiple sclerosis). A
neurologic exam should also be performed.

questions.question: What are the recommendations for treatment?
questions.answer:
According to the Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial (ONTT), if the MRI is positive, then consider systemic
steroids (methylprednisolone 250 mg IV q6h for 3 days, followed by prednisone 1 mg/kg/day for 11 days
and rapid taper 20 mg/day on day 12 and 10 mg/day on days 13-15). Visual recovery occurred 2 weeks
faster with this regimen than with other treatments; however, there was no difference in the final visual
acuity. Furthermore, there was a decreased incidence of MS over the next 2 years but there was no
difference after 3 years. Oral steroids should not be used alone, because this led to an increased risk of
recurrent optic neuritis.
Additional treatment recommendations come from the CHAMPS study group, which showed that
patients who received weekly intramuscular interferon β-1a (Avonex) after steroid therapy for the first
episode of optic neuritis associated with at least two lesions on MRI greater than 3 mm had a reduction
in onset of clinical MS over 3 years and improvement or less worsening of MRI lesions.

steroids (methylprednisolone 250 mg IV q6h for 3 days, followed by prednisone 1 mg/kg/day for 11 days
and rapid taper 20 mg/day on day 12 and 10 mg/day on days 13-15). Visual recovery occurred 2 weeks
faster with this regimen than with other treatments; however, there was no difference in the final visual
acuity. Furthermore, there was a decreased incidence of MS over the next 2 years but there was no
difference after 3 years. Oral steroids should not be used alone, because this led to an increased risk of
recurrent optic neuritis.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
Most patients recover vision over the following months, but the final acuity depends upon the severity of
the visual loss. The majority of patients recover 20/20 vision; however, permanent subtle color vision and
contrast sensitivity deficits are common. Uhthoff’s symptom (blurred vision with increased body
temperature or exercise) may occur. Approximately 30% of patients with isolated optic neuritis will
experience another attack in either eye, and up to 50% of patients will develop MS over 5–10 years.

contrast sensitivity deficits are common. Uhthoff’s symptom (blurred vision with increased body
temperature or exercise) may occur. Approximately 30% of patients with isolated optic neuritis will
experience another attack in either eye, and up to 50% of patients will develop MS over 5–10 years.

questions.question: What do you suspect may be the problem and what other questions might be helpful?
questions.answer:
This case is suspicious for functional visual loss (i.e. malingering, hysteria). Malingering is the fabrication
of a disorder for secondary gain (usually financial), whereas hysteria is a subconscious expression of
symptoms. In order to distinguish between them and rule out any organic disease, it would be helpful to
ask about any injuries, medical problems, medications, and social history with particular attention to
employment status, stresses, and alcohol / drug use.
keyPoints: This case is suspicious for functional visual loss (i.e. malingering, hysteria). Malingering is the fabrication
f a disorder for secondary gain (usually financial), whereas hysteria is a subconscious expression of
symptoms. In order to distinguish between them and rule out any organic disease, it would be helpful to
ask about any injuries, medical problems, medications, and social history with particular attention to
employment status, stresses, and alcohol / drug use.

questions.question: What tests could you perform to confirm your suspicion?
questions.answer:
Carefully performing a variety of vision tests to trick the patient can usually uncover functional visual
loss because of inconsistent results. These include distance and near, monocular and binocular, varying
the test distance, fogging, red–green glasses with duochrome test or Worth 4 dot test, prism dissociation,
stereopsis, startle reflex, proprioception, name signing, mirror tracking, and optokinetic nystagmus
response. Visual fields may show unusual patterns such as tunnel vision, spiraling fields, and crossing
isopters. Finally, in difficult cases electrophysiologic testing, optical coherence tomography (OCT),
fluorescein angiography, or neuroimaging may be necessary.

questions.question: What strategies can you use to help him ‘recover’ his vision?
questions.answer:
It is helpful to acknowledge the decreased vision and then offer reassurance and a potential way out such
as administering a topical medication in the office and then retesting, or having the patient return in
several weeks for a repeat examination. Psychiatry consultation is usually not required.

questions.question: What are the etiologies?
questions.answer:
The etiology depends upon which neuron is affected: central causes include cerebrovascular accident,
neck trauma, tumor, cervical disc disease, and demyelinating disease. These rarely result in an isolated
Horner’s syndrome. Preganglionic causes include tumors (mediastinal, apical, thyroid), trauma,
pneumothorax, cervical infections, brachial plexus syndromes, carotid artery dissection, aneurysm,
trauma. Postganglionic causes include neck lesion, head trauma, migraine, cavernous sinus lesion,
vascular lesions (carotid dissection, carotid-cavernous fistula, internal carotid artery aneurysm), and
infections.
keyPoints: The etiology depends upon which neuron is affected: central causes include cerebrovascular accident,
neck trauma, tumor, cervical disc disease, and demyelinating disease. These rarely result in an isolated
Horner’s syndrome. Preganglionic causes include tumors (mediastinal, apical, thyroid), trauma,
pneumothorax, cervical infections, brachial plexus syndromes, carotid artery dissection, aneurysm,
trauma. Postganglionic causes include neck lesion, head trauma, migraine, cavernous sinus lesion,

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis of anisocoria?
questions.answer:
This depends on whether the abnormal pupil is smaller (i.e. greater anisocoria in a dim / dark room) or
larger (i.e. greater anisocoria in a bright / light room). For a miotic pupil, the differential diagnosis
includes Horner’s syndrome, Argyll Robertson pupil, iritis, and pharmacologic (pilocarpine, brimonidine,
narcotics, insecticides). For a mydriatic pupil, the differential diagnosis includes Adie’s tonic pupil, CN 3
palsy, Hutchinson’s pupil, pharmacologic (mydriatic, cycloplegic, cocaine, hallucinogen), iris damage
(trauma, ischemia, or surgery (Urrets-Zavalia syndrome). If the anisocoria is equal in light and dark, then
the diagnosis is physiologic anisocoria (difference in pupil size ≤1 mm).

larger (i.e. greater anisocoria in a bright / light room). For a miotic pupil, the differential diagnosis

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis of transient diplopia?
questions.answer:
The most common causes of transient diplopia are decompensated phoria, convergence or divergence
insufficiency, MG, and spasm of accommodation. Other etiologies include GCA (extraocular muscle
[EOM] ischemia), vertibrobasilar insufficiency, superior oblique myokymia, cyclic esotropia, and skew
deviation.

deviation.

questions.question:
What are the characteristic eye findings of this disease and what tests would be useful for confirming the
diagnosis?
questions.answer:
The characteristic ocular findings of MG are asymmetric ptosis and variable strabismus. Gaze-evoked
nystagmus also occurs. There is no pupil or ciliary muscle involvement. The hallmark of the disease is
variability and fatigability, so there is worsening of the ptosis with extended upgaze and improvement
with rest or cold. This can be evaluated with the rest test (improvement in ptosis after closing the eyes
for 30 minutes) or ice test (improvement in ptosis after application of an ice pack for 2 minutes). Forced
ductions are negative. A Tensilon test can be administered to observe for improvement in ocular signs;
however, a negative test does not rule out MG. The diagnostic lab test is acetylcholine (ACh) receptor
antibodies. If these tests are negative, then definitive diagnosis is by single fiber electromyography of
peripheral or orbicularis muscles.

questions.question: What other workup and treatment are necessary for this disease?
questions.answer:
It is important to rule out thyroid disease, thymoma, and other autoimmune disorders, so lab tests
(thyroid function tests, ANA, and rheumatoid factor (RF)) and a chest CT or MRI scan should be
obtained. The patient should be referred to a neurologist or internist for systemic treatment. Strabismus
can be managed with prism spectacles and potentially surgery when stable for at least 6 months.

(thyroid function tests, ANA, and rheumatoid factor (RF)) and a chest CT or MRI scan should be
btained. The patient should be referred to a neurologist or internist for systemic treatment. Strabismus
can be managed with prism spectacles and potentially surgery when stable for at least 6 months.

cases.title: 32F - diplopia for several weeks. She has also had headaches...
cases.presentation: A 32-year-old woman complains of diplopia for several weeks. She has also had headaches for months. On exam, you find an obese, slightly anxious woman with vision of 20/25 in both eyes, normal pupillary response, and esotropia in primary gaze. The optic nerve appearance is shown.

questions.question: What is the abnormality?
questions.answer: Signs and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (i.e. headache, vomiting, papilledema).
keyPoints: Signs and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (i.e. headache, vomiting, papilledema).
scoringKeywords: Signs and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (i.e. headache, vomiting, papilledema).

keyPoints: High CSF pressure (>250 mmH O) with normal composition.

questions.answer: Normal neuroimaging studies.

questions.question:
What is the workup for papilledema, and how do you determine whether she has idiopathic intracranial
hypertension (IIH)?
questions.answer: Normal neurologic examination except for possible CN 6 palsies.
keyPoints: Normal neurologic examination except for possible CN 6 palsies.
scoringKeywords: Normal neurologic examination except for possible CN 6 palsies.

questions.question: What are the associations?
questions.answer:
IIH is associated with medications (steroids [use or withdrawal], oral contraceptive pills [due to
hypercoagulability and dural sinus thrombosis], vitamin A, tetracycline, nalidixic acid, lithium, and
isotretinoin), chronic obstructive lung disease, dural sinus thrombosis, radical neck surgery, recent weight
gain, and pregnancy.
keyPoints: IIH is associated with medications (steroids [use or withdrawal], oral contraceptive pills [due to
hypercoagulability and dural sinus thrombosis], vitamin A, tetracycline, nalidixic acid, lithium, and
isotretinoin), chronic obstructive lung disease, dural sinus thrombosis, radical neck surgery, recent weight
gain, and pregnancy.
scoringKeywords: IIH is associated with medications (steroids [use or withdrawal], oral contraceptive pills [due to
hypercoagulability and dural sinus thrombosis], vitamin A, tetracycline, nalidixic acid, lithium, and
isotretinoin), chronic obstructive lung disease, dural sinus thrombosis, radical neck surgery, recent weight
gain, and pregnancy.

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Any medication associated with IIH should be discontinued, and a weight loss regimen should be
instituted for obesity. Patients with vision loss, VF defects, or intractable headaches are treated with
systemic medications (acetazolamide [Diamox] or furosemide [Lasix]. Patients with progressive visual
loss may require surgery (ON sheath fenestration, lumbar–peritoneal shunt).
keyPoints: Any medication associated with IIH should be discontinued, and a weight loss regimen should be
instituted for obesity. Patients with vision loss, VF defects, or intractable headaches are treated with
systemic medications (acetazolamide [Diamox] or furosemide [Lasix]. Patients with progressive visual
loss may require surgery (ON sheath fenestration, lumbar–peritoneal shunt).
scoringKeywords: Any medication associated with IIH should be discontinued, and a weight loss regimen should be
instituted for obesity. Patients with vision loss, VF defects, or intractable headaches are treated with

cases.title: 49M - pain, swelling, and sensitivity to light. Exam of the...
cases.presentation: A 49-year-old man reports being hit by an elbow in his left eye while playing basketball yesterday night. He complains of pain, swelling, and sensitivity to light. Exam of the left eye reveals a visual acuity of 20/40, limited supraduction, and an external exam as noted in the photo. There is subconjunctival hemorrhage and anterior chamber cells and flare. The posterior segment is normal.

questions.question: What would you expect to see on orbital CT scan?
questions.answer:
The presentation is consistent with an orbital floor fracture with entrapment, which should be evident
on orbital CT scan. This is the most common type of orbital fracture, and usually involves the maxillary
bone and the posterior medial floor, the weakest point of the orbit.
keyPoints: The presentation is consistent with an orbital floor fracture with entrapment, which should be evident
n orbital CT scan. This is the most common type of orbital fracture, and usually involves the maxillary
bone and the posterior medial floor, the weakest point of the orbit.

n orbital CT scan. This is the most common type of orbital fracture, and usually involves the maxillary
bone and the posterior medial floor, the weakest point of the orbit.

questions.question: What other signs may be present?
questions.answer: Other signs of a blowout fracture are globe ptosis, infraorbital nerve hypesthesia, and lid emphysema.
keyPoints: Other signs of a blowout fracture are globe ptosis, infraorbital nerve hypesthesia, and lid emphysema.
scoringKeywords: Other signs of a blowout fracture are globe ptosis, infraorbital nerve hypesthesia, and lid emphysema.

questions.question: How would you treat this patient?
questions.answer:
Initially, the patient should be treated with ice compresses, nasal decongestants, and avoid blowing his
nose. Consideration should be given to prescribing oral antiobiotics and steroids for 10 days. He also has
a traumatic iritis, which should be treated with a topical steroid and cycloplegic. He should be
reevaluated for surgery in 7–10 days to allow for reduction of swelling.

questions.question: What are the indications for surgery?
questions.answer: The indications for repair are diplopia, muscle entrapment, enophthalmos, and facial asymmetry.
keyPoints: The indications for repair are diplopia, muscle entrapment, enophthalmos, and facial asymmetry.
scoringKeywords: The indications for repair are diplopia, muscle entrapment, enophthalmos, and facial asymmetry.

questions.question: If this patient were a child, how would the management differ?
questions.answer:
Pediatric floor fractures are different from those in adults because the bones are pliable and a trapdoor
situation may occur, in which the inferior rectus or surrounding tissue can become entrapped. Nausea
and bradycardia occur owing to the oculocardiac reflex. This is commonly referred to as a ‘white- eyed
blowout fracture’ because the eye is usually quiet; however, it requires emergent surgery to prevent
permanent damage to the entrapped tissue.

situation may occur, in which the inferior rectus or surrounding tissue can become entrapped. Nausea
and bradycardia occur owing to the oculocardiac reflex. This is commonly referred to as a ‘white- eyed
blowout fracture’ because the eye is usually quiet; however, it requires emergent surgery to prevent
permanent damage to the entrapped tissue.

questions.answer: Horizontal gaze palsy with an inability to adduct the left eye.
keyPoints: Horizontal gaze palsy with an inability to adduct the left eye.
scoringKeywords: Horizontal gaze palsy with an inability to adduct the left eye.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia, medial rectus palsy, myasthenia gravis.
keyPoints: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia, medial rectus palsy, myasthenia gravis.
scoringKeywords: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia, medial rectus palsy, myasthenia gravis.

questions.question: What exam findings would be helpful to determine the diagnosis?
questions.answer:
The presence of other neurologic signs, contralateral abducting nystagmus (internuclear ophthalmoplegia
[INO]), absence of convergence (anterior INO), absent doll’s head maneuvers and caloric stimulation
(INO), ptosis (MG), other extraocular motility limitations (MG), variability / fatigability (MG).
keyPoints: The presence of other neurologic signs, contralateral abducting nystagmus (internuclear ophthalmoplegia
[INO]), absence of convergence (anterior INO), absent doll’s head maneuvers and caloric stimulation
(INO), ptosis (MG), other extraocular motility limitations (MG), variability / fatigability (MG).
scoringKeywords: The presence of other neurologic signs, contralateral abducting nystagmus (internuclear ophthalmoplegia
[INO]), absence of convergence (anterior INO), absent doll’s head maneuvers and caloric stimulation
(INO), ptosis (MG), other extraocular motility limitations (MG), variability / fatigability (MG).

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia.
keyPoints: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia.
scoringKeywords: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia.

questions.question: Where is the lesion?
questions.answer: Medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF).
keyPoints: Medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF).
scoringKeywords: Medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF).

questions.question: What are the etiologies?
questions.answer:
The etiology depends on age. In patients younger than age 50 years old it is usually demyelination or
tumor. In patients older than age 50 years old it is usually vascular. Other causes are trauma, infection,
and compression.
keyPoints: The etiology depends on age. In patients younger than age 50 years old it is usually demyelination or
tumor. In patients older than age 50 years old it is usually vascular. Other causes are trauma, infection,
and compression.
scoringKeywords: The etiology depends on age. In patients younger than age 50 years old it is usually demyelination or
tumor. In patients older than age 50 years old it is usually vascular. Other causes are trauma, infection,
and compression.

questions.question: What are the signs of bilateral involvement?
questions.answer: Inability to adduct either eye, impaired convergence, appearance of exotropia in primary gaze.
keyPoints: Inability to adduct either eye, impaired convergence, appearance of exotropia in primary gaze.
scoringKeywords: Inability to adduct either eye, impaired convergence, appearance of exotropia in primary gaze.

questions.question: How does this differ from one-and-a-half syndrome?
questions.answer:
One-and-a-half syndrome is due to a lesion of the paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) or CN
6 nucleus, and the ipsilateral MLF. This causes ipsilateral gaze palsy and INO, so the only eye movement
present is abduction of the contralateral eye with nystagmus. Etiologies include stroke, multiple sclerosis,
basilar artery occlusion, and pontine tumors.
keyPoints: One-and-a-half syndrome is due to a lesion of the paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) or CN
6 nucleus, and the ipsilateral MLF. This causes ipsilateral gaze palsy and INO, so the only eye movement
present is abduction of the contralateral eye with nystagmus. Etiologies include stroke, multiple sclerosis,
basilar artery occlusion, and pontine tumors.

cases.title: 8 - All of the following would be considered in the...
cases.presentation:
All of the following would be considered in the differential diagnosis of vitreous
hemorrhage in an 8-year-old child EXCEPT:

questions.question:
All of the following would be considered in the differential diagnosis of vitreous
hemorrhage in an 8-year-old child EXCEPT:
questions.answer:
melanocytoma
Causes of vitreous hemorrhage in children include conditions such as pars planitis,
juvenile X-linked retinoschisis, and trauma. Melanocytomas are elevated, deeply
pigmented lesions most often found at the optic nerve head. Hislologically, they
appear as a benign proliferation of melanocytic cells. They are not known to cause
vitreous hemorrhage.
keyPoints: melanocytoma

Macular microcysts exhibit classic petalloid leakage on fluorescein angiography
Juvenile X-linked retinoschisis is characterized by cleavage of the retina at the nerve
fiber layer as opposed to senile retinoschisis where cleavage is in the outer plexiform
layer. Because the photoreceptors are unaffected, the a-wave on the ERG is intact,
but both the scotopic and photopic b-waves are reduced in proportion to the
amount of retinal schisis. The EOG and dark adaptation lest are normal or
abnormal depending on the stage of disease. The macula is involved early, showing
microcysts and radiating retinal folds, but fluorescein angiography exhibits no
leakage. Peripheral schisis usually develops later. Vitreous veils and strands form. If a
vessel is lorn with these veils, vitreous hemorrhage results; this often is how children
present. Typically, vision is reduced to the 20/50 to 20/100 level, but expressivity is
variable.

cases.title: 5M - evaluation of decreased vision. Ophthalmologic
cases.presentation:
A 5-year-old boy presents for evaluation of decreased vision. Ophthalmologic
examination reveals degenerative changes involving the vitreous and retina and
an optically empty vitreous. Which one of the following is the LEAST likely
diagnosis?

questions.question:
A 5-year-old boy presents for evaluation of decreased vision. Ophthalmologic
examination reveals degenerative changes involving the vitreous and retina and
an optically empty vitreous. Which one of the following is the LEAST likely
diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Kearns-Sayre syndrome
Wagner vitreoretinal dystrophy is characterized by vitreoretinal degenerative
changes, including an optically empty vitreous, high myopia, perivascuiar lattice
degeneration, and retinal detachment. Cataract formation may also be associated
with this disorder.
Stickler syndrome describes findings typical ofWagnei dystrophy in association
with die systemic features of Marfanoid habitus, Pierre-Robin anomaly, progressive
joirn degeneration, arthritis, deafness, and heart defects, It is an autosomal
dominant disease with variable expressivity.
Characteristic findings in the autosomal recessively inherited Goldmann-Favre
dystrophy include vitreous si rands and veils, an optically empty vitreous, foveal and
peripheral retinoschisis, attenuated retinal vessels, optic nerve pallor, and cataract
formation. Both (he ERG and EOG are abnormal, differentiating this condition
from juvenile retinoschisis, in which the EOG is normal.
Although a characteristic of Kearns-Sayre syndrome is pigmentary degeneration of
the retina, visual function is typically preserved. There is no significant vitreous
involvement. Other features of this syndrome include progressive external
uphihalnioplegia and, importantly, heart block, which may result in sudden death.
Inheritance is via mitochondria] DNA, and onset is before the age of 20 years.

cases.title: Which (me oi the following statements regarding cone...
cases.presentation: Which (me oi the following statements regarding cone dystrophies is FALSE?

questions.question: Which (me oi the following statements regarding cone dystrophies is FALSE?
questions.answer:
Night blindness and loss of peripheral vision frequently develop late in the
course of the disease.
Characteristics of cone dystrophies include decreased central vision, color blindness,
and photophobia, which develop within the first or second decade of life. The mode
of inheritance is usually autosomal recessive; however, most familial cases are
autosomal dominant.
Night blindness and loss of peripheral vision are uncommon even in advanced
cases, distinguishing this disorder from retinilis pigmentosa. Subjective visual
complaints typically precede macular changes on eye examination. The most
common abnormality is the "bull's-eye" macular lesion, followed by the "salt-and-
peppcr" appearing macula with diffuse pigment stippling. The least common form is
characterized by atrophy of the choriocapillaris, choroidal vessels, pigment
epithelium, and photoreceptors. Some patients may develop a pattern of
degeneration that mimics closely that of Stargardt's disease or fundus
liavimaculatus. Optic atrophy, especially temporally, may occur as well.
ERG findings of decreased amplitude on the single-flash photopic and flicker
responses along with reduced flicker fusion frequency confirm the diagnosis.
Ultimately, visual acuity ranges from 20/60 to 20/400 with symmetric involvement of
both eyes.
keyPoints: Night blindness and loss of peripheral vision frequently develop late in the
course of the disease.

questions.question:
A 10-year-old boy is referred for evaluation of decreased vision in his left eye.
Examination reveals an uncorrected visual acuity of 20/20 in his right eye and 20/40
in his left, which is correctable to 20/20 with glasses. Examination of the left fundus
reveals a yellow-orange cystic macular lesion (Fig. 5-14). The father of this child
reports that several members of his family including himself and a sister have
suffered from mild to moderate deterioration of vision since youth. Which one of
the following would be most helpful in establishing the diagnosis?
questions.answer:
EOG
Best's vitelliform dystrophy is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by an
abnormal EOG in carriers, asymptomatic patients with normal-appearing fttndi, as
well as in affected individuals; and a normal ERG. Both ERG and EOG are needed
to confirm this diagnosis.
In the early (pre-vitelliform) stage, the retina may appear normal, although the
EOG is abnormal. In the vitelliform stage, a cyst-like yellow-orange lesion develops,
typically in the macula, between the ages of 4 and 10 years. It is usually 1 to 5 disc-
diameters in size and has a "sunny-side-up" egg yolk appearance. Central vision is
usually good at this stage. With time, the vitelliform stage evolves into the
"scrambled egg" stage, in which the material within the cystic structure becomes

cases.title: 2 - unilateral periorbilal inflammation. Which one of
cases.presentation:
A 2-year-old child presents with unilateral periorbilal inflammation. Which one of
the following is the LEAST likely cause?

questions.question:
A 2-year-old child presents with unilateral periorbilal inflammation. Which one of
the following is the LEAST likely cause?
questions.answer:
Toxoplasmosis
A ruptured dermoid cyst can incite a tremendous amount of inflammation.
Rhabdomyosarcoma presents with rapid proplosis and reddish discoloration of the
eyelids, mimicking orbital cellulitis. However, the redness is generally not
accompanied by increased warmth. Other things to consider include pseudotumor,
leukemia, eosinophilic granuloma, and infantile cortical hyperostosis.
Toxoplasmosis is limited to intraocular inflammation and does not cause periocular
inflammation.

cases.title: 2M - periorbital ecchymosis. The differential diagnosis
cases.presentation:
A 2-year-old boy presents with periorbital ecchymosis. The differential diagnosis
includes -.ill of the rollowing EXCEPT:

questions.question:
A 2-year-old boy presents with periorbital ecchymosis. The differential diagnosis
includes -.ill of the rollowing EXCEPT:
questions.answer:
dermoid cyst
Neuroblastoma is the most common source of orbital metastasis in children.
Metastatic neuroblasioma produces proptosis with periorbital ecchymosis. Bilateral
involvement is seen in half of the cases. Orbital infiltration by leukemia causes lid
swelling, proptosis, and ecchymosis. Lymphangioma lesions consist of lymph-filled
channels lined by endothelium and separated by thin, delicate walls with small
blood vessels that are broken easily. Lymphangiomas infiltrate orbital tissues exten
sively, and intralesional bleeding is common. Dermoid cysts may rupture and spill
their contents, inciting a significant amount of inflammation, but bleeding does
not occur.

muscle can appeal' lo underact (inhibitional palsy of the contralaleral antagonist)
and the coniralaieral inferior rectus can undergo contracture, leading lo a "double
elevator palsy."

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Untreated chronic uveitis can lead to cataracts, glaucoma and cystoid macular edema. I would advise that the
patient follow-up every 3 months to monitor uveitis as the inflammation can be chronic and relatively asymptomatic.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Cloudy and enlarged cornea

Il_ Differential Diagnosis:


®)
Ill His

e)

Congenital Glaucoma

Congenital Hereditary Endothelial Dystrophy (CHED)

Congenital Hereditary Stromal Dystrophy (CHSD)

STUMPED (sclerocornea, trauma, ulcers, metabolic, peters, PPMD, dermoid)
Encephalofacial angiomatosis (Sturge-Weber syndrome)

tory/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

| would seek a history of photophobia, epiphora, and blepharospasm. I
want to know if there is family history of congenital glaucoma or
CHED, as these are inherited. Finally, a history of trauma (i.e. forceps
injury) and maternal infections during pregnancy to narrow the DDX.
Examination, | would evaluate vision and check for RAPD. Anterior
segment exam looking for corneal edema, corneal diameter (> 12mm
suggests congenital glaucoma), horizontal breaks in Descemet’s
membrane (i.e. Haab striae), and corneal thickness (pachymetry).
Gonioscopy may reveal abnormal angle anatomy associated with
congenital glaucoma. I would evaluate the eye pressure, and perform a
dilated exam evaluating the optic nerve for cupping and rule-out diffuse
choroidal hemangiomas (encephalofacial angiomatosis). A cycloplegic
retinoscopy to determine myopic shift related to progressive elongation
(i.e. congenital glaucoma). | would examine adnexa for port-wine
stains (encephalofacial angiomatosis) and for abrasions/scars associated
with trauma to help narrow DDxX. I might need to examine under
anesthesia if unable to accomplish test in the office.
keyPoints: Cloudy and enlarged cornea

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o I would suggest surgical intervention with goniotomy or trabeculotomy

to lower the intraocular pressure and preserve optic nerve function. I
would also initiate treatment with oral and topical intraocular pressure
lowering agents initially to gauge response and buy time until surgery
possible. Would definitely avoid brimonidine or equivalents due to
apnea risk.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
[e)}

Young girl with band keratopathy, I would seek a history of uveitis,
ocular trauma, ocular surgery, or retinal detachment repair. A careful
ROS to elucidate history of joint pain or swelling associated with
arthritis, systemic metabolic disorders, or renal failure that can lead to
hypercalcemic states. Would document vision and then focus on
anterior segment. Would perform a slit-lamp exam to confirm the
diagnosis of band keratopathy looking for deposits in the interpalpebral
zone starting in the limbal regions of 3 and 9 o’clock. Would look to
see that its location is indeed within Bowman’s layer. Would examine
the corneal endothelial surface for keratic precipitates, and look at the
anterior chamber for findings indicative of active inflammation. Would
also study the iris and lens looking for posterior synechiae, iris bombe,
and cataract. A measurement of eye pressure would be critical to
ensure that it is not elevated, and a dilated exam to rule-out posterior
pathology (optic nerve cupping and post. inflammation). Would
evaluate hands, wrists, knees, and ankles for arthritic signs and palpate
thyroid for enlargement. Would obtain rheumatoid factor (RF), anti-
nuclear antibody (ANA), calcium, phosphorous, angiotensin converting
enzyme, and lysozyme labs to rule-in JIA and rule-out sarcoid and
metabolic d/o.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Would discuss natural progression of chronic inflammation and the
development of cataracts, glaucoma, cystoids macular edema, and band
keratopathy with the parents. Would inform them that follow—up every
3 months is needed to monitor uveitis; as pauci-articular, RF- and
ANAG+ JIA can be silent, recurrent, and refractory to treatment.
Moreover, inflammation can also occur in other forms of JIA.

cases.title: bilateral discharge
cases.presentation: A 10-day-old baby presents with bilateral discharge.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
©. _Bilateral, mucopurulent discharge from the eyes

Differential Diagnosis:
o Bacterial conjunctivitis

o Chlamydial conjunctivitis

o Herpetic conjunctivitis

o Chemical conjunctivitis

IML History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

© The history is critical in this type of case and must focus first on the
birth history. I would start by asking if there were any maternal
gonorrheal, chlamydial, or herpetic infections or if there was prolonged
rupture of membranes. A history of ocular prophylaxis will help
narrow the differential also; specifically, it is important to know if
antibiotics, iodine, or silver nitrate were instilled into the baby’s eyes at
birth. In addition, the timing of the bilateral discharge is important as
chemical conjunctivitis typically occurs within 24 hours and infectious
etiologies typically occur days to weeks later. I would then focus my
examination on lids, adnexa, conjunctiva, and cornea looking for areas
of epithelial breakdown where ulceration and perforation can occur. A
dilated posterior segment evaluation should also be performed to rule-
out any other pathology that may be associated with the findings. My
evaluation would also include laboratory testing of the discharge for
gram stain and culture for gonorrhea and Chlamydia. A gram stain
showing gram-negative, intracellular diplococci and a culture on
Thayer-Martin and chocolate agars can confirm gonorrheal
conjunctivitis. In addition, scrapings must be sent for enzyme-linked
immunoassays or direct fluorescent antibody tests for Chlamydia.
keyPoints: ©. _Bilateral, mucopurulent discharge from the eyes

Bacterial conjunctivitis
Chlamydial conjunctivitis
Herpetic conjunctivitis
Chemical conjunctivitis

questions.question: What is your assessment?
questions.answer: o Presumed gonococcal or Chlamydial conjunctivitis
keyPoints: Presumed gonococcal or Chlamydial conjunctivitis
scoringKeywords: Presumed gonococcal or Chlamydial conjunctivitis

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
© The treatment would consist of intravenous or intramuscular
ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for 1 week or intravenous penicillin G for
gonorrhea. Oral erythromycin 50 mg/kg per day in 4 divided doses for
10-14 days is needed for Chlamydia. Therapy can then be tailored
based on culture results once those are available. I would also initiate
topical irrigation of the ocular surface to remove conjunctival
discharge, but it may not help unless there is a corneal ulcer. Finally,
involvement of a pediatrician or neonatologist would be important
especially for Chlamydial infections as respiratory and gastrointestinal
involvement can be co-existent and can cause significant morbidity.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
o Would enquire about gestational age and birth weight, as likelihood of
ROP increases with decreasing gestational age and birth weights below
1500 g. In addition, supplemental oxygen, respiratory and GI disease
also increases risk of ROP. Since this child is a male, IP is unlikely.
Also supporting this conclusion would be the lack of a whirl-like,
“splashed-paint” rash on the skin. Would consider Norrie, which occur
in males. Would begin exam by evaluating the anterior segment for
abnormalities such as prominent iris and pupillary vessels that can be
associated with ROP; and cataracts or anterior segment anomalies that
are seen in Norrie disease. Would examine the periphery of both eyes
to stage and zone the ROP, and look for presence of Plus disease.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
External photograph showing unilateral leukocoria.

Differential Diagnosis:
Retinoblastoma (RB)

Coat’s disease

Persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (PHPV)
Retinopathy of prematurity (ROP)

Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy (FEVR)
Toxocariasis

III _History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

Would ask parents when white pupil was first noted, ask family history
of retinoblastoma or consanguinity, premature birth, previous episodes
of ared, irritated eye, and previous eye exams that documented a
normal fundus. Would evaluate the vision, and perform a full anterior
and posterior segment exam with dilation in the office to rule-out
cataract and to gauge posterior segment. May need to examine under
anesthesia to evaluate corneal diameter, eye pressure, anterior chamber
for cell or pseudohypopyon, iris for neovascularization, and lens for
cataract. Would perform a dilated exam looking for vitreous cells and
seeding, masses within and below the retina, and large areas of
telangiectasias and exudation from Coat’s disease, RB, or FEVR. |
would use sclera depression to obtain views to the ora serrata. If
masses found I would perform an ultrasound to look for calcifications
and to measure the height of the lesions. I would also obtain an MRI of
the brain and orbits to evaluate for optic nerve enlargement, extraorbital
extension, and pineal gland for involvement in trilateral retinoblastoma.

questions.answer: o Retinoblastoma until proven otherwise

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Would involve oncologist and ophthalmologist with expertise in RB.

Treatment would depend on staging in that small, isolated lesions may
be amenable to cryotherapy and photocoagulation, while larger masses
would require chemoreduction with systemic therapy. In unilateral
and/or large tumors, enucleation may be an option. Repeat exam is
indicated to assess for treatment effect and development of tumors in
the other eye between cycles of therapy and every 3 months thereafter.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Would educate family regarding diagnosis and its natural history. In
the US, there is better than 95% survival with appropriate treatment.
There is risk of developing tumors in the other eye, and 1/3 of the time
this is inherited and as such siblings might be at risk. I would also
arrange genetic counseling, especially if planning to more children.
Would discuss risk of secondary malignancies in cases of germline
mutation RBs and to avoid radiation in these patients. Although
effective it is avoided in patients with suspected germline RB.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
External photograph of an infant showing a large-angle esotropia

Differential Diagnosis:
Infantile esotropia

Accommodative esotropia

Sensory esotropia

Sixth nerve palsy/Duane’s retraction syndrome

Il _History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

Would ask about the onset of eye turn, whether it is intermittent or
constant, if it involves just one eye or both, and if there is any family
history of strabismus. Would also ask to review old photographs to
help with some of this history and to determine whether there might be
a fixation preference. Would focus my exam on checking stereopsis
with Lang card, ocular alignment with cover-uncover and alternate
cover testing if possible, and ocular motility to make sure there is no
limitation of abduction. I would assess the vision in each eye by
preferential looking tests to determine if either eye has amblyopia. A
full anterior and posterior segment exam would be critical to rule-out
other ocular pathology such as cataracts and retinoblastoma that can
cause a sensory strabismus. Would perform a cylcoplegic refraction to
see if there is significant hyperopia as this could also be an
accommodative esotropia. Accomodative esotropias tend to have more
than +2.00 diopters of hyperopia.

questions.answer: o Esotropia, likely congenital

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o If amblyopia were present, I would prescribe patching of the better

seeing eye to combat the amblyopia. The duration of patching would
depend on the depth of the amblyopia but starts with 2 hours per day
while the child is engaged in any activities. With regards to the
esotropia, | would prescribe the full hyperopic correction if greater than
+2.00 diopters of hyperopia were present. In its absence, | would
suggest surgical correction of the esotropia by symmetric bilateral
medial rectus recessions prior to the age of 2 to maximize binocular
potential.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Oo


I would educate parents about the natural history of the condition,
which is for good cosmetic result and the retention of stereopsis if the
esotropia is corrected early. Would inform them that patching therapy
can stave off amblyopia completely. The child must be monitored for
subsequent development of dissociated vertical deviations, consecutive
exotropia, and recurrence of esotropia later in life.

Ill History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

o Would ask about the age of onset, intermittency, and the laterality of
the wandering eye. Would ask about complaints from the child of not
seeing well or history of clumsiness, frequent blinking or frequent eye
rubbing to assess for diplopia the child may be experiencing. Would
ask about a family history of strabismus and about a history of
prematurity since retinopathy of prematurity can lead to macular
dragging and a positive angle kappa. Would assess stereopsis with a
Lang or Titmus stereoacuity card, and the degree of exotropia with
either cover-uncover, alternate cover testing, or Krimsky light reflex
testing. Would also examine ocular motility to rule-out Duane’s

adduction. I would check the visual acuity by Lea or HOTV-matching
to determine if there is any amblyopia. Would also perform an exam of
the anterior and posterior segments with dilation to rule-out media
opacities, retinal pathologies, and optic nerve anomalies that might
cause sensory deprivation-related exotropia. Finally, I would
retinoscope the child for cycloplegic refraction as intermittent exotropia
can be associated with myopia that may improve with correction,

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
Would enquire about a family history of congenital cataracts or
retinoblastoma, and whether the child was born premature, as ROP can
lead to bilateral retinal detachments causing leucokoria. Would assess
vision as best as possible, and would perform a slit-lamp exam to
determine if there are cataracts. A dilated fundus exam would also be
critical to rule-out retinoblastoma, primary hyperplastic persistent
vitreous, meduloepithelioma, or other retinal pathology that can cause
leukocoria despite clear media. An ultrasound may be necessary if there
were no view.

questions.question: What is your assessment?
questions.answer: o Congenital cataracts, bilateral
keyPoints: Congenital cataracts, bilateral
scoringKeywords: Congenital cataracts, bilateral

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Would refer the child for pediatrician exam for other stigmata of

systemic disease and to assess for TORCH infections, including titers.
Would screen the urine for reducing substances such as galactosemia,
amino acids (Lowe’s syndrome), and red blood cell galactokinase
(Alport’s syndrome). Ifthe cataracts were not visually significant, they
can be observed. If they are visually significant, early surgery can
reduce amblyopia and maximize visual outcome. The cataract
extraction is done by aspiration because of the soft lens, and must be
accompanied by a posterior capsulotomy and anterior vitrectomy
because of high rates of posterior capsular opacification in children.
Most young children, especially < 6 months of age, I would not implant
an intraocular lens.

questions.answer:
O°

Would educate family regarding the natural history of congenital
cataracts, which is for good final visual acuity if removed early enough
to decrease risk of amblyopia and that patching might be required to
maximize final acuity. In addition, the refractive rehabilitation would
likely involve aphakic contact lenses or spectacles. An intraocular lens
might be implanted later in life.

questions.question: What is your assessment?
questions.answer: o Capillary hemangioma
keyPoints: Capillary hemangioma
scoringKeywords: Capillary hemangioma

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Would correct any astigmatism with glasses. Generally,
capillary hemangiomas can flatten the adjacent cornea, which are
corrected with negative cylinder 90 degrees away. Would patch
the left eye if evidence of amblyopia. If no amblyopia, it would
be reasonable to observe with spectacle correction, as there is
expected resolution by a few years of age. However, if there is
evidence of worsening amblyopia despite patching, treatment
can be instituted with oral propranolol, systemic steroids, topical
timolol, or intralesional steroid injection. All of these treatment
options carry risks such as propranolol causing bradycardia,
corticosteroids causing growth retardation, topical timolol being
unlikely to resolve large and deep lesions, and intralesional
injection steroid injection causing central retinal artery occlusion
from retrograde embolization. If amblyopia is severe and the
lesion is increasing in size, would consider surgical excision.

about history of trauma and perform a brief physical exam for systemic
signs of child abuse, systemic findings such as tall, slender body
habitus with arachnodactyly (Marfan’s), mental retardation and light-
colored hair (homocysteinuria), elastic skin (Ehlers-Danlos), or short
stature with stubby fingers (Weill-Marchesani). Would assess vision
and refract the affected eye prior to dilation to see if acuity could be
improved. Would perform a slit-lamp exam looking for signs of lens
capsule violation, microspherophakia (Weill-Marchesani), and blue
coloration to the sclera (Ehlers-Danlos type VI). Would look for signs
of retinal damage from trauma such as vitreal or retinal hemorrhages,
retinal tears, commotion retinae, or retinal detachments.

questions.question: What is your assessment?
questions.answer:
o. While trauma is the most common cause of subluxation, assuming no
history of trauma, Marfan’s is the most likely cause of lens subluxation
in the upward and outward direction. Both Ehlers-Danlos and
Homocysteinuria tend to sublux downward and inward, and
homocysteinuria can be excluded by the absence of mental retardation.

Plan:

o If vision is good and no symptomatic diplopia, the lens can be observed
as long as there is no capsule violation, With capsular violation or if
vision is uncorrectable, | would consider pars plana lensectomy with
either implantation of sutured intraocular lens, an anterior chamber
intraocular lens, an aphakic contact lens or spectacle correction. Would
also get an internist evaluation for physical signs of systemic disease,
including cardiac echo for aortic valve involvement (Marfan’s) and
sodium nitroprusside testing (homocysteinuria).

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
oT would educate the patient’s parents stating that the prognosis for the
eye can be good with appropriate management of the lens subluxation.
However, I would counsel them that retinal detachments can manifest
in these children. If a systemic condition is suspected based on the
physical exam findings (and I suspect Marfan’s in this case), then I
would coordinate care with the child’s pediatrician and arrange for
genetic counseling.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
Would ask the patient’s age, the age when epiphora was first noted, as
well as any history of swelling or erythema near the medial canthal
area, mucopurulent discharge, trauma, or chronic photophobia. Would
assess the vision, eye pressure, corneal diameters, the appearance of the
puncta, and whether there is an elevated tear lake and crusting along the
lid margins and medial canthus. Would look for corneal edema,
clouding, and Haab striae on slit-lamp exam to exclude congenital
glaucoma. Would evert the eyelid looking for a foreign body if there
were abrasions on the cornea and if the tearing were acute in onset.
Would palpate over the nasolacrimal sac to feel for warmth or swelling
and to express mucopurulent material if present. Would perform a dye
disappearance test. Would perform a cycloplegic retinoscopy and full
dilated exam to rule-out other ocular pathology, which can be
associated with NLDO.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
I would educate the parents regarding the natural history of the disease,
which is caused by an imperforate valve of Hasner, and which resolves
spontaneously in 90% of cases by | year of age with digital pressure. If
it does not resolve, then probing of the nasolacrimal system should be
done after 1 year of age. The prognosis is good. Also, parents must be
warned to monitor for signs of infection such as warmth, swelling and
tenderness over the lacrimal sac area, or purulent discharge from the
puncta.

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient and what exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
I would enquire about variability in the eye turn with time of
day, focusing on near objects, or tiredness. I would also inquire
about intermittent exotropia and esotropia that is worse than
what the parents perceive to be present now. A full family
history of pediatric eye disease or of strabismus or amblyopia
would also be important. I would start with cover-uncover and
alternate cover testing to look for a manifest tropia if the child
can participate; otherwise, I would assess for a manifest
deviation by Hirschberg testing to assess the corneal like reflexes
for centration and symmetry. I would examine the visual acuity
as best as possible by preferential-looking testing and I would
gauge the child for asymmetric objection to occlusion of either
eye. Lastly, I would perform a cycloplegic retinoscopy and a
dilated fundus examination to ensure the remainder of the child’s
eye has no pathology.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o External photograph showing a young child with slight ptosis of
the left upper eyelid, approximately 2 mm of anisocoria with the i
left being larger than right pupil, and iris heterochromia. |

Il_ Differential Diagnosis:

o Congenital Horner’s syndrome |

; o Acquired Horner’s syndrome |
o Physiological anisocoria
o Pharmacologic anisocoria
Ill History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

o IL would ask the age at which the anisocoria was first noted and
whether it is intermittent or progressively worsening. I would
ask for old photographs of the child to corroborate this I would
also ask about any history of recent exposure to
parasympathomimetics, such as scopolamine patches, or recent
eye examinations with dilation. I would examine the pupil size
in light and dark, expecting to see anisocoria greater in the dark
than in the light, and dilation lag, and would examine the lid for
ptosis (1-2mm), which is associated with Horner’s syndrome. I
would examine the irides for heterochromia, as a congenital
Horner’s is associated with heterochromia, whereas acquired
Horner’s is not (outside of the first few months of life). This is
because sympathetic innervation is required for darkening of the
irides.

o | would ask about the time course and age of onset, and any history of

history of congenital ptosis. I would also ask to review old
photographs of the child to see if there is any evidence of congenital

expect to see decreased levator function in upgaze, and expect a low or
indistinct eyelid crease. A cycloplegic refraction may show significant
anisometropia with high cylinder on the affected side.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
> If the degree of ptosis is mild and no evidence of amblyopia is found,
then I would observe. However, if the ptosis is severe, OF if amblyopia
is developing, then I would treat with frontalis suspension using
autologous fascia lata or exogenous artificial material. In addition, I
would prescribe appropriate spectacles if necessary and patching of the
sound eye if there were evidence of amblyopia.
