questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
o Photograph showing patches of reddish skin bilaterally, mostly on the
left V1 and V2 trigeminal nerve distribution. Appears to have corneal
diameter asymmetry and heterochromia irides.

Differential Diagnosis:
o Nevus flammeus/port-wine stain +/- Sturge-Weber syndrome (SWS)

o Periocular contact dermatitis

o Erysipilis

o -Preseptal cellulitis

Ill History/Physical Exam/Evaluation:

6 Would ask about the age of onset and history of progression of the
lesion. Would ask about signs of glaucoma such as tearing,
photophobia, differences in the size of the eyes, and intermittent
clouding of the cornea that can occur on the affected side with SWS.
My exam would include an evaluation of the vision with emphasis on
eye preference indicating amblyopia. Would perform a slit-lamp exam
of the conjunctiva for telangiectatic vessels, the cornea for edema,
Haab’s striae, and horizontal diameter, and irides for heterochromia,
and evaluate eye pressure. Would perform a dilated exam looking for
diffuse choroidal hemangioma and optic nerve cupping. Would include
a cycloplegic refraction expecting long-standing glaucoma to cause a
relative myopia of the affected eye. Would consider neuroimaging
with MRI because of association with leptomeningieal angiomatosis.

© Ifthe IOP is elevated, I would initiate topical agents such as timolol
and dorzolamide (avoiding brimonidine in infants because of risk of
CNS depression) and oral acetazolamide. If the IOP is uncontrollable
medically, the child would need to proceed to goniotomy or
trabeculotomy. The child should also be referred to a neurologist for
consultation of possible seizure activity and mental retardation that can
be associated with Sturge-Weber syndrome. | would prescribe the
correction for any anisometropia for full-time wear and patching of the
sound eye if amblyopia is present. Ifa diffuse choroidal hemangioma
is present and limiting vision, options such as external or proton beam
irradiation could be considered.

because of elevated episcleral venous pressure.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Possible diagnoses include nasolacrimal duct obstruction (NLDO), corneal abrasion, foreign body, iritis,
and congenital glaucoma.

and congenital glaucoma.

questions.question: What exam findings would you look for?
questions.answer:
Specific signs to look for on exam include increased lacrimal lake, discharge from lacrimal puncta,
corneal staining, foreign body on the ocular surface or tarsal conjunctiva, anterior chamber cell and flare,
buphthalmos, myopia, enlarged corneas, increased IOP, and optic disc cupping.

questions.question: What finding is depicted and what is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Haab’s striae (breaks in Descemet’s membrane), which is a sign of congenital glaucoma.
keyPoints: Haab’s striae (breaks in Descemet’s membrane), which is a sign of congenital glaucoma.
scoringKeywords: Haab’s striae (breaks in Descemet’s membrane), which is a sign of congenital glaucoma.

questions.question: What would you tell the mother about the diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Congenital glaucoma occurs in the first few months of life, is usually bilateral, and can be primary
(developmental abnormality of the angle structures, most commonly due to a mutation in the CYP1B1
gene), secondary, or associated with ocular or systemic syndromes. An examination under anesthesia may
be required to check tonometry, corneal diameter, gonioscopy, and ophthalmoscopy.
keyPoints: Congenital glaucoma occurs in the first few months of life, is usually bilateral, and can be primary
(developmental abnormality of the angle structures, most commonly due to a mutation in the CYP1B1
gene), secondary, or associated with ocular or systemic syndromes. An examination under anesthesia may
be required to check tonometry, corneal diameter, gonioscopy, and ophthalmoscopy.

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Treatment is surgical. Topical medications (beta-blockers or carbonic anhydrase inhibitors) are used to
control IOP until surgery can be performed. Brimonidine is contraindicated because it can be associated
with death. Surgical options include goniotomy (children <1.5 years old with clear cornea) and
trabeculotomy (cloudy cornea, children >1.5 years old, or two failed goniotomies). If these fail, then
trabeculectomy with mitomycin C, glaucoma drainage implant, and cycloablation are options. Correction
of any refractive error and treatment of amblyopia must also be performed.

with death. Surgical options include goniotomy (children <1.5 years old with clear cornea) and
trabeculotomy (cloudy cornea, children >1.5 years old, or two failed goniotomies). If these fail, then

f any refractive error and treatment of amblyopia must also be performed.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
The esotropia (ET) may be congenital, accommodative, nonaccommodative, mixed, or due to Duane’s

questions.question: What exam findings would enable you to determine the correct diagnosis?
questions.answer:
It is important to know the visual acuity and whether there is any amblyopia or refractive error. What is
the AC/A ratio? The ocular motility must be assessed and the deviation measured at distance and near.
Is the deviation comitant or incomitant, is there an A or V pattern, and is there any oblique muscle
overaction? Does she have a DVD? Is there latent nystagmus? Does she cross fixate? Is there a suppression
scotoma?

questions.question: How would you treat her?
questions.answer:
Rule out an accommodative component by prescribing glasses with the full cycloplegic refraction.
Strabismus surgery is performed on either one eye with a medial rectus recession and lateral rectus
resection (R&R), or both eyes with bilateral medial rectus recessions. The inferior oblique overaction is
also treated surgically with a weakening procedure.

resection (R&R), or both eyes with bilateral medial rectus recessions. The inferior oblique overaction is

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Congenital nystagmus is most commonly afferent (due to sensory deprivation), efferent (motor), latent,
or spasmus nutans. It is important to rule out acquired nystagmus.
keyPoints: Congenital nystagmus is most commonly afferent (due to sensory deprivation), efferent (motor), latent,
r spasmus nutans. It is important to rule out acquired nystagmus.

The boy turns his head to the side. If the nystagmus dampens in right gaze, what direction is the boy’s
head turn?
questions.answer: Left head turn to place the eyes in the direction of the null point.
keyPoints: Left head turn to place the eyes in the direction of the null point.
scoringKeywords: Left head turn to place the eyes in the direction of the null point.

questions.question: What are the typical features of motor nystagmus?
questions.answer:
The characteristics of motor nystagmus are: usually horizontal in all positions of gaze, increased intensity
with fixation, presence of a null point, decreased by convergence, absent during sleep, may have latent
component, reversal with horizontal optokinetic nystagmus (OKN) testing, and associated strabismus.
keyPoints: The characteristics of motor nystagmus are: usually horizontal in all positions of gaze, increased intensity
with fixation, presence of a null point, decreased by convergence, absent during sleep, may have latent
component, reversal with horizontal optokinetic nystagmus (OKN) testing, and associated strabismus.

questions.question: What treatments can be used for congenital nystagmus?
questions.answer:
Consider base-out prism glasses to stimulate convergence and thereby dampen the nystagmus. If there is
a head turn >50% of the time to keep eyes in null point, then surgical correction with the Kestenbaum
procedure can be performed.
keyPoints: Consider base-out prism glasses to stimulate convergence and thereby dampen the nystagmus. If there is

questions.question: What is the triad of findings in spasmus nutans?
questions.answer: Fine, rapid, asymmetric eye movements, head nodding, and torticollis.
keyPoints: Fine, rapid, asymmetric eye movements, head nodding, and torticollis.
scoringKeywords: Fine, rapid, asymmetric eye movements, head nodding, and torticollis.

questions.question: What is the work up of spasmus nutans?
questions.answer:
Spasmus nutans is a diagnosis of exclusion, so a careful exam of the pupils (RAPD) and optic nerve is
necessary, and neuroimaging is indicated to rule out a tumor (chiasmal glioma or parasellar tumor,
which can produce similar eye movements). Spasmus nutans is benign and disappears by the age of 5
years.

questions.answer:
Parks-Bielschowsky 3-step test. If this shows a superior oblique palsy, then vertical fusion amplitudes
would be helpful to distinguish between congenital and other causes.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis and what other history would be relevant?
questions.answer:
Left superior oblique palsy, which is most commonly congenital or traumatic. This could be a
decompensated congenital superior oblique (SO) palsy or a traumatic one. It would be helpful to ask
about recent illness or trauma. It is also necessary to ask about other neurologic symptoms. Old
photographs may be useful to determine whether there was a long-standing head posture.

questions.question: What head position would the boy have?
questions.answer:
The head is positioned in the direction of action of the weak muscle in order to minimize the diplopia.
Therefore, he would have his chin down, face turned to the opposite side, and head tilted to the opposite
shoulder.

questions.question: The patient plays football and says he was stunned after a rough tackle. How would you treat him?
questions.answer:
For an isolated palsy, the initial treatment is observation and prism glasses or occlusion of one eye to
alleviate the diplopia. If there are other neurologic deficits, then neuroimaging is indicated. The palsy
may improve with time. If it does not and is stable for at least 6 months, then muscle surgery can be
considered. The Knapp classification is helpful for determining the best procedure. The Harado Ito
procedure (lateral transposition of superior oblique tendon) may be used to correct the torsional
component.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: V-pattern exotropia (XT).
keyPoints: V-pattern exotropia (XT).
scoringKeywords: V-pattern exotropia (XT).

questions.question: What is the appropriate treatment?
questions.answer:
Correct any refractive error and treat amblyopia if present. The XT can be treated with overminused
spectacles to induce accommodative convergence, fusional convergence training with progressive base-out
prism, or prism therapy with base-in prisms. If the ocular misalignment is present >50% of the time,
then surgery is necessary. A prism adaptation test is performed to uncover the full amount of deviation.
Surgical correction is performed on either one eye with a lateral rectus recession and medial rectus
resection (R&R), or both eyes with bilateral lateral rectus recessions. The V pattern is corrected with
horizontal rectus muscle transposition (in the direction of the desired weakening [mnemonic:
MALE – Medial recti moved toward Apex of pattern or Lateral recti moved toward Empty space of
pattern]) or with oblique muscle weakening, depending on whether or not there is any oblique
overaction.

questions.question: What are the most important parts of the eye exam to check?
questions.answer:
It is important to determine whether the lid lesion is affecting vision. Therefore, it is critical to measure
the visual acuity, refractive error, ocular alignment, and motility.
keyPoints: It is important to determine whether the lid lesion is affecting vision. Therefore, it is critical to measure
the visual acuity, refractive error, ocular alignment, and motility.
scoringKeywords: It is important to determine whether the lid lesion is affecting vision. Therefore, it is critical to measure
the visual acuity, refractive error, ocular alignment, and motility.

questions.question: What are the treatment options?
questions.answer:
Treat any refractive error and amblyopia if present. Most capillary hemangiomas regress by age 10 years
old; however, if the tumor is affecting vision it may require intervention with intralesional (risk of central
retinal artery occlusion) or systemic steroids, systemic propranolol, laser photocoagulation, embolization,
or excision. Topical timolol may also be effective and is a safer alternative to the aforementioned
traditional treatments.

traditional treatments.

questions.question: What systemic syndrome is associated with this tumor?
questions.answer:
Kassabach-Merritt syndrome, which is a consumptive coagulopathy with platelet trapping that causes
thrombocytopenia and cardiac failure and has a mortality rate of 30%.
keyPoints: Kassabach-Merritt syndrome, which is a consumptive coagulopathy with platelet trapping that causes
thrombocytopenia and cardiac failure and has a mortality rate of 30%.
scoringKeywords: Kassabach-Merritt syndrome, which is a consumptive coagulopathy with platelet trapping that causes
thrombocytopenia and cardiac failure and has a mortality rate of 30%.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Capillary hemangioma, which is the most common benign eyelid tumor in children

cases.title: 2F - the girl’s pupils look funny in certain lighting...
cases.presentation: A mother brings her 2-year-old daughter to see you because she notices the girl’s pupils look funny in certain lighting conditions. The child has normal vision and ocular motility. Slit lamp exam reveals the abnormality seen in the photo.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Posterior polar cataract.
keyPoints: Posterior polar cataract.
scoringKeywords: Posterior polar cataract.

questions.question: What is the etiology of bilateral congenital cataracts?
questions.answer:
Idiopathic, hereditary (usually autosomal dominant [AD]), metabolic, associated with ocular disorder,
intrauterine infection (TORCH – [Toxoplasma, Other viruses, Rubella, Cytomegalovirus, Herpesvirus]),
maternal drug ingestion or malnutrition, or trauma.
keyPoints: Idiopathic, hereditary (usually autosomal dominant [AD]), metabolic, associated with ocular disorder,
intrauterine infection (TORCH – [Toxoplasma, Other viruses, Rubella, Cytomegalovirus, Herpesvirus]),
maternal drug ingestion or malnutrition, or trauma.
scoringKeywords: Idiopathic, hereditary (usually autosomal dominant [AD]), metabolic, associated with ocular disorder,
intrauterine infection (TORCH – [Toxoplasma, Other viruses, Rubella, Cytomegalovirus, Herpesvirus]),
maternal drug ingestion or malnutrition, or trauma.

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
The treatment depends on the size of the cataract and whether it is affecting vision. The critical size for
causing vision impairment is ≥3 mm. Posterior polar cataracts <3 mm in diameter should be followed
closely for growth and decreased visual acuity. Surgery (cataract extraction and IOL insertion) is
performed when the cataract is visually significant.
keyPoints: The treatment depends on the size of the cataract and whether it is affecting vision. The critical size for
causing vision impairment is ≥3 mm. Posterior polar cataracts <3 mm in diameter should be followed
closely for growth and decreased visual acuity. Surgery (cataract extraction and IOL insertion) is
performed when the cataract is visually significant.
scoringKeywords: The treatment depends on the size of the cataract and whether it is affecting vision. The critical size for
causing vision impairment is ≥3 mm. Posterior polar cataracts <3 mm in diameter should be followed

performed when the cataract is visually significant.

questions.question:
When a patient with the type of cataract shown above undergoes phacoemulsification, there is an
increased risk of what complication?
questions.answer: Posterior capsular rupture with possible vitreous loss and / or retained lens material.
keyPoints: Posterior capsular rupture with possible vitreous loss and / or retained lens material.
scoringKeywords: Posterior capsular rupture with possible vitreous loss and / or retained lens material.

questions.question: What routine step of cataract surgery should not be performed?
questions.answer:
A posterior capsular defect is often present, so hydrodissection should not be performed since this step
can rupture the posterior capsule.
keyPoints: A posterior capsular defect is often present, so hydrodissection should not be performed since this step
can rupture the posterior capsule.
scoringKeywords: A posterior capsular defect is often present, so hydrodissection should not be performed since this step
can rupture the posterior capsule.

cases.title: You are called to the neonatal intensive care unit...
cases.presentation: You are called to the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) to see a 31-week-old premature infant. The retinal appearance is demonstrated in the photo.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Stage 3 retinopathy of prematurity (ROP).
keyPoints: Stage 3 retinopathy of prematurity (ROP).
scoringKeywords: Stage 3 retinopathy of prematurity (ROP).

questions.question: What is the classification system of this disorder?
questions.answer:
ROP is classified by the type of retinal changes (stage), location (zone), and clock hours of retinal
involvement (extent).
Stage 1 = flat, circumferential, thin, white, demarcation line between the posterior vascularized and
peripheral avascular retina.
Stage 2 = demarcation line becomes elevated and organized into a pink-white ridge, no fibrovascular
growth visible.
Stage 3 = extraretinal fibrovascular proliferation from the surface of the ridge.
Stage 4 = dragging of vessels and subtotal traction retinal detachment:
4A = macula attached;
4B = macula detached.
Stage 5 = total retinal detachment.
Zone 1 = inner zone (posterior pole) corresponding to the area enclosed by a circle around the optic disc
with a radius equal to twice the distance from the disc to the macula (diameter of 60°).
Zone 2 = the area between Zone 1 and a circle centered on the optic disc and tangent to the nasal ora
serrata.
Zone 3 = the remaining temporal crescent of retina (last area to become vascularized).

involvement (extent).

questions.question: What are the risk factors?
questions.answer:
Premature birth (<36 weeks’ gestation), low birth weight (<1.5 kg), supplemental oxygen (>50 days
[controversial]), and complicated hospital course. The risk of ROP increases exponentially with earlier
prematurity and lower birth weight.
keyPoints: Premature birth (<36 weeks’ gestation), low birth weight (<1.5 kg), supplemental oxygen (>50 days
[controversial]), and complicated hospital course. The risk of ROP increases exponentially with earlier
prematurity and lower birth weight.
scoringKeywords: Premature birth (<36 weeks’ gestation), low birth weight (<1.5 kg), supplemental oxygen (>50 days
[controversial]), and complicated hospital course. The risk of ROP increases exponentially with earlier
prematurity and lower birth weight.

questions.question: What is plus disease?
questions.answer:
Plus disease occurs when there are at least 2 quadrants of shunted blood, which produces engorged,
tortuous vessels in the posterior pole, vitreous haze, and iris vascular congestion. This is a poor
prognostic sign.
keyPoints: Plus disease occurs when there are at least 2 quadrants of shunted blood, which produces engorged,
tortuous vessels in the posterior pole, vitreous haze, and iris vascular congestion. This is a poor
prognostic sign.
scoringKeywords: Plus disease occurs when there are at least 2 quadrants of shunted blood, which produces engorged,
tortuous vessels in the posterior pole, vitreous haze, and iris vascular congestion. This is a poor
prognostic sign.

questions.question: When is treatment indicated?
questions.answer:
Treatment used to be indicated for threshold disease (i.e. Stage 3+ disease with at least 5 contiguous or 8
noncontiguous clock hours of involvement in Zone 1 or 2), which represents the level at which 50% will
go blind without treatment.
The Early Treatment of ROP (ETROP) Study concluded that ablation of peripheral avascular retina with
laser photocoagulation should be performed earlier (i.e. type 1 ROP, which is defined as: Zone 1, any
stage of ROP with Plus disease; Zone 1, Stage 3 with or without Plus disease; Zone 2, Stage 2 or 3 with
Plus disease).
In certain circumstances, intravitreal injection of bevacizumab has been advocated, but is both off-label
and experimental. For Stage 4 or greater, pars plana vitrectomy is recommended.

questions.question: How would you treat him?
questions.answer:
This baby does not require treatment with laser or cyrotherapy. He should have serial retinal exams every
1–2 weeks until the extreme retinal periphery is vascularized, then monthly exams. Aggressive posterior
(AP) ROP or ‘Rush’ disease (i.e. Plus disease in Zone 1 or posterior Zone 2) must be detected because it
can rapidly progress to Stage 5 ROP within a few days.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer: The prognosis depends on the amount and stage of ROP. Up to 90% of cases resolve spontaneously.
keyPoints: The prognosis depends on the amount and stage of ROP. Up to 90% of cases resolve spontaneously.
scoringKeywords: The prognosis depends on the amount and stage of ROP. Up to 90% of cases resolve spontaneously.

questions.question: What are the potential complications?
questions.answer:
High myopia, strabismus, amblyopia, macular dragging, nystagmus, glaucoma, cataracts, keratoconus,
band keratopathy, retinal detachment, and phthisis bulbi.
keyPoints: High myopia, strabismus, amblyopia, macular dragging, nystagmus, glaucoma, cataracts, keratoconus,
band keratopathy, retinal detachment, and phthisis bulbi.
scoringKeywords: High myopia, strabismus, amblyopia, macular dragging, nystagmus, glaucoma, cataracts, keratoconus,
band keratopathy, retinal detachment, and phthisis bulbi.

cases.title: 8F - headaches in school. On exam her uncorrected vision is...
cases.presentation: An 8-year-old girl complains of headaches in school. On exam her uncorrected vision is 20/30 OD and 20/25 OS, manifest refraction of +1.00 D OD and –1.00 D OS yields 20/30 OD and 20/15 OS. Anterior and posterior segment exams are normal.

questions.question: What is the next test you would perform?
questions.answer: Cycloplegic refraction.
keyPoints: Cycloplegic refraction.
scoringKeywords: Cycloplegic refraction.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Hyperopia and anisometropic amblyopia OD.
keyPoints: Hyperopia and anisometropic amblyopia OD.
scoringKeywords: Hyperopia and anisometropic amblyopia OD.

questions.question: How would you manage this patient?
questions.answer:
She should be prescribed the full cycloplegic refraction and patching / penalization therapy started OS for
no more than 1 week per year of age before re-examination. This is continued until the vision in the
right eye has normalized or stabilized.
In an older patient who may not tolerate the full prescription, a postcycloplegic refraction pushing plus
is necessary and the glasses prescription may need to be increased gradually over time. If the patient is
unable to adapt to the degree of anisometropia in the glasses, then consider treatment with a contact
lens or laser vision correction OD.

questions.question:
What are the levels of anisometropia (myopic, hyperopic, and astigmatic) that require correction to
prevent possible amblyopia?
questions.answer: Myopic anisometropia ≥3 D, hyperopic anisometropia ≥1 D, and astigmatic anisometropia ≥1.5 D.
keyPoints: Myopic anisometropia ≥3 D, hyperopic anisometropia ≥1 D, and astigmatic anisometropia ≥1.5 D.
scoringKeywords: Myopic anisometropia ≥3 D, hyperopic anisometropia ≥1 D, and astigmatic anisometropia ≥1.5 D.

cases.title: 7F - accommodative esotropia (ET)
cases.presentation: This 7-year-old girl was diagnosed with accommodative esotropia (ET). Her mother would like a second opinion and brings her to see you.

questions.question: What are the different types of accommodative ET?
questions.answer:
Refractive accommodative (normal AC/A ratio), nonrefractive accommodative (high AC/A ratio), mixed
mechanism, and decompensated accommodative.
keyPoints: Refractive accommodative (normal AC/A ratio), nonrefractive accommodative (high AC/A ratio), mixed
mechanism, and decompensated accommodative.
scoringKeywords: Refractive accommodative (normal AC/A ratio), nonrefractive accommodative (high AC/A ratio), mixed
mechanism, and decompensated accommodative.

questions.question: How would you measure her AC/A ratio?
questions.answer:
There are 2 methods for determining the AC/A ratio:
Heterophoria method: AC/A = IPD + [(N−D)/diopter]
IPD = interpupillary distance (cm); N = near deviation; D = distance deviation; diopter = accommodative
demand at fixation distance.
Lens gradient method: AC/A = (WL−NL)/D
WL = deviation with lens in front of eye; NL = deviation without lens in front of eye; D = dioptric power
of lens used.
keyPoints: There are 2 methods for determining the AC/A ratio:
Heterophoria method: AC/A = IPD + [(N−D)/diopter]
IPD = interpupillary distance (cm); N = near deviation; D = distance deviation; diopter = accommodative
demand at fixation distance.
Lens gradient method: AC/A = (WL−NL)/D
WL = deviation with lens in front of eye; NL = deviation without lens in front of eye; D = dioptric power
f lens used.

questions.question: What type of ET does she have?
questions.answer:
The high hyperopia, normal AC/A ratio, and similar ET at distance and near indicate that she has a
refractive accommodative ET.
keyPoints: The high hyperopia, normal AC/A ratio, and similar ET at distance and near indicate that she has a
refractive accommodative ET.
scoringKeywords: The high hyperopia, normal AC/A ratio, and similar ET at distance and near indicate that she has a
refractive accommodative ET.

questions.question: How would you treat her?
questions.answer:
She should be prescribed the full cycloplegic refraction. The amblyopia OS should be treated with
patching or penalization OD for no more than 1 week per year of age before re-examination. Once the
vision in the left eye has normalized or stabilized, surgery can be performed for any residual or
nonaccommodative component of ET (any deviation >10 PD that is not eliminated with glasses).

cases.title: 3M - always looked smaller
cases.presentation: A 3-year-old boy is brought to your office because of a droopy eyelid. His mother states that his right eye has always looked smaller.

questions.question: What are the diagnosis and possible etiologies?
questions.answer:
Congenital ptosis, which can be myogenic (most common), neurogenic (congenital CN 3 palsy or
Horner’s syndrome), or rarely aponeurotic (birth trauma).
keyPoints: Congenital ptosis, which can be myogenic (most common), neurogenic (congenital CN 3 palsy or
Horner’s syndrome), or rarely aponeurotic (birth trauma).
scoringKeywords: Congenital ptosis, which can be myogenic (most common), neurogenic (congenital CN 3 palsy or
Horner’s syndrome), or rarely aponeurotic (birth trauma).

questions.question: What would you look for on exam to determine the etiology?
questions.answer:
Visual acuity, levator function, pupil size and response, ocular motility, presence of jaw winking, and iris
color.
keyPoints: Visual acuity, levator function, pupil size and response, ocular motility, presence of jaw winking, and iris
color.
scoringKeywords: Visual acuity, levator function, pupil size and response, ocular motility, presence of jaw winking, and iris
color.

questions.question: What are the characteristic findings of congenital myogenic ptosis?
questions.answer: Poor levator function, loss of the lid crease, eyelid lag, sometimes lagophthalmos, and rarely amblyopia.
keyPoints: Poor levator function, loss of the lid crease, eyelid lag, sometimes lagophthalmos, and rarely amblyopia.
scoringKeywords: Poor levator function, loss of the lid crease, eyelid lag, sometimes lagophthalmos, and rarely amblyopia.

questions.question: What is the treatment of congenital myogenic ptosis?
questions.answer:
Treat amblyopia if present, then surgical lid repair. For poor levator function, the usual technique is
frontalis suspension with silicone rods, fascia lata, or frontalis flap. Maximal levator resection may be
useful in some cases.
keyPoints: Treat amblyopia if present, then surgical lid repair. For poor levator function, the usual technique is
frontalis suspension with silicone rods, fascia lata, or frontalis flap. Maximal levator resection may be
useful in some cases.
scoringKeywords: Treat amblyopia if present, then surgical lid repair. For poor levator function, the usual technique is
frontalis suspension with silicone rods, fascia lata, or frontalis flap. Maximal levator resection may be
useful in some cases.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
The outcome for congenital ptosis repair is variable and depends on the degree of levator function, poor
function being more difficult to treat.
keyPoints: The outcome for congenital ptosis repair is variable and depends on the degree of levator function, poor
function being more difficult to treat.
scoringKeywords: The outcome for congenital ptosis repair is variable and depends on the degree of levator function, poor
function being more difficult to treat.

cases.title: 23 - she has a white spot on her eye that she wants removed
cases.presentation: A 23-year-old graduate student says she has a white spot on her eye that she wants removed.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Limbal dermoid.
keyPoints: Limbal dermoid.
scoringKeywords: Limbal dermoid.

questions.question: What type of tumor is this?
questions.answer: Choristoma that represents normal tissue in an abnormal location.
keyPoints: Choristoma that represents normal tissue in an abnormal location.
scoringKeywords: Choristoma that represents normal tissue in an abnormal location.

questions.question: Are there any associations?
questions.answer:
Limbal dermoids can cause astigmatism and amblyopia. They can be isolated or associated with
Goldenhar’s syndrome (preauricular skin tags, aural fistulas, eyelid coloboma, and vertebral anomalies).
keyPoints: Limbal dermoids can cause astigmatism and amblyopia. They can be isolated or associated with
Goldenhar’s syndrome (preauricular skin tags, aural fistulas, eyelid coloboma, and vertebral anomalies).
scoringKeywords: Limbal dermoids can cause astigmatism and amblyopia. They can be isolated or associated with
Goldenhar’s syndrome (preauricular skin tags, aural fistulas, eyelid coloboma, and vertebral anomalies).

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Treatment is observation or surgical excision. When excising a dermoid, it is important to pay particular
attention to the depth of the keratectomy to avoid penetration of the globe. A variable amount of corneal
scarring may remain.
keyPoints: Treatment is observation or surgical excision. When excising a dermoid, it is important to pay particular
attention to the depth of the keratectomy to avoid penetration of the globe. A variable amount of corneal
scarring may remain.
scoringKeywords: Treatment is observation or surgical excision. When excising a dermoid, it is important to pay particular
attention to the depth of the keratectomy to avoid penetration of the globe. A variable amount of corneal
scarring may remain.

cases.title: 51M - a gradual decrease in vision over years. He states that...
cases.presentation: A 51-year-old man complains of a gradual decrease in vision over years. He states that his vision in the left eye has always been worse than in the right eye, and he has worn glasses since childhood for nearsightedness and astigmatism. He has early cataracts that are similar in both eyes with a best-corrected visual acuity of 20/25 OD and 20/80 OS.

questions.question: What anterior segment findings are evident in the photo?
questions.answer: Corneal scars and cataract.
keyPoints: Corneal scars and cataract.
scoringKeywords: Corneal scars and cataract.

questions.question: What other questions would you ask this patient?
questions.answer:
Is there any history of eye trauma or surgery? Is there a family history of eye disease? What is his past
medical history including birth history (i.e., birth trauma)?
keyPoints: Is there any history of eye trauma or surgery? Is there a family history of eye disease? What is his past
medical history including birth history (i.e., birth trauma)?
scoringKeywords: Is there any history of eye trauma or surgery? Is there a family history of eye disease? What is his past
medical history including birth history (i.e., birth trauma)?

questions.question: What is the corneal diagnosis and etiology?
questions.answer:
Corneal edema and scarring from Descemet’s membrane tears secondary to birth trauma (i.e. forceps
injury).
keyPoints: Corneal edema and scarring from Descemet’s membrane tears secondary to birth trauma (i.e. forceps
injury).
scoringKeywords: Corneal edema and scarring from Descemet’s membrane tears secondary to birth trauma (i.e. forceps
injury).

questions.question: How is this differentiated from congenital glaucoma?
questions.answer:
The breaks in Descemet’s membrane from forceps injury are vertical or oblique whereas those from
congenital glaucoma (Haab’s striae) are oriented horizontally or concentric to the limbus.
keyPoints: The breaks in Descemet’s membrane from forceps injury are vertical or oblique whereas those from
congenital glaucoma (Haab’s striae) are oriented horizontally or concentric to the limbus.
scoringKeywords: The breaks in Descemet’s membrane from forceps injury are vertical or oblique whereas those from
congenital glaucoma (Haab’s striae) are oriented horizontally or concentric to the limbus.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis of a congenital cloudy cornea?
questions.answer:
Mnemonic STUMPED: Sclerocornea, Tear in Descemet’s membrane, Ulcers, Metabolic disease, Peter’s
anomaly, Edema (CHED), Dermoid.
Other causes include congenital hereditary stromal dystrophy (CHSD), rubella, posterior keratoconus,
and corneal staphyloma.
keyPoints: Mnemonic STUMPED: Sclerocornea, Tear in Descemet’s membrane, Ulcers, Metabolic disease, Peter’s
anomaly, Edema (CHED), Dermoid.
Other causes include congenital hereditary stromal dystrophy (CHSD), rubella, posterior keratoconus,
and corneal staphyloma.

Other causes include congenital hereditary stromal dystrophy (CHSD), rubella, posterior keratoconus,

questions.question: What are the treatment options for this patient?
questions.answer:
Corneal transplant with Descemet’s stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK) or penetrating
keratoplasty for corneal decompensation.
keyPoints: Corneal transplant with Descemet’s stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK) or penetrating
keratoplasty for corneal decompensation.

keratoplasty for corneal decompensation.

cases.title: 11M - his eyes have been red and itchy for 2 months. He denies...
cases.presentation: An 11-year-old boy says his eyes have been red and itchy for 2 months. He denies any change in vision but occasionally has some discharge. He has tried Visine with minimal relief. His past ocular and medical history are negative.

questions.question: What is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Allergic conjunctivitis.
keyPoints: Allergic conjunctivitis.
scoringKeywords: Allergic conjunctivitis.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis, and what are the distinguishing findings?
questions.answer:
Seasonal or perennial allergic conjunctivitis, giant papillary conjunctivitis, vernal keratoconjunctivitis,
and atopic keratoconjunctivitis. GPC, VKC, and AKC are more severe conditions with characteristic
findings (i.e. large or giant papillae [GPC and VKC], and may have symblepharon [AKC] or keratitis). The
age of onset, duration, precipitating factors, associated contact lens wear, and a history of atopy are all
helpful in determining the exact diagnosis.
keyPoints: Seasonal or perennial allergic conjunctivitis, giant papillary conjunctivitis, vernal keratoconjunctivitis,
and atopic keratoconjunctivitis. GPC, VKC, and AKC are more severe conditions with characteristic
findings (i.e. large or giant papillae [GPC and VKC], and may have symblepharon [AKC] or keratitis). The
age of onset, duration, precipitating factors, associated contact lens wear, and a history of atopy are all
helpful in determining the exact diagnosis.

and atopic keratoconjunctivitis. GPC, VKC, and AKC are more severe conditions with characteristic
findings (i.e. large or giant papillae [GPC and VKC], and may have symblepharon [AKC] or keratitis). The
age of onset, duration, precipitating factors, associated contact lens wear, and a history of atopy are all
helpful in determining the exact diagnosis.

questions.question: What is the finding, and what is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: The picture shows a Horner-Trantas dot (collection of eosinophils at the limbus). The diagnosis is VKC.
keyPoints: The picture shows a Horner-Trantas dot (collection of eosinophils at the limbus). The diagnosis is VKC.
scoringKeywords: The picture shows a Horner-Trantas dot (collection of eosinophils at the limbus). The diagnosis is VKC.

questions.question: What other characteristic findings may occur?
questions.answer: Ropy discharge, limbal follicles, and shield ulcer.
keyPoints: Ropy discharge, limbal follicles, and shield ulcer.
scoringKeywords: Ropy discharge, limbal follicles, and shield ulcer.

questions.question: What is the treatment?
questions.answer:
Chronic therapy with topical steroids and a combination mast cell stabilizer / antihistamine. A steroid
with less risk of causing increased IOP and cataract, such as loteprednol, is preferred since patients are
young and often require treatment for months or years. The steroids should be tapered slowly and
discontinued when possible. Topical cyclosporine may be helpful.
keyPoints: Chronic therapy with topical steroids and a combination mast cell stabilizer / antihistamine. A steroid
with less risk of causing increased IOP and cataract, such as loteprednol, is preferred since patients are
young and often require treatment for months or years. The steroids should be tapered slowly and
discontinued when possible. Topical cyclosporine may be helpful.
scoringKeywords: Chronic therapy with topical steroids and a combination mast cell stabilizer / antihistamine. A steroid
with less risk of causing increased IOP and cataract, such as loteprednol, is preferred since patients are
young and often require treatment for months or years. The steroids should be tapered slowly and
discontinued when possible. Topical cyclosporine may be helpful.

questions.question: What are the possible complications?
questions.answer:
Corneal ulceration, scarring, and neovascularization. Steroid treatment can cause cataracts (posterior
subcapsular) and increased IOP (and possibly glaucoma).
keyPoints: Corneal ulceration, scarring, and neovascularization. Steroid treatment can cause cataracts (posterior
subcapsular) and increased IOP (and possibly glaucoma).
scoringKeywords: Corneal ulceration, scarring, and neovascularization. Steroid treatment can cause cataracts (posterior
subcapsular) and increased IOP (and possibly glaucoma).

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
VKC is self-limited. It usually lasts up to 10 years and then resolves. The prognosis is very good if
complications do not occur.
keyPoints: VKC is self-limited. It usually lasts up to 10 years and then resolves. The prognosis is very good if
complications do not occur.
scoringKeywords: VKC is self-limited. It usually lasts up to 10 years and then resolves. The prognosis is very good if
complications do not occur.

cases.title: 20M - progressively decreased vision and similar retinal...
cases.presentation: A 20-year-old man presents with progressively decreased vision and similar retinal findings in both eyes.

questions.question: What does the picture show?
questions.answer:
Deep, symmetric, yellow pisciform (fish-tail shaped) flecks (yellow flecks are groups of enlarged retinal
pigment epithelium (RPE) cells packed with a granular substance with ultrastructural, autofluorescent,
and histochemical properties consistent with lipofuscin) scattered throughout the posterior pole at the
level of the RPE. There is also a ‘bull’s eye’ atrophic maculopathy with ‘beaten bronze’ appearance.
keyPoints: Deep, symmetric, yellow pisciform (fish-tail shaped) flecks (yellow flecks are groups of enlarged retinal
pigment epithelium (RPE) cells packed with a granular substance with ultrastructural, autofluorescent,
and histochemical properties consistent with lipofuscin) scattered throughout the posterior pole at the
level of the RPE. There is also a ‘bull’s eye’ atrophic maculopathy with ‘beaten bronze’ appearance.
scoringKeywords: Deep, symmetric, yellow pisciform (fish-tail shaped) flecks (yellow flecks are groups of enlarged retinal
pigment epithelium (RPE) cells packed with a granular substance with ultrastructural, autofluorescent,
and histochemical properties consistent with lipofuscin) scattered throughout the posterior pole at the
level of the RPE. There is also a ‘bull’s eye’ atrophic maculopathy with ‘beaten bronze’ appearance.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
The pisciform lesions with ‘bull’s eye’ maculopathy is most consistent with Stargardt’s disease, but other
causes of a bull’s eye include pericentral retinitis pigmentosa, cone and cone-rod dystrophy, age-related
macular degeneration (ARMD), central areolar choroidal dystrophy, chloroquine / hydroxychoroquine
retinal toxicity, chronic macular hole, olivopontocerebellar atrophy, and ceroid lipofuscinosis.
keyPoints: The pisciform lesions with ‘bull’s eye’ maculopathy is most consistent with Stargardt’s disease, but other
causes of a bull’s eye include pericentral retinitis pigmentosa, cone and cone-rod dystrophy, age-related
macular degeneration (ARMD), central areolar choroidal dystrophy, chloroquine / hydroxychoroquine
retinal toxicity, chronic macular hole, olivopontocerebellar atrophy, and ceroid lipofuscinosis.
scoringKeywords: The pisciform lesions with ‘bull’s eye’ maculopathy is most consistent with Stargardt’s disease, but other
causes of a bull’s eye include pericentral retinitis pigmentosa, cone and cone-rod dystrophy, age-related
macular degeneration (ARMD), central areolar choroidal dystrophy, chloroquine / hydroxychoroquine
retinal toxicity, chronic macular hole, olivopontocerebellar atrophy, and ceroid lipofuscinosis.

questions.question: What other test may be useful to determine the correct diagnosis?
questions.answer:
A fluorescein angiogram highlights the RPE abnormalities of the ‘bull’s eye’ maculopathy, but more
importantly shows the generalized decreased choroidal fluorescence (dark or ‘silent’ choroid sign) and a
central zone of hyperfluorescence as well as blotchy hyperfluorescent spots that do not correspond to the
flecks seen clinically (Note: flecks demonstrate early blockage and late hyperfluorescent staining) of
Stargardt’s disease. Optical coherence tomography is not very useful in this disease, but shows loss of
photoreceptor layers corresponding to macular atrophic areas. Color vision is abnormal.
keyPoints: A fluorescein angiogram highlights the RPE abnormalities of the ‘bull’s eye’ maculopathy, but more
importantly shows the generalized decreased choroidal fluorescence (dark or ‘silent’ choroid sign) and a
central zone of hyperfluorescence as well as blotchy hyperfluorescent spots that do not correspond to the
flecks seen clinically (Note: flecks demonstrate early blockage and late hyperfluorescent staining) of
Stargardt’s disease. Optical coherence tomography is not very useful in this disease, but shows loss of
photoreceptor layers corresponding to macular atrophic areas. Color vision is abnormal.

Stargardt’s disease. Optical coherence tomography is not very useful in this disease, but shows loss of
photoreceptor layers corresponding to macular atrophic areas. Color vision is abnormal.

questions.question: What does the FA show, and what is the diagnosis?
questions.answer: Dark choroid consistent with Stargardt’s disease.
keyPoints: Dark choroid consistent with Stargardt’s disease.
scoringKeywords: Dark choroid consistent with Stargardt’s disease.

questions.question: How would you treat this patient?
questions.answer:
There is no effective treatment. Targeting the Vitamin A cycle may potentially lower lipofuscin levels and
A2E accumulation, but this approach is still experimental. Low-vision aids would be beneficial.
keyPoints: There is no effective treatment. Targeting the Vitamin A cycle may potentially lower lipofuscin levels and
A2E accumulation, but this approach is still experimental. Low-vision aids would be beneficial.
scoringKeywords: There is no effective treatment. Targeting the Vitamin A cycle may potentially lower lipofuscin levels and
A2E accumulation, but this approach is still experimental. Low-vision aids would be beneficial.

questions.question: What would you tell this patient about the genetics of this disease?
questions.answer:
Stargardt’s disease has been mapped to a mutation in the STGD1/ABCA4 gene on chromosome 1p21-p22
in the more common autosomal recessive form. In contrast, the autosomal dominant form has been
mapped to STGD4 on chromosome 4p and STGD3/ELOVL4 on chromosome 6q14 encoding a
photoreceptor-specific component of a polyunsaturated fatty acid elongation system. Fundus
flavimaculatus has been mapped to ABCA4 gene on chromosome 1p21-p13.
keyPoints: Stargardt’s disease has been mapped to a mutation in the STGD1/ABCA4 gene on chromosome 1p21-p22
in the more common autosomal recessive form. In contrast, the autosomal dominant form has been
mapped to STGD4 on chromosome 4p and STGD3/ELOVL4 on chromosome 6q14 encoding a
photoreceptor-specific component of a polyunsaturated fatty acid elongation system. Fundus
flavimaculatus has been mapped to ABCA4 gene on chromosome 1p21-p13.
scoringKeywords: Stargardt’s disease has been mapped to a mutation in the STGD1/ABCA4 gene on chromosome 1p21-p22
in the more common autosomal recessive form. In contrast, the autosomal dominant form has been
mapped to STGD4 on chromosome 4p and STGD3/ELOVL4 on chromosome 6q14 encoding a
photoreceptor-specific component of a polyunsaturated fatty acid elongation system. Fundus
flavimaculatus has been mapped to ABCA4 gene on chromosome 1p21-p13.

questions.question: What is the prognosis?
questions.answer:
Stargardt’s disease carries a poor prognosis with vision deteriorating to 20/200 or worse by the 3rd
decade of life. The autosomal dominant form has a more benign course with milder color and night
vision changes, later onset, and less severe clinical course.
keyPoints: Stargardt’s disease carries a poor prognosis with vision deteriorating to 20/200 or worse by the 3rd

vision changes, later onset, and less severe clinical course.

cases.title: Quantify low vision
cases.presentation: Quantify low vision

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Low vision is when a patient’s visual needs exceed his or her visual capabilities. This may occur with a VA of 20/40 in one patient
and 20/200 in another. As such, there are no set measurements that quantify low vision as in legal blindness.
The low vision examination consists of history, examination and a trial of variety of low vision aids. The history determines the
patient’s visual and functional goals in regards to attention to daily living, occupation, or hobbies. The examination should include
measuring visual acuity with a low vision chart or by moving the patient closer to the chart. Both distance and near visual acuity
should be measured. Other findings that may influence the type of aid prescribed should be noted such as other physical disabilities,
nystagmus, photophobia, aniridia, etc. An optimal refraction should be obtained using Kestenbaum’s Rule to estimate the strength of a
near add.

cases.title: What needs to be considered for IOL calculations
cases.presentation: What needs to be considered for IOL calculations post-refractive surgery?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Keratometry assumes a normal relationship between the anterior and posterior corneal curvatures and measure the anterior corneal
radius. Incisional keratorefractive surgery for myopia flattens both the anterior and posterior corneal radius. Ablative keratorefractive
surgery for myopia flattens the anterior corneal radius but leaves the posterior corneal radius mostly unchanged. Standard
keratometry extrapolates the central corneal power based on paracentral measurements but in these settings will over-
estimate the central corneal power leading to postoperative hyperopic surprise.
In addition, if the central power is very low, many formulas assume the anterior chamber is shallow and the IOL will sit closer to the
cornea than normal and call for less power. The Holladay 2 formula and Double K correction method with SRK/T, Hoffer Q or
Holladay 1 formulas can compensate for this error.

cases.diagnosisTitle: What needs to be considered for IOL calculations post-refractive surgery?

cases.title: How is accommodative amplitude measured?
cases.presentation: How is accommodative amplitude measured?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
One eye is occluded with the best distance correction in place. Slowly move a target closer to the eye while strongly encouraging the
patient to keep it in focus. The target is slid along a Prince rule or an RAF (royal air force) rule. Record the point at which the patient
is no longer able to keep the target in focus. Repeat for the other eye. The dioptric value of the NPA is then equal to the
accommodative amplitude.
For presbyopes, test with a +3.00 add then subtract 3D from accommodative amplitude measured

cases.title: Transposing plus to minus cylinder
cases.presentation: Transposing plus to minus cylinder

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Transpose the following: +2.50 + 0.75 x 55
In order to transpose from plus to minus cylinder you must:
1) Add the sphere and cylinder together
(+2.50 +0.75= +3.25)
2) Change the sign of the cylinder (+0.75 🡪 - 0.75)
3) Add 90 to the axis (55 +90 = 145)
Now put it together! +3.25 – 0.75 x 145

cases.title: Dealing with a patient who is not accepting spectacle
cases.presentation: Dealing with a patient who is not accepting spectacle correction of astigmatism?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
- Always re-refract and ensure the patient has the correct refraction (pay attention to significant anisometropia)
- Check if ordered glasses were correctly made (Rx, placement of optical centers, base curves of new glasses match old
glasses, minus cylinder ground on to back of lens)
- Minimize vertex distance to decrease magnification
- Reassure the patient that it may take time to adjust to new prescription
- Note if the cylinders are at oblique angles to one another
- Decrease the power of the cylinder while keeping S.E. constant
- Rotate the cylinder towards 90 or 180 meridian
- Discuss CL or refractive surgery when appropriate

cases.title: How do you assess the fit of an RGP lens? Soft lens?
cases.presentation: How do you assess the fit of an RGP lens? Soft lens?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Basic Evaluation
● refract the patient, get the sphere in minus cylinder (use sphere only unless fitting toric lens)
● correct this value for vertex distance
● evaluate the patient’s corneal health
● obtain keratometry
RGP LENS (better for patients with high ametropia, astigmatism, corneal irregularities / less comfortable)
● First lens choice should have base curve 0.5D plus the flattest K reading
● Fluorescein test
o Slight apical clearing, mid peripheral touch with slight edge clearance => Good Fit
o Apical touch with peripheral => too loose
▪ choose a steeper base curve (smaller radius of curvature) or larger lens diameter
o Apical clearance with central pooling, bubbles => too tight
▪ choose a flatter base curve (larger radius of curvature) or smaller lens diameter
● Remember SAM/FAP rule if you change base curve
o Steeper add Minus to power
o Flatter add Plus to power
SOFT LENS
▪ Movement on Blink: CTL should move 1mm down with blink and reposition
o too much movement, it is too loose (flat)
o too little, too tight (steep)
▪ Centration: should cover entire cornea
▪ Sag: lens should drop 0.5 to 1 mm on upgaze
▪ Toric: check lens rotation and stability

cases.title: What is chromatic aberration & duochrome test?
cases.presentation: What is chromatic aberration & duochrome test?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Chromatic aberration
When polychromatic light passes through a lens, shorter wavelengths (blue) are refracted (bent) more than longer wavelengths
(red) so that the former comes in focus closer to the lens than the latter. This is caused by the prismatic effect of lenses.
Duochrome test
This principle is applied in the duochrome test used to refine the spherical endpoint after performing manifest refraction. It is a
monocular test (done with each eye separately). First, the eye is fogged by adding +0.5 D to relax accommodation. A chart is shown
with black letters on a half-red and half-green background. The letters on the red side should be in sharper focus first due to fogging.
Then, minus sphere is dialed in until the letters are equally clear on the red and green sides.
“RAM GAP” - Red add minus, green add plus
There is a 1.0 D difference between the red and green light-rays. The duochrome test results in the image being in best focus on the
retina for white light conditions.
Does this test work on color blind individuals?
Yes, because it relies on chromatic aberration, not on color discrimination.
What range of VA is required to perform the duochrome test?
20/30 or better

cases.title: What is a Geneva lens clock?
cases.presentation: What is a Geneva lens clock?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Measures the radius of curvature of a lens surface by the deflection of a movable pin. Base curves affect the magnification of the lens
independent of the lens power. If a patient is accustomed to a certain base curve (magnification), a change in base curve - with
constant overall dioptric power – may cause asthenopic symptoms.

cases.title: What is a lensmeter?
cases.presentation: What is a lens meter?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
A lens meter is used to measure sphere, cylinder, axis, prism and locate the optical center of a lens
It works based on the optometer principle and is made of an illuminated movable target behind a fixed lens, and a place between the
fixed lens and telescope to position the unknown lens. The target is moved to determine the focal length and thus the power of the
unknown lens.
How to use the instrument:
1. Rotate the axis dial until the narrow lines are all parallel
2. Rotate the drum until the narrow lines are in focus. This is sphere
3. Rotate the drum until the solid lines are in focus. Should be in positive direction otherwise rotate axis 90 degrees.
For prism: Make sure lens is centered and location of center of target from center cross hairs is noted. Each concentric circle away
from center equals 1 PD. Nasal= Base-in, Temporal= Base-out, Superior = Base up, Inferior = Base down

cases.title: Unhappy patient with current glasses: what do you do?
cases.presentation: Unhappy patient with current glasses: what do you do?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Take a detailed history to find out:
● Symptoms: blurred vision, distorted vision, anisokonia, asthenopia, diploplia
● Setting: Difficulty with distance reading? near reading? Bifocal segment? Night-time? Glare?
● Old Mrx better: have patient bring in old glasses to compare
Evaluation
● Correct Mrx – repeat refraction, cycloplegic if concern for over-minused
● Correct glasses: Does not match Mrx, astigmatism axis wrong/power too strong
● Amount and axis of cylinder – rotate closer to 90 or 180 degrees
● Mechanical
o Check fit
o Check optical centers (unintentional prism)
o Check vertex distance (may be different from old glasses)
o Check base curve using lens clock (if different then previous lenses can change magnification)
● Bifocals: style of add changed, segment placement, progressive Rx not strong enough requiring patient to look too low,
● Induced prism from anisometropia – slab off prism, CL, lower both optical centers, single vision glasses, dissimilar segments,
Fresnel prism over bifocal segment
● Night vision issues: Pt may over accommodate at night due to no target and induce myopia. Add -0.25D
● Ocular pathology: surgically induced anisometropia(Usually < 2 D), ocular surface, cataract, retina finding
● Check for phoria or tropia

cases.title: Explain Spherical aberration
cases.presentation: Explain Spherical aberration

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Rays that strike the periphery of a lens system will be more strongly bent (if positive spherical aberration is present), effectively
shortening its focal length. This becomes a problem with widely dilated pupils (pharmacologic or in low light levels) causing the
patient to become more myopic,
This explains the phenomenon in retinoscopy when you have against movement in periphery and with movement centrally. Peripheral
lens refracts light more strongly than central rays so periphery appears more myopic. One should neutralize central reflex.

cases.title: Explain critical angle
cases.presentation: Explain critical angle, total internal reflection and its application to ophthalmology

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
CRITICAL ANGLE is the angle at which light is bent exactly 90 degress away from the normal causing light to run parallel with the
refractive interface. When the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle the light is reflected back into the material with the higher
index of refraction causing TOTAL INTERNAL REFRACTION. Light leaving the angle (i.e trabecular meshwork) is reflected back
into the anterior chamber at the tear-air interface bacause of total internal reflection. Gonioscopy overcomes this by eliminating the
tear-air interface.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Explain critical angle, total internal reflection and its application to ophthalmology

Plus lenses decrease the measured deviation, whether ET, XT or HT
Minus Lenses increase the measured deviation, whether ET, XT or HT
The true deviation is changed by approximately (2.5) (D) % where D = bilateral spectacle lens power
For example an exotrope of 40 prism D wearing -10.00 sphere glasses will measure:
(2.5) (10) = 25% more XT for a total of 50 prism D of XT
3M mnemonic: Minus Measures More

cases.title: Bifocal Segments- prismatic effects (image jump vs.
cases.presentation: Bifocal Segments- prismatic effects (image jump vs. image displacement)

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Image jump – due to sudden introduction of prismatic power at top of bifocal segment. With round top segments, sudden introduction
of base down prism causes an object to “jump” upward as the eye moves down. If optical center of bifocal is at top of segment (as in
Franklin or Executive type) or progressives, there is no image jump.
Image displacement – produced by total prismatic power acting in the reading position. Minimized when prismatic effect of bifocal is
opposite to that of the distance segment.
Myopic patients should be given flat top segments (Franklin/Executive style). This minimizes both image jump and image
displacement.
Hyperopic patients – round tops minimize image displacement and maximize image jump; flat top or Franklin-type segments
minimize image jump while maximizing image displacement.
Image jump is more bothersome than image displacement. In practice, many opticians give flat top segments to myopes and hyperopes
because flat top segments are easier and cheaper to assemble. As long as a patient is not complaining, this is acceptable.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Bifocal Segments- prismatic effects (image jump vs. image displacement)

cases.title: Patient complains that glasses fog and get dirty.
cases.presentation: Patient complains that glasses fog and get dirty

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Tight Frames (poor air circulation): adjust tilt to minimize contact and increase vertex distance.
Large frames (rub the eyebrows or rest on the cheeks): change to a smaller frame size.
Glass frames: change to plastic lenses which conduct heat more poorly than glass.
Treat underlying eye conditions:
- epiphora may cause fogging
- seborrheic dermatitis may promote deposition of dirt and oil on the lenses.
keyPoints: epiphora may cause fogging
seborrheic dermatitis may promote deposition of dirt and oil on the lenses.

epiphora may cause fogging

cases.title: Why would hyperopic patient slide glasses down his/her
cases.presentation: Why would hyperopic patient slide glasses down his/her nose?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Moving a lens, either plus or minus, away from the eye gives it more effective plus power since it pulls its far point with it. For
distance viewing this would help the patient if the plus lens prescribed is too weak. The patient can slide his glasses forward to adjust
for this error. Another reason is that near viewing can be enhanced by increasing the effective plus power.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Why would hyperopic patient slide glasses down his/her nose?

cases.title: Describe the technique of streak retinoscopy.
cases.presentation: Describe the technique of streak retinoscopy

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Retinoscopy is a method of objectively measuring the refractive state of an eye. With a phoropter or free lenses, the examiner moves
the light streak across the pupil and changes the correcting lens until the reflex movement is neutralized (pupil appears uniformly
illuminated). The final refraction is determined by adjusting for working distance.
Myopic: far point between examiner and pupil => reflex moves in direction opposite to retinoscope sweep.
Hyperopic: far point behind examiner => reflex moves in same direction as retinoscope sweep.

cases.title: What are some causes of astigmatism?
cases.presentation: What are some causes of astigmatism?

questions.question: What are some causes of astigmatism?
questions.answer:
There are external factors such as ptosis, lid masses and pterygia.
Then you can have corneal issues such as keratoconus, corneal dermoids,
Salzmann’s nodules, peripheral corneal problems (pellucid marginal
degeneration, peripheral ulcerative keratitis) or ectasia (i.e. post refractive
co]
surgery). In addition the lens can induce astigmatism in such conditions as
lens coloboma, simple lenticular astigmatism, lens dislocations (Marfan’s,
Trauma) and lenticonus. A life threatening cause of acquired astigmatism is
a ciliary body melanoma. It is important to note that retinal disease does not
cause astigmatism. It causes vision changes such as distortion.

cases.presentation: What is this piece of equipment and how does it work?

questions.question: What is this piece of equipment and how does it work?
questions.answer:
A lens meter is used to measure sphere, cylinder, axis, and prism and
to locate the optical center.
It is made of an illuminated movable target behind a fixed lens, anda
place between the fixed lens and the telescope (eyepiece) to position the
unknown lens. It works by moving the target to determine the focal length of
the spectacle lens. By using a fixed lens of known power, the distance
needed to move the target is reduced. This is known as the optometer
princip! -
“ow to use the instrument: When looking through the telescope,
you see a target with a line with three narrow striations perpendicular to three
wide solid lines.
_ Rotate the axis dial until the narrow lines are all parallel.
2. Rotate the drum until the narrow lines are in focus. This is the
sphere.
3. Rotate the drum until the solid lines are now in focus.
The drum should be rotated in a positive power direction. If the
drum needs to be rotated in a negative power direction, the axis is off by 90
degrees. Rotate the axis 90 degrees and repeat steps 2 & 3. Once in focus,
the difference between the reading in step 2 and 3 is the amount of cylinder.
Vor prism, make sure the lens is centered and the location of the
center of the target from the center cross hairs is noted. There are concentric
circle and for each circle away from center equals 1 PD. If nasal then base-in
(BI), if up then base up (BU), etc. To find the optical center one must adjust
for PD or if no prism then line lens up so target is center in the cross hairs.
There is a pin marker with the lensometer, if not, use a non-permanent
marker 0 mark.
Note: This is helpful when evaluating glasses fit on a patient.

cases.title: Unhappy patient with glasses, what do you do?
cases.presentation: Unhappy patient with glasses, what do you do?

questions.question: Unhappy patient with glasses, what do you do?
questions.answer:
First listen to the problem. Determine if the issue is acuity near/far or
poth. glare, night vision, fit or distortion.
Check current glasses Rx and compare with written prescription.
See if the problem is the due to wrong glasses prescription. If so then check
refraction again and perform trial test. If better, then write new Rx.
Check fit; see if glasses are positioned so as to maximize the optical
centers, and alignment of bifocal segments. Locate center using lens meter.
Mark and place back on patient. Make adjustment if possible to frame.
If the problem occurs only at night, then see if a night myopia
adjustment was made. Typically add .25 minus to compensate for the
Purkinje effect of night myopia. This occurs when patients over
accommodate at night due to no target and thus induces myopia.
(f distortion, look at the amount and axis of the cylinder. Decrease
the power and rotate as close to 90 or 180 as possible without sacrificing
vision. Patients can have distortion from oblique astigmatism when the
cylinder power is significant. Patients tolerate meridinal magnification more
when the axis is at 90 or 180.
Vor problems with induced prism, first make sure the optical center
of the lens corresponds with the PD. For induced prism in downgaze,
consider lowering the optical center, grinding the lens edge (slab off),
separating into reading and distance glasses, or contact lens wear.
Note: Most important is to listen to problem then correct accordingly.

questions.answer:
A young patient one must consider the possibility of organic disease
such as Vitamin A deficiency or retinitis pigmentosa. Start with a complete
family history. Would perform a full eye exam looking for Bitot’s spots and
perform a dilated fundus exam looking for waxy pallor of the disc, attenuated
retinal vessels and bone spicules. If suspicion of RP is high, then perform
autofluorescence photography and an ERG.
'f no retinal pathology is found, then consider a refractive etiology.
At night the pupil tends to dilate and the lens periphery refracts more
powerfu ly and can cause a mild myopia. In addition the shorter wavelengths
of light «| night converge more anterior to the retina call a Purkinje Shift.
Lastly, in the dark of the night there are poor accommodative targets. This
causes over accommodation and induces a myopic shift and poor vision at
night.

questions.question: with the rule astigmatism?”
questions.answer:
With-the-rule astigmatism (WTR) simply indicates that the cornea is
eper vertically than it is horizontally. Thus, the steepest K reading will be
s 20 degrees of 90. WTR is corrected by adding plus cylinder with the
q at 090. Alternatively, WTR can be corrected with minus cylinder with
the axis set at 180 degrees.
WTR is commonly associated with younger individuals due to the
tightness of the eyelids. Older people tend to have, against-the-rule
1 sc
astigmatism due to decreased eyelid muscle tone

prescription is:
OD: -2.50 + .50 x 180: add +3.00

The patient’s diplopia in reading position is being induced by a
prismatic effect. When they are looking down through the glasses there is a
pase down position in one eye and a base up in the other eye. This disparity
can be overcome if it is small, as we have the ability to correct some vertical
fusiona! discrepancies. You can calculate this using the Prentice rule
principles. In order help them with their symptoms you should perform
refraction at distance and near to ensure that the current prescription is
correct. You should also make sure the current glasses are the correct
prescripon. If determined to be correct you can improve symptoms many
differen’ ways. You could have a Slab off performed on the more minus
lens, lower the optical centers of the glasses to reduce the prismatic effect or
add Fresnel lenses. You can also discuss the option of contact lenses or
separatin® into reading and distance glasses.

One must , jook at the cause of low vision and ensure patient has maximal medical and surgical care. Then you must look at impairment and look to visual aids and devices to maximize vision for distance and near. Then you look at the erson and consider skills training, counseling, ADL’s, vocational needs and seek occupation consultation to maximize quality of life issues.

cases.title: 48F - progressive deterioration in distance and near vision in...
cases.presentation: A 48-year-old woman with myopia complains of progressive deterioration in distance and near vision in both eyes for the past 2 years. She can improve her vision by sliding her glasses down her nose. Her past medical history is significant for diabetes for 10 years, for which she takes glyburide. She reports blood sugar levels usually between 120 and 140 mg/dL and a recent hemoglobin A1c of 6.8%.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
Decreased myopia and increased presbyopia. The change in refractive error may be due to overcorrection
in her current distance spectacle prescription, cataracts, diabetic macular edema, or medications (i.e.
chloroquine, phenothiazines, antihistamines, benzodiazepines).
keyPoints: Decreased myopia and increased presbyopia. The change in refractive error may be due to overcorrection
in her current distance spectacle prescription, cataracts, diabetic macular edema, or medications (i.e.
chloroquine, phenothiazines, antihistamines, benzodiazepines).
scoringKeywords: Decreased myopia and increased presbyopia. The change in refractive error may be due to overcorrection
in her current distance spectacle prescription, cataracts, diabetic macular edema, or medications (i.e.
chloroquine, phenothiazines, antihistamines, benzodiazepines).

questions.question: What other history would you like to know?
questions.answer:
How old is her current prescription and what type of glasses are they? Does she have glare / halo /
starburst from lights? Has the diabetes ever affected her retina and if so did she ever have any retinal
treatment? Is she taking any other medications?
keyPoints: How old is her current prescription and what type of glasses are they? Does she have glare / halo /
starburst from lights? Has the diabetes ever affected her retina and if so did she ever have any retinal
treatment? Is she taking any other medications?
scoringKeywords: How old is her current prescription and what type of glasses are they? Does she have glare / halo /
starburst from lights? Has the diabetes ever affected her retina and if so did she ever have any retinal

questions.question: What would your exam entail?
questions.answer:
Measure her current glass prescription, perform a manifest refraction, and then a complete eye exam with
attention to the crystalline lens for cataract and retina for diabetic macular edema.
keyPoints: Measure her current glass prescription, perform a manifest refraction, and then a complete eye exam with
attention to the crystalline lens for cataract and retina for diabetic macular edema.
scoringKeywords: Measure her current glass prescription, perform a manifest refraction, and then a complete eye exam with
attention to the crystalline lens for cataract and retina for diabetic macular edema.

questions.question: What is your diagnosis and treatment plan?
questions.answer:
Myopia / presbyopia with overminused glasses. A new glasses prescription should be given using the
power from her manifest refraction. She should continue good blood sugar control and return for annual
eye exams.

scoringKeywords: Myopia / presbyopia with overminused glasses. A new glasses prescription should be given using the
power from her manifest refraction. She should continue good blood sugar control and return for annual

questions.question: What are his options?
questions.answer:
The glasses options are progressive, bifocal, or single vision lenses. Glasses for the computer can be single
vision, trifocal, or computer bifocals.
keyPoints: The glasses options are progressive, bifocal, or single vision lenses. Glasses for the computer can be single
vision, trifocal, or computer bifocals.

vision, trifocal, or computer bifocals.

questions.question: What are the 2 prismatic effects that occur with bifocals?
questions.answer:
Image jump and image displacement.
Image jump is related to the position of the optical center of the add segment. It is produced by the
sudden prismatic power at the top of the bifocal segment (it is not influenced by the type of underlying
lens). As the patient’s line of sight crosses from the optical center of the distance lens to the bifocal
segment, the image position suddenly shifts up owing to the base-down (BD) prismatic effect of the
bifocal segment.
Image displacement is due to the total prismatic power of the lens and bifocal segment. It is minimized
when the prismatic effect of the bifocal segment and distance lens are in opposite directions.
Image jump is more bothersome than image displacement, so the segment type should be chosen to
minimize image jump.
keyPoints: Image jump and image displacement.
Image jump is related to the position of the optical center of the add segment. It is produced by the
sudden prismatic power at the top of the bifocal segment (it is not influenced by the type of underlying
lens). As the patient’s line of sight crosses from the optical center of the distance lens to the bifocal
segment, the image position suddenly shifts up owing to the base-down (BD) prismatic effect of the
bifocal segment.
Image displacement is due to the total prismatic power of the lens and bifocal segment. It is minimized
when the prismatic effect of the bifocal segment and distance lens are in opposite directions.
scoringKeywords: Image jump and image displacement.
Image jump is related to the position of the optical center of the add segment. It is produced by the
sudden prismatic power at the top of the bifocal segment (it is not influenced by the type of underlying
lens). As the patient’s line of sight crosses from the optical center of the distance lens to the bifocal
segment, the image position suddenly shifts up owing to the base-down (BD) prismatic effect of the
bifocal segment.
Image displacement is due to the total prismatic power of the lens and bifocal segment. It is minimized
when the prismatic effect of the bifocal segment and distance lens are in opposite directions.

questions.question: Discuss the pros and cons of different bifocal designs.
questions.answer:
The advantage of progressive lenses is the blended segment without a visible line and there is no image
jump; however, there is usually an adaptation period, especially for patients who have previously worn
lined bifocals.
Bifocal lenses have a visible line and there are different types of segment styles – round-top, flat-top, and
executive. Round-top segments produce the most image jump, and they cause more image displacement
in myopes than in hyperopes. Flat-top segments minimize image jump, and image displacement is less
in a myope than a hyperope. Executive bifocals have a larger area dedicated to near vision, and there is
no image jump because the optical centers are at the top of the segment.
keyPoints: The advantage of progressive lenses is the blended segment without a visible line and there is no image
jump; however, there is usually an adaptation period, especially for patients who have previously worn
lined bifocals.

executive. Round-top segments produce the most image jump, and they cause more image displacement

cases.title: 46F - trouble with near vision. Her current glasses are 4...
cases.presentation: A 46-year-old woman complains of trouble with near vision. Her current glasses are 4 years old and she says her eyes feel strained when she reads. She wants to get a new prescription for glasses.

questions.question: What is the technique for subjective manifest refraction?
questions.answer:
Starting with her current prescription in either a trial lens or phoropter, the distance vision is checked
monocularly and the sphere is adjusted first as she is asked to read progressively smaller lines on the
acuity chart. The axis of any cylinder is then refined with a Jackson cross cylinder. The vision must be at
least 20/40 to use the 0.25 cross cylinder. Once the correct axis is determined, the amount of cylinder is
then determined in a similar fashion with the cross cylinder. The sphere is then rechecked until the best
acuity is achieved. Finally, three methods can be used to perform binocular balance to equally control
accommodation in both eyes during distance refraction: prism dissociation (3 prism diopters [PD] BU
over one eye and 3 PD BD over the other with a Risley prism), balanced fogging (fog both eyes and
alternate cover until equally fogged), or duochrome test (red–green balance both eyes).
keyPoints: Starting with her current prescription in either a trial lens or phoropter, the distance vision is checked
monocularly and the sphere is adjusted first as she is asked to read progressively smaller lines on the
acuity chart. The axis of any cylinder is then refined with a Jackson cross cylinder. The vision must be at
least 20/40 to use the 0.25 cross cylinder. Once the correct axis is determined, the amount of cylinder is
then determined in a similar fashion with the cross cylinder. The sphere is then rechecked until the best
acuity is achieved. Finally, three methods can be used to perform binocular balance to equally control
